subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Explain why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives H2 at cathode and Cl2 at anode. Write overall reaction.
{tex}\left( \begin{gathered} E_{N{a^ + }/Na}^0 = - 2.71V;\;E_{{H_2}O/{H_2}}^0 = - 0.83V \hfill \\ E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V;\;E_{{H^ + } + {O_2}/{H_2}O}^0 = 1.23V \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right){/tex}
question_text: Explain why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives H 2 at cathode and Cl 2 at anode. Write overall reaction. {tex}\left( \begin{gathered} E_{N{a^ + }/Na}^0 = - 2.71V;\;E_{{H_2}O/{H_2}}^0 = - 0.83V \hfill \\ E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V;\;E_{{H^ + } + {O_2}/{H_2}O}^0 = 1.23V \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right){/tex}
explanation: At cathode, the following reduction reactions can take place: {tex}Na_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to N{a_{(s)}};E_{N{a^ + }/Na}^0 = - 2.71V{/tex} {tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2}(g);\;E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^0 = OV{/tex} A reduction reaction with higher reduction potential is preferred. Therefore, the reaction at the cathode during electrolysis is {tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_{2(g)}}{/tex} This is why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives H 2 at the cathode. At anode, the following oxidation reactions can take place: {tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V{/tex} {tex}2{H_2}{O_{(l)}} \to {O_{2(g)}} + 4H_{(aq)}^ + + 4{e^ - }E_{{H^ + } + {O_2}/{H_2}O}^0 {/tex} = 1.23V At anode, the reaction with lower value of E 0 is preferred. But, due to overvoltage, oxidation of chloride ion occurs and chlorine gas is obtained. Hence, the reaction at the anode during electrolysis is {tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }\;E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V{/tex} This is why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives Cl 2 at the anode. The overall cell reaction is given below. {tex}{H^ + } + C{l^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2} + \frac{1}{2}C{l_2}{/tex}
solution: At cathode, the following reduction reactions can take place: {tex}Na_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to N{a_{(s)}};E_{N{a^ + }/Na}^0 = - 2.71V{/tex} {tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2}(g);\;E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^0 = OV{/tex} A reduction reaction with higher reduction potential is preferred. Therefore, the reaction at the cathode during electrolysis is {tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_{2(g)}}{/tex} This is why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives H 2 at the cathode. At anode, the following oxidation reactions can take place: {tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V{/tex} {tex}2{H_2}{O_{(l)}} \to {O_{2(g)}} + 4H_{(aq)}^ + + 4{e^ - }E_{{H^ + } + {O_2}/{H_2}O}^0 {/tex} = 1.23V At anode, the reaction with lower value of E 0 is preferred. But, due to overvoltage, oxidation of chloride ion occurs and chlorine gas is obtained. Hence, the reaction at the anode during electrolysis is {tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }\;E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V{/tex} This is why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives Cl 2 at the anode. The overall cell reaction is given below. {tex}{H^ + } + C{l^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2} + \frac{1}{2}C{l_2}{/tex}
solution_html:
At cathode, the following reduction reactions can take place:
{tex}Na_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to N{a_{(s)}};E_{N{a^ + }/Na}^0 = - 2.71V{/tex}
{tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2}(g);\;E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^0 = OV{/tex}
A reduction reaction with higher reduction potential is preferred. Therefore, the reaction at the cathode during electrolysis is
{tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_{2(g)}}{/tex}
This is why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives H2 at the cathode.
At anode, the following oxidation reactions can take place:
{tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V{/tex}
{tex}2{H_2}{O_{(l)}} \to {O_{2(g)}} + 4H_{(aq)}^ + + 4{e^ - }E_{{H^ + } + {O_2}/{H_2}O}^0 {/tex} = 1.23V
At anode, the reaction with lower value of E0 is preferred. But, due to overvoltage, oxidation of chloride ion occurs and chlorine gas is obtained. Hence, the reaction at the anode during electrolysis is
{tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }\;E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V{/tex}
This is why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives Cl2 at the anode.
The overall cell reaction is given below.
{tex}{H^ + } + C{l^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2} + \frac{1}{2}C{l_2}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Write the cell reaction and calculate the e.m.f. of the following cell at 298 K:
    Sn(s) | Sn2+(0.004M) || H+(0.020M) | H2(g) (1 bar) | Pt(s)
    (Given: {tex}E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Sn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.14 V, {tex}\left.\mathrm{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{H}+\mid \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g}), \mathrm{Pt}}=0.00 \mathrm{~V}\right){/tex}
  2. Account for the following:
    1. On the basis of Eo values, O2 gas should be liberated at anode but it is Cl2 gas which is liberated in the electrolysis of aqueous NaCl.
    2. Conductivity of CH3COOH decreases on dilution.
question_text: Write the cell reaction and calculate the e.m.f. of the following cell at 298 K: Sn(s) | Sn 2+ (0.004M) || H + (0.020M) | H 2 (g) (1 bar) | Pt(s) (Given: {tex}E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Sn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.14 V, {tex}\left.\mathrm{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{H}+\mid \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g}), \mathrm{Pt}}=0.00 \mathrm{~V}\right){/tex} Account for the following: On the basis of E o values, O 2 gas should be liberated at anode but it is Cl 2 gas which is liberated in the electrolysis of aqueous NaCl. Conductivity of CH 3 COOH decreases on dilution.
explanation: Sn + 2H + {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Sn 2+ + H 2 (Equation must be balanced) E cell = {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^0{/tex} - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[S n^{2+}\right]}{\left[H^{+}\right]^2}{/tex} = [0 - (-0.14)] - 0.0295 log {tex}\frac{(0.004)}{(0.02)^2}{/tex} = 0.14 - 0.0295 log 10 = 0.1105 V Due to over potential overvoltage of O 2 The number of ions per unit volume decreases.
solution: Sn + 2H + {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Sn 2+ + H 2 (Equation must be balanced) E cell = {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^0{/tex} - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[S n^{2+}\right]}{\left[H^{+}\right]^2}{/tex} = [0 - (-0.14)] - 0.0295 log {tex}\frac{(0.004)}{(0.02)^2}{/tex} = 0.14 - 0.0295 log 10 = 0.1105 V Due to over potential overvoltage of O 2 The number of ions per unit volume decreases.
solution_html:
1. Sn + 2H+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Sn2+ + H2 (Equation must be balanced)
    Ecell = {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^0{/tex} - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log{tex}\frac{\left[S n^{2+}\right]}{\left[H^{+}\right]^2}{/tex}
    = [0 - (-0.14)] - 0.0295 log{tex}\frac{(0.004)}{(0.02)^2}{/tex}
    = 0.14 - 0.0295 log 10 = 0.1105 V
    1. Due to over potential overvoltage of O2
    2. The number of ions per unit volume decreases.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Standard electrode potential for Sn4+/Sn2+ couple is +0.15 V and that for the Cr3+/Cr couple is -0.74 V. These two couples in their standard state are connected to make a cell. The cell potential will be:
question_text: Standard electrode potential for Sn 4+ /Sn 2+ couple is +0.15 V and that for the Cr 3+ /Cr couple is -0.74 V. These two couples in their standard state are connected to make a cell. The cell potential will be:

options.explanation: Sn 4+ + 2e {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Sn 2+ ; {tex}E_{R P}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.15 V Cr 3+ + 3e {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cr; {tex}E_{R P}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.74 (Higher E OP ) Redox change 2Cr + 3Sn 4+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ + 3Sn 2+ {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E_{\text {cell }}=E_{O P_{\mathrm{Cr}}}^{o}+E_{R P_{\mathrm{Sn}}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.74 + 0.15 = +0.89 V

explanation: Sn 4+ + 2e {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Sn 2+ ; {tex}E_{R P}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.15 V Cr 3+ + 3e {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cr; {tex}E_{R P}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.74 (Higher E OP ) Redox change 2Cr + 3Sn 4+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ + 3Sn 2+ {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E_{\text {cell }}=E_{O P_{\mathrm{Cr}}}^{o}+E_{R P_{\mathrm{Sn}}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.74 + 0.15 = +0.89 V

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What amount of electric charge is required for the reduction of 1 mole of {tex} MnO_4^ - {/tex} to {tex}Mn^{2+}{/tex}?
question_text: What amount of electric charge is required for the reduction of 1 mole of {tex} MnO_4^ - {/tex} to {tex}Mn^{2+}{/tex} ?

options.explanation: For reduction of {tex}MnO_4^ - {/tex} to Mn 2+ 5 mol of electrons are required to deposit 1 mol of Mn 2+ ion. so total charge required will be 5F. 5e - + 8H + + {tex}MnO_4^ - {/tex} = Mn 2+ + 4H 2 O

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Assertion (A): For measuring the resistance of an ionic solution an AC source is used.
Reason (R): Concentration of ionic solution will change if the DC source is used.
question_text: Assertion (A): For measuring the resistance of an ionic solution an AC source is used. Reason (R): Concentration of ionic solution will change if the DC source is used.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.explanation: Alternating current is used in the measurement of resistance of electrolyte(ionic) solution because of the concentration of ionic solution change with DC current due to electrolysis.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.

correct_option_text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
explanation: Alternating current is used in the measurement of resistance of electrolyte(ionic) solution because of the concentration of ionic solution change with DC current due to electrolysis.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
In an electrochemical cell, the following reaction takes place:
{tex}2 {Cu}^{+}({aq})+{Zn}({~s}) \rightarrow 2 {Cu}({~s})+{Zn}^{2+}({aq}){/tex}
{tex}{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=1 \cdot 28 {~V}{/tex}
As the reaction progresses, what will happen to the overall voltage of the cell?
question_text: In an electrochemical cell, the following reaction takes place: {tex}2 {Cu}^{+}({aq})+{Zn}({~s}) \rightarrow 2 {Cu}({~s})+{Zn}^{2+}({aq}){/tex} {tex}{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=1 \cdot 28 {~V}{/tex} As the reaction progresses, what will happen to the overall voltage of the cell?

options.explanation: It will decrease as {tex}\left[{Zn}^{2+}\right]{/tex} increases.

explanation: It will decrease as {tex}\left[{Zn}^{2+}\right]{/tex} increases.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate {tex}\Delta_{{r}} {G}^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\log {K}_{{C}}{/tex} of the reaction.
{tex}2 {Cr}({s})+3 {Cd}^{2+}({aq}) {/tex}{tex}\longrightarrow 2 {Cr}^{3+}({aq})+3 {Cd}({s}){/tex}
Given {tex}{E}_{{Cr}^{3+} / {Cr}}^{\circ}=-0.74 {~V}{/tex}
{tex}{E}_{{Cd}^{2+} / {Cd}}^{\circ}=-0.40 {~V}{/tex}
{tex}\left[{R}=8.314 {~J} {~K}^{-1} {~mol}^{-1}, {~F}=96500 {C} {mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex}
question_text: Calculate {tex}\Delta_{{r}} {G}^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\log {K}_{{C}}{/tex} of the reaction. {tex}2 {Cr}({s})+3 {Cd}^{2+}({aq}) {/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow 2 {Cr}^{3+}({aq})+3 {Cd}({s}){/tex} Given {tex}{E}_{{Cr}^{3+} / {Cr}}^{\circ}=-0.74 {~V}{/tex} {tex}{E}_{{Cd}^{2+} / {Cd}}^{\circ}=-0.40 {~V}{/tex} {tex}\left[{R}=8.314 {~J} {~K}^{-1} {~mol}^{-1}, {~F}=96500 {C} {mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex}
explanation: {tex}{E}^{\circ} {C}_{\text {ell }} ={E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {anode }}{/tex} {tex}=-0.40-(-0.74){/tex} {tex}=+0.34 {~V}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {G}^{0} =-n {~F} \ {E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cell }}{/tex} {tex}=-(6 \times 96500 \times 0.34) {J}{/tex} {tex}=-196860 {~J} / {mol}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {G}^{0}=-2.303 {RT} \operatorname{logKc}{/tex} {tex}\log {Kc}=(196860) / 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298{/tex} = 34.576
solution: {tex}{E}^{\circ} {C}_{\text {ell }} ={E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {anode }}{/tex} {tex}=-0.40-(-0.74){/tex} {tex}=+0.34 {~V}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {G}^{0} =-n {~F} \ {E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cell }}{/tex} {tex}=-(6 \times 96500 \times 0.34) {J}{/tex} {tex}=-196860 {~J} / {mol}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {G}^{0}=-2.303 {RT} \operatorname{logKc}{/tex} {tex}\log {Kc}=(196860) / 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298{/tex} = 34.576
solution_html:
{tex}{E}^{\circ} {C}_{\text {ell }} ={E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {anode }}{/tex}
{tex}=-0.40-(-0.74){/tex}
{tex}=+0.34 {~V}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {G}^{0} =-n {~F} \ {E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cell }}{/tex}
{tex}=-(6 \times 96500 \times 0.34) {J}{/tex}
{tex}=-196860 {~J} / {mol}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {G}^{0}=-2.303 {RT} \operatorname{logKc}{/tex}
{tex}\log {Kc}=(196860) / 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298{/tex}
= 34.576

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Complete and balance the following chemical equations:

  1. {tex}8 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+3 {~S}_{2} {O}_{3}^{2-}+{H}_{2} {O} \longrightarrow{/tex}
  2. {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}^{2-}+3 {Sn}^{2+}+14 {H}^{+} \longrightarrow{/tex}
question_text: Complete and balance the following chemical equations: {tex}8 {MnO}_{4}^{-}+3 {~S}_{2} {O}_{3}^{2-}+{H}_{2} {O} \longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}^{2-}+3 {Sn}^{2+}+14 {H}^{+} \longrightarrow{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}8 {MnO}_4^{-}+3 {~S}_2 {O}_3^{2-}+{H}_2 {O} {/tex}{tex}\longrightarrow 8 {MnO}_2+6 {SO}_4^{2-}+2 {OH}^{-}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{Cr}_2 {O}_7{ }^{2-}+3 {Sn}^{2+}+14 {H}^{+}{/tex}{tex} \rightarrow 2 {Cr}^{3+}+3 {Sn}^{4+}+7 {H}_2 {O}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate {tex}\Delta_{{r}} {G}^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\log {K}_{{C}}{/tex} of the reaction:
{tex}{Fe}^{2+}({aq})+{Ag}^{+}({aq}) \longrightarrow {Fe}^{3+}({aq})+{Ag}({s}){/tex}
Given {tex}{E}_{{Ag}^{+} / {Ag}}^{\circ}=0.80 {~V}, {E}_{{Fe}^{3+} / {Fe}^{2+}}^{\circ}=0.77 {~V}{/tex}
{tex}\left[{R}=8.314 {~J} {~K}^{-1} {~mol}^{-1}, {~F}=96500 {C} {mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex}
question_text: Calculate {tex}\Delta_{{r}} {G}^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\log {K}_{{C}}{/tex} of the reaction: {tex}{Fe}^{2+}({aq})+{Ag}^{+}({aq}) \longrightarrow {Fe}^{3+}({aq})+{Ag}({s}){/tex} Given {tex}{E}_{{Ag}^{+} / {Ag}}^{\circ}=0.80 {~V}, {E}_{{Fe}^{3+} / {Fe}^{2+}}^{\circ}=0.77 {~V}{/tex} {tex}\left[{R}=8.314 {~J} {~K}^{-1} {~mol}^{-1}, {~F}=96500 {C} {mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex}
explanation: {tex}{E}^{\circ} _{\text {Cell }} ={E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {anode }}{/tex} {tex}={E}^{\circ}{ }_{{Ag}^+/ {Ag}^{-}}-{E}^{\circ} _{{Fe}^{ 3+}/ {Fe}^{ 2+}}{/tex} {tex}=0.80-0.77{/tex} {tex}=0.03 {~V}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {G}^{0} =-{nF} \ {E}^{\circ} _{\text {Cell }}{/tex} {tex}=-1 \times 96500 \times 0.03{/tex} {tex}=-2895 {~J} / {mol}{/tex} {tex}=-2.895 {KJ} / {mol}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {G}^{0} =-2.303 {RT} \log {Kc}{/tex} {tex}\log {Kc} =-\Delta {G}^{0} / 2.303 {RT}{/tex} {tex}=2895 / 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298{/tex} {tex}\log {Kc} =2895 / 5700{/tex} {tex}=0.508{/tex}
solution: {tex}{E}^{\circ} _{\text {Cell }} ={E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {anode }}{/tex} {tex}={E}^{\circ}{ }_{{Ag}^+/ {Ag}^{-}}-{E}^{\circ} _{{Fe}^{ 3+}/ {Fe}^{ 2+}}{/tex} {tex}=0.80-0.77{/tex} {tex}=0.03 {~V}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {G}^{0} =-{nF} \ {E}^{\circ} _{\text {Cell }}{/tex} {tex}=-1 \times 96500 \times 0.03{/tex} {tex}=-2895 {~J} / {mol}{/tex} {tex}=-2.895 {KJ} / {mol}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {G}^{0} =-2.303 {RT} \log {Kc}{/tex} {tex}\log {Kc} =-\Delta {G}^{0} / 2.303 {RT}{/tex} {tex}=2895 / 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298{/tex} {tex}\log {Kc} =2895 / 5700{/tex} {tex}=0.508{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{E}^{\circ} _{\text {Cell }} ={E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {anode }}{/tex}
{tex}={E}^{\circ}{ }_{{Ag}^+/ {Ag}^{-}}-{E}^{\circ} _{{Fe}^{ 3+}/ {Fe}^{ 2+}}{/tex}
{tex}=0.80-0.77{/tex}
{tex}=0.03 {~V}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {G}^{0} =-{nF} \ {E}^{\circ} _{\text {Cell }}{/tex}
{tex}=-1 \times 96500 \times 0.03{/tex}
{tex}=-2895 {~J} / {mol}{/tex}
{tex}=-2.895 {KJ} / {mol}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {G}^{0} =-2.303 {RT} \log {Kc}{/tex}
{tex}\log {Kc} =-\Delta {G}^{0} / 2.303 {RT}{/tex}
{tex}=2895 / 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298{/tex}
{tex}\log {Kc} =2895 / 5700{/tex}
{tex}=0.508{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Write the anode and cathode reactions and the overall cell reaction occurring in a lead storage battery during its use.
  2. Calculate the potential for half-cell containing 0.01 M {tex}{K}_{2} {Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}({aq}), 0.01 {M} {Cr}^{3+}({aq}){/tex} and {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-4} {M} {H}^{+}({aq}){/tex}.
    The half cell reaction is
    {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}^{2-}({aq})+14 {H}^{+}({aq})+6 {e}^{-}{/tex} {tex} \longrightarrow 2 {Cr}^{3+}({aq})+7 {H}_{2} {O}(l){/tex}
    and the standard electrode potential is given as {tex}{E}^{\circ}=1.33 {~V}{/tex}.
    [Given: {tex}\log 10=1{/tex}]
question_text: Write the anode and cathode reactions and the overall cell reaction occurring in a lead storage battery during its use. Calculate the potential for half-cell containing 0.01 M {tex}{K}_{2} {Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}({aq}), 0.01 {M} {Cr}^{3+}({aq}){/tex} and {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-4} {M} {H}^{+}({aq}){/tex} . The half cell reaction is {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}^{2-}({aq})+14 {H}^{+}({aq})+6 {e}^{-}{/tex} {tex} \longrightarrow 2 {Cr}^{3+}({aq})+7 {H}_{2} {O}(l){/tex} and the standard electrode potential is given as {tex}{E}^{\circ}=1.33 {~V}{/tex} . [Given: {tex}\log 10=1{/tex} ]
explanation: At anode: {tex}{Pb}+{SO}_{4}^{-2} \rightarrow {PbSO}_{4}+2 {e}^-{/tex} At cathode: {tex}{PbO}_{2}+{SO}_{4}{ }^{-2}+4 {H}^{+}+2 {e}^-{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow {PbSO}_{4}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} Overall reaction: {tex}{Pb}+{PbO}_{2}+2 {SO}_{4}^{-2}+4 {H}^{+}{/tex} {tex} \rightarrow 2 {PbSO}_{4}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} {tex}{E}_{\text {Cell }}={E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {Cell }}-{ }_{{n}}^{{0 . 0 5 9}} \boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }}{/tex} {tex} \left[\frac{\left[{Cr}^{3+}\right]^2}{\left[{Cr}^2 {O}^{2-}\right][{H}+]^{14}}\right] {/tex} {tex} E_{cell} = 1.33-\frac {0.059}{6} \log {/tex} {tex}\left(10^{-2}\right)^{2} /\left(10^{-2}\right){/tex} {tex}(1\times 10^{-4})^{14}{/tex} {tex}=1.33- \frac {0.059 }{6}(54) \log 10{/tex} {tex}=1.33-0.059 \times 9{/tex} {tex}=1.33-0.531{/tex} {tex}=0.799 {~V}{/tex}
solution: At anode: {tex}{Pb}+{SO}_{4}^{-2} \rightarrow {PbSO}_{4}+2 {e}^-{/tex} At cathode: {tex}{PbO}_{2}+{SO}_{4}{ }^{-2}+4 {H}^{+}+2 {e}^-{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow {PbSO}_{4}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} Overall reaction: {tex}{Pb}+{PbO}_{2}+2 {SO}_{4}^{-2}+4 {H}^{+}{/tex} {tex} \rightarrow 2 {PbSO}_{4}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} {tex}{E}_{\text {Cell }}={E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {Cell }}-{ }_{{n}}^{{0 . 0 5 9}} \boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }}{/tex} {tex} \left[\frac{\left[{Cr}^{3+}\right]^2}{\left[{Cr}^2 {O}^{2-}\right][{H}+]^{14}}\right] {/tex} {tex} E_{cell} = 1.33-\frac {0.059}{6} \log {/tex} {tex}\left(10^{-2}\right)^{2} /\left(10^{-2}\right){/tex} {tex}(1\times 10^{-4})^{14}{/tex} {tex}=1.33- \frac {0.059 }{6}(54) \log 10{/tex} {tex}=1.33-0.059 \times 9{/tex} {tex}=1.33-0.531{/tex} {tex}=0.799 {~V}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. At anode:
    {tex}{Pb}+{SO}_{4}^{-2} \rightarrow {PbSO}_{4}+2 {e}^-{/tex}
    At cathode:
    {tex}{PbO}_{2}+{SO}_{4}{ }^{-2}+4 {H}^{+}+2 {e}^-{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow {PbSO}_{4}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex}
    Overall reaction:
    {tex}{Pb}+{PbO}_{2}+2 {SO}_{4}^{-2}+4 {H}^{+}{/tex}{tex} \rightarrow 2 {PbSO}_{4}+2 {H}_{2} {O}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{E}_{\text {Cell }}={E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {Cell }}-{ }_{{n}}^{{0 . 0 5 9}} \boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }}{/tex}{tex} \left[\frac{\left[{Cr}^{3+}\right]^2}{\left[{Cr}^2 {O}^{2-}\right][{H}+]^{14}}\right] {/tex}
    {tex} E_{cell} = 1.33-\frac {0.059}{6} \log {/tex}{tex}\left(10^{-2}\right)^{2} /\left(10^{-2}\right){/tex}{tex}(1\times 10^{-4})^{14}{/tex}

    {tex}=1.33-0.059 \times 9{/tex}
    {tex}=1.33-0.531{/tex}
    {tex}=0.799 {~V}{/tex}

question_text: Concentration of KCl solution in {tex}{mol} / {L}{/tex} Conductivity at 298.15 K in {tex}{S}\ {cm}^{-1}{/tex} Molar Conductivity at {tex}298.15 {~K} \ {in} \ {S} \ {cm}^2 {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} 1.000 0.1113 111.3 0.100 0.0129 129.0 0.010 0.00141 141.0 Based on the data given above, give plausible reason for the variation of conductivity and molar conductivity with concentration.
explanation: Conductivity decreases with decrease in concentration Due to decrease in number of current carrying ions per unit volume. Molar conductivity increases with decrease in concentration Due to decrease in inter-ionic attraction or increase in dissociation or increase in number of ions.
solution: Conductivity decreases with decrease in concentration Due to decrease in number of current carrying ions per unit volume. Molar conductivity increases with decrease in concentration Due to decrease in inter-ionic attraction or increase in dissociation or increase in number of ions.
solution_html:
Conductivity decreases with decrease in concentration
Due to decrease in number of current carrying ions per unit volume.
Molar conductivity increases with decrease in concentration
Due to decrease in inter-ionic attraction or increase in dissociation or increase in number of ions.

question_text: For a galvanic cell, the following half reactions are given. Decide, which will remain as reduction reaction and which will be reversed to become an oxidation reaction. Give reason for your answer. {tex}{Cr}^{3+}+3 {e}^{-} \rightarrow \operatorname{Cr}({s}) ; {E}^{\circ}=-0 \cdot 74 {~V}{/tex} {tex}{Fe}^{2+}+2 {e}^{-} \rightarrow {Fe}({s}) ; {E}^{\circ}=-0 \cdot 44 {~V}{/tex} Represent the cell in which the following reaction takes place: {tex}{Mg}({s})+2 {Ag}^{+}(0 \cdot 001 {M}){/tex} {tex} \rightarrow {Mg}^{2+}(0 \cdot 100 {M})+2 {Ag}({s}){/tex} Calculate {tex}{E}_{\text {cell }}{/tex} if {tex}{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=3 \cdot 17 {~V} .(\log 10=1){/tex}
explanation: Will be reversed to become an oxidation reaction Due to low reduction potential of Cr will remain as reduction reaction Cell representation {tex}{Mg}({s}) / {Mg}^{2+}({aq}, 0.100 {M}) {/tex} {tex}\| {Ag}^{+}({aq}, 0.001 {M}) / {Ag}({s}){/tex} {tex} {n}=2 {/tex} {tex}{ E_{cell} } ={E}^{\circ} _ \text { cell }-\frac{2.303 {RT}}{{nF}} \log \frac{\left[{Mg}^{2+}\right]}{\left[{Ag}^{+}\right]^2} {/tex} {tex} =3.17-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{0.100}{(0.001)^2} {/tex} {tex} =3.17-\frac{0.059}{2} \log 10^5 {/tex} {tex} =3.17-0.0295 \times 5 {/tex} {tex} =3.17-0.1475 {/tex} {tex} =3.0225 {~V} \text { or } 3.02 {~V} {/tex}
solution: Will be reversed to become an oxidation reaction Due to low reduction potential of Cr will remain as reduction reaction Cell representation {tex}{Mg}({s}) / {Mg}^{2+}({aq}, 0.100 {M}) {/tex} {tex}\| {Ag}^{+}({aq}, 0.001 {M}) / {Ag}({s}){/tex} {tex} {n}=2 {/tex} {tex}{ E_{cell} } ={E}^{\circ} _ \text { cell }-\frac{2.303 {RT}}{{nF}} \log \frac{\left[{Mg}^{2+}\right]}{\left[{Ag}^{+}\right]^2} {/tex} {tex} =3.17-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{0.100}{(0.001)^2} {/tex} {tex} =3.17-\frac{0.059}{2} \log 10^5 {/tex} {tex} =3.17-0.0295 \times 5 {/tex} {tex} =3.17-0.1475 {/tex} {tex} =3.0225 {~V} \text { or } 3.02 {~V} {/tex}

1. Will be reversed to become an oxidation reaction
      Due to low reduction potential of Cr
    2. will remain as reduction reaction

    {tex}{ E_{cell} } ={E}^{\circ} _ \text { cell }-\frac{2.303 {RT}}{{nF}} \log \frac{\left[{Mg}^{2+}\right]}{\left[{Ag}^{+}\right]^2} {/tex}
    {tex} =3.17-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{0.100}{(0.001)^2} {/tex}
    {tex} =3.17-\frac{0.059}{2} \log 10^5 {/tex}
    {tex} =3.17-0.0295 \times 5 {/tex}

explanation: {tex} 2 {Al}+3 {Ni}^{2+} \rightarrow 2 {Al}^{3+}+3 {Ni} {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}={E}^{\circ}{ }_{{Ni} ^{2+}/ {Ni}}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{{Al}^{ 3+}/ {Al}} ; {/tex} {tex}{E}^{\circ} _\text { cell }=-0.25-(-1.66)=1.41 {~V} {/tex} {tex} {n}=6 {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}={E}^{\circ}_ \text { cell}- \frac{2.303 {RT}}{{nF}} \log \frac{\left[{Al}^{3+}\right]^2}{\left[{Ni}^{2+}\right]^3} {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}=1.41-\frac{0.059}{6} \log \frac{[0.002]^2}{[0.002]^3} {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}=1.41-\frac{0.059}{6} \log \frac{\left[2 \times 10^{-3}\right]^2}{\left[2 \times 10^{-3}\right]^3} {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}=1.41-\frac{0.059}{6} \log \left(5 \times 10^2\right) {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}=1.41-\frac{0.059}{6}(2.6990) {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}=1.41-0.0265 {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}=1.3835 {~V}{/tex}
solution: {tex} 2 {Al}+3 {Ni}^{2+} \rightarrow 2 {Al}^{3+}+3 {Ni} {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}={E}^{\circ}{ }_{{Ni} ^{2+}/ {Ni}}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{{Al}^{ 3+}/ {Al}} ; {/tex} {tex}{E}^{\circ} _\text { cell }=-0.25-(-1.66)=1.41 {~V} {/tex} {tex} {n}=6 {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}={E}^{\circ}_ \text { cell}- \frac{2.303 {RT}}{{nF}} \log \frac{\left[{Al}^{3+}\right]^2}{\left[{Ni}^{2+}\right]^3} {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}=1.41-\frac{0.059}{6} \log \frac{[0.002]^2}{[0.002]^3} {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}=1.41-\frac{0.059}{6} \log \frac{\left[2 \times 10^{-3}\right]^2}{\left[2 \times 10^{-3}\right]^3} {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}=1.41-\frac{0.059}{6} \log \left(5 \times 10^2\right) {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}=1.41-\frac{0.059}{6}(2.6990) {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}=1.41-0.0265 {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}=1.3835 {~V}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} 2 {Al}+3 {Ni}^{2+} \rightarrow 2 {Al}^{3+}+3 {Ni} {/tex}
{tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}={E}^{\circ}{ }_{{Ni} ^{2+}/ {Ni}}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{{Al}^{ 3+}/ {Al}} ; {/tex} {tex}{E}^{\circ} _\text { cell }=-0.25-(-1.66)=1.41 {~V} {/tex}
{tex} {n}=6 {/tex}

{tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}=1.41-\frac{0.059}{6} \log \frac{[0.002]^2}{[0.002]^3} {/tex}

explanation: Potential difference between two electrodes of a galvanic cell. subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Calculate the standard Gibbs energy ({tex}\Delta_{{r}} {G}^{\circ}{/tex}) of the following reaction at {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex} :
    {tex}{Au}({s})+{Ca}^{2+}(1 {M}) \rightarrow {Au}^{3+}(1 {M})+{Ca}({s}){/tex}
    {tex}{E}_{{Au}^{3+} / {Au}}^{\circ}=+1.5 {~V}, {E}_{{Ca}^{2+} / {Ca}}^{\circ}=-2.87 {~V}{/tex}
    Predict whether the reaction will be spontaneous or not at 25 {tex}{ }^{\circ} {C}{/tex}.
    {tex}\left[1 {~F}=96500 {C} {mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex}
  2. Tarnished silver contains {tex}{Ag}_{2} {~S}{/tex}. Can this tarnish be removed by placing tarnished silverware in an aluminium pan containing an inert electrolytic solution such as NaCl? The standard electrode potential for half reaction:
    {tex}{Ag}_{2} {~S}({~s})+2 {e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 {Ag}({s})+{S}^{2-}{/tex} is -0.71 V and for
    {tex}{Al}^{3+}+3 {e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 {Al}({s}){/tex} is -1.66 V
question_text: Calculate the standard Gibbs energy ( {tex}\Delta_{{r}} {G}^{\circ}{/tex} ) of the following reaction at {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex} : {tex}{Au}({s})+{Ca}^{2+}(1 {M}) \rightarrow {Au}^{3+}(1 {M})+{Ca}({s}){/tex} {tex}{E}_{{Au}^{3+} / {Au}}^{\circ}=+1.5 {~V}, {E}_{{Ca}^{2+} / {Ca}}^{\circ}=-2.87 {~V}{/tex} Predict whether the reaction will be spontaneous or not at 25 {tex}{ }^{\circ} {C}{/tex} . {tex}\left[1 {~F}=96500 {C} {mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex} Tarnished silver contains {tex}{Ag}_{2} {~S}{/tex} . Can this tarnish be removed by placing tarnished silverware in an aluminium pan containing an inert electrolytic solution such as NaCl? The standard electrode potential for half reaction: {tex}{Ag}_{2} {~S}({~s})+2 {e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 {Ag}({s})+{S}^{2-}{/tex} is -0.71 V and for {tex}{Al}^{3+}+3 {e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 {Al}({s}){/tex} is -1.66 V
explanation: {tex}{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cell }}={E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {anode }}{/tex} {tex}=-2.87-1.5 {~V}{/tex} {tex}=-4.37 {~V}{/tex} {tex}\Delta G^{0}=-n F E^{\circ} _{\text {Cell }}{/tex} {tex}=-6 \times 96500 {X}(-4.37){/tex} {tex}=2530.230 {~kJ} / {mol}{/tex} Reaction is non-spontaneous. Yes, the tarnish can be removed. Aluminium has more negative standard electrode potential than silver so will reduce silver sulphide to silver, tarnish will be removed. {tex}3 {Ag}^{+}+{Al} \longrightarrow 3 {Ag}+{Al}^{3+}{/tex} {tex}{E}^{\circ} {C}_{\text {ell }}={E}^{{O}}{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {anode }}{/tex} {tex}=-0.71-(-1.66) {V}{/tex} {tex}=0.95 {~V}{/tex} This indicates that the reaction is feasible and tarnish can be removed.
solution: {tex}{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cell }}={E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {anode }}{/tex} {tex}=-2.87-1.5 {~V}{/tex} {tex}=-4.37 {~V}{/tex} {tex}\Delta G^{0}=-n F E^{\circ} _{\text {Cell }}{/tex} {tex}=-6 \times 96500 {X}(-4.37){/tex} {tex}=2530.230 {~kJ} / {mol}{/tex} Reaction is non-spontaneous. Yes, the tarnish can be removed. Aluminium has more negative standard electrode potential than silver so will reduce silver sulphide to silver, tarnish will be removed. {tex}3 {Ag}^{+}+{Al} \longrightarrow 3 {Ag}+{Al}^{3+}{/tex} {tex}{E}^{\circ} {C}_{\text {ell }}={E}^{{O}}{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {anode }}{/tex} {tex}=-0.71-(-1.66) {V}{/tex} {tex}=0.95 {~V}{/tex} This indicates that the reaction is feasible and tarnish can be removed.
solution_html:
1. {tex}{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cell }}={E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {anode }}{/tex}
    {tex}=-2.87-1.5 {~V}{/tex}
    {tex}=-4.37 {~V}{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta G^{0}=-n F E^{\circ} _{\text {Cell }}{/tex}
    {tex}=-6 \times 96500 {X}(-4.37){/tex}
    {tex}=2530.230 {~kJ} / {mol}{/tex}
    Reaction is non-spontaneous.
  2. Yes, the tarnish can be removed.
    Aluminium has more negative standard electrode potential than silver so will reduce silver sulphide to silver, tarnish will be removed. 
    {tex}3 {Ag}^{+}+{Al} \longrightarrow 3 {Ag}+{Al}^{3+}{/tex}
    {tex}{E}^{\circ} {C}_{\text {ell }}={E}^{{O}}{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {anode }}{/tex}
    {tex}=-0.71-(-1.66) {V}{/tex}
    {tex}=0.95 {~V}{/tex}
    This indicates that the reaction is feasible and tarnish can be removed.

question_text: Calculate the cell voltage of the voltaic cell which is set up by joining following half-cells at {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex} : {tex}{Al} / {Al}^{3+}(0.001 {M}){/tex} and {tex}{Ni} / {Ni}^{2+}(0.1 {M}){/tex} Given: {tex}{E}_{{Ni}^{2+} / {Ni}}^{0}=-0.25 {~V}, {E}_{{Al}^{3+} / {Al}}^{0}=-1.66 {~V}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. The resistance of a conductivity cell containing 0.001 M KCl solution at 298 K is 1000 ohm. What is the cell constant if conductivity of 0.001 M KCl solution at 298 K is {tex}0.125 \times 10^{-3} {~S} {~cm}^{-1}{/tex}?
  2. Calculate the {tex}{E}_{{Mg}^{2+} / {Mg}}{/tex} potential for the following half cell at {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex}:
    {tex} {Mg} / {Mg}^{2+}\left(1 \times 10^{-4} {M}\right) ; {E}_{{Mg}^{2+} / {Mg}}^{\circ}=+2.36 {~V} {/tex}
    [Given : {tex}\log 10=1{/tex}]
  3. What is the effect of temperature on the electrical conductance of metallic conductor?
question_text: The resistance of a conductivity cell containing 0.001 M KCl solution at 298 K is 1000 ohm. What is the cell constant if conductivity of 0.001 M KCl solution at 298 K is {tex}0.125 \times 10^{-3} {~S} {~cm}^{-1}{/tex} ? Calculate the {tex}{E}_{{Mg}^{2+} / {Mg}}{/tex} potential for the following half cell at {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex} : {tex} {Mg} / {Mg}^{2+}\left(1 \times 10^{-4} {M}\right) ; {E}_{{Mg}^{2+} / {Mg}}^{\circ}=+2.36 {~V} {/tex} [Given : {tex}\log 10=1{/tex} ] What is the effect of temperature on the electrical conductance of metallic conductor?
explanation: {tex} {k}={G}^* / {R} {/tex} {tex} {G}^* ={k} \times {R}=0.125 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 {/tex} {tex} =0.125 {~cm}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} {E} {Mg}^{2+} / {Mg}= {E}^0 {Mg}^{2+} / {Mg}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{1}{\left[{Mg}^{2+}\right]} {/tex} {tex} =2.36 {~V}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{1}{10^{-4}} {/tex} {tex} =2.36-0.0295 \times 4 \log 10 {/tex} {tex} =2.242 {~V} {/tex} It decreases with increase in temperature
solution: {tex} {k}={G}^* / {R} {/tex} {tex} {G}^* ={k} \times {R}=0.125 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 {/tex} {tex} =0.125 {~cm}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} {E} {Mg}^{2+} / {Mg}= {E}^0 {Mg}^{2+} / {Mg}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{1}{\left[{Mg}^{2+}\right]} {/tex} {tex} =2.36 {~V}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{1}{10^{-4}} {/tex} {tex} =2.36-0.0295 \times 4 \log 10 {/tex} {tex} =2.242 {~V} {/tex} It decreases with increase in temperature
solution_html:
1. {tex} {k}={G}^* / {R} {/tex}
    {tex} {G}^* ={k} \times {R}=0.125 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 {/tex}
    {tex} =0.125 {~cm}^{-1} {/tex}
  2. {tex} {E} {Mg}^{2+} / {Mg}= {E}^0 {Mg}^{2+} / {Mg}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{1}{\left[{Mg}^{2+}\right]} {/tex}
    {tex} =2.36 {~V}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{1}{10^{-4}} {/tex}
    {tex} =2.36-0.0295 \times 4 \log 10 {/tex}
    {tex} =2.242 {~V} {/tex}
  3. It decreases with increase in temperature

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Calculate {tex}{E}_{\text {cell }}{/tex} of a galvanic cell in which the following reaction takes place at {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex}:
    {tex}{Zn}({s})+{Pb}^{2+}(0 \cdot 02 {M}) \longrightarrow {Zn}^{2+}(0 \cdot 1 {M})+{Pb}({s}){/tex}
    ［Given: {tex}{E}_{{Zn}^{2+} / {Zn}}^{\circ}=-0 \cdot 76 {~V}, {E}_{{Pb}^{2+} / {Pb}}^{\circ}=-0 \cdot 13 {~V}{/tex};
    {tex}\log 2=0 \cdot 3010, \log 4=0 \cdot 6021, \log 5=0 \cdot 6990]{/tex}.
  2. State Faraday's first law of electrolysis. How much electricity, in terms of Faraday, is required to reduce one mol of {tex}{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex}to {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} ion?
question_text: Calculate {tex}{E}_{\text {cell }}{/tex} of a galvanic cell in which the following reaction takes place at {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex} : {tex}{Zn}({s})+{Pb}^{2+}(0 \cdot 02 {M}) \longrightarrow {Zn}^{2+}(0 \cdot 1 {M})+{Pb}({s}){/tex} ［Given: {tex}{E}_{{Zn}^{2+} / {Zn}}^{\circ}=-0 \cdot 76 {~V}, {E}_{{Pb}^{2+} / {Pb}}^{\circ}=-0 \cdot 13 {~V}{/tex} ; {tex}\log 2=0 \cdot 3010, \log 4=0 \cdot 6021, \log 5=0 \cdot 6990]{/tex} . State Faraday's first law of electrolysis. How much electricity, in terms of Faraday, is required to reduce one mol of {tex}{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} to {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} ion?
explanation: {tex} {E}_{\text {Cell }}=\left(E^o{ }_c-E^o{ }_a\right)-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \left[\frac{Z n^{2+}}{P b^{2+}}\right] {/tex} {tex} =[(-0 \cdot 13)-(-0 \cdot 76)]-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{0.1}{0.02} {/tex} {tex} =0.63-0.0295 \log 5 {/tex} {tex} =0.63-0.0295 \times 0.699 {/tex} {tex} =0.63-0.02 {/tex} {tex} =0.61 {~V} {/tex} The amount of chemical reaction which occurs at any electrode during electrolysis by a current is proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.
solution: {tex} {E}_{\text {Cell }}=\left(E^o{ }_c-E^o{ }_a\right)-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \left[\frac{Z n^{2+}}{P b^{2+}}\right] {/tex} {tex} =[(-0 \cdot 13)-(-0 \cdot 76)]-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{0.1}{0.02} {/tex} {tex} =0.63-0.0295 \log 5 {/tex} {tex} =0.63-0.0295 \times 0.699 {/tex} {tex} =0.63-0.02 {/tex} {tex} =0.61 {~V} {/tex} The amount of chemical reaction which occurs at any electrode during electrolysis by a current is proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.
solution_html:
1. {tex} {E}_{\text {Cell }}=\left(E^o{ }_c-E^o{ }_a\right)-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \left[\frac{Z n^{2+}}{P b^{2+}}\right] {/tex}
    {tex} =[(-0 \cdot 13)-(-0 \cdot 76)]-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{0.1}{0.02} {/tex}
    {tex} =0.63-0.0295 \log 5 {/tex}
    {tex} =0.63-0.0295 \times 0.699 {/tex}
    {tex} =0.63-0.02 {/tex}
    {tex} =0.61 {~V} {/tex}
  2. The amount of chemical reaction which occurs at any electrode during electrolysis by a current is proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Answer the following questions:

  1. The conductivity of 0.20 M solution of KCl is {tex}2.48 \times 10^{-2} {~S} {~cm}^{-1}{/tex}. Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex}).
    {tex}{\left[\text { Given : } \lambda_{\left({K}^{+}\right)}^{\circ}\right.} =73.5 {~S} {~cm}^{2} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda_{\left({Cl}^{-}\right)}^{\circ} \left.=76.5 {~S} {~cm}^{2} {~mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex}
  2. Calculate {tex}\Delta_{{r}} {G}^{\circ}{/tex} of the following cell:
    {tex}{Mg}({~s})+{Cu}^{2+}({aq}) \longrightarrow {Mg}^{2+}({aq})+{Cu}({~s}){/tex}
    {tex}{\left[\text { Given }: {E}_{{Mg}^{2+} / {Mg}}^{\circ}=-2 \cdot 37 {~V}, {E}_{{Cu}^{2+} / {Cu}}^{\circ}=+0 \cdot 34 {~V}\right.}{/tex}{tex}\left. 1 {~F}=96500 {C} {~mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex}
  3. What type of cell is mercury cell ? Why is it more advantageous than dry cell?
question_text: Answer the following questions: The conductivity of 0.20 M solution of KCl is {tex}2.48 \times 10^{-2} {~S} {~cm}^{-1}{/tex} . Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation ( {tex}\alpha{/tex} ). {tex}{\left[\text { Given : } \lambda_{\left({K}^{+}\right)}^{\circ}\right.} =73.5 {~S} {~cm}^{2} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\left({Cl}^{-}\right)}^{\circ} \left.=76.5 {~S} {~cm}^{2} {~mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex} Calculate {tex}\Delta_{{r}} {G}^{\circ}{/tex} of the following cell: {tex}{Mg}({~s})+{Cu}^{2+}({aq}) \longrightarrow {Mg}^{2+}({aq})+{Cu}({~s}){/tex} {tex}{\left[\text { Given }: {E}_{{Mg}^{2+} / {Mg}}^{\circ}=-2 \cdot 37 {~V}, {E}_{{Cu}^{2+} / {Cu}}^{\circ}=+0 \cdot 34 {~V}\right.}{/tex} {tex}\left. 1 {~F}=96500 {C} {~mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex} What type of cell is mercury cell ? Why is it more advantageous than dry cell?
explanation: {tex} \Lambda_m=\frac{K}{c} \times 1000 {/tex} {tex} \Lambda_m =\frac{2.48 \times 10^{-2}}{0.2} \times 1000 {/tex} {tex} =124 {Scm}^2 {~mol}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} =\lambda_{K^{+}}+\lambda_{{Cl}}{ }^{-} {/tex} {tex} \Lambda_m =73.5+76.5 {/tex} {tex} =150 {Scm} {mol}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \alpha =\frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{124}{150} {/tex} {tex} = 0.827 {/tex} {tex} \text {} {E}_{\text {cell }}^0 ={E}^0{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^0 \text { anode } {/tex} {tex} =0.34-(-2.37) {/tex} {tex} =2.71 {~V} {/tex} {tex} \Delta_{{rG}} {G}^0= -n {FE}^0 \text { cell } {/tex} {tex} = -2 \times 96500 \times 2.71 {/tex} {tex} = -523030 {Jmol}^{-1} \text { or }-523.03 {kJmol}^{-1} {/tex} Primary cell Maintains constant potential throughout its usage/ longer lifespan
solution: {tex} \Lambda_m=\frac{K}{c} \times 1000 {/tex} {tex} \Lambda_m =\frac{2.48 \times 10^{-2}}{0.2} \times 1000 {/tex} {tex} =124 {Scm}^2 {~mol}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} =\lambda_{K^{+}}+\lambda_{{Cl}}{ }^{-} {/tex} {tex} \Lambda_m =73.5+76.5 {/tex} {tex} =150 {Scm} {mol}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \alpha =\frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{124}{150} {/tex} {tex} = 0.827 {/tex} {tex} \text {} {E}_{\text {cell }}^0 ={E}^0{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^0 \text { anode } {/tex} {tex} =0.34-(-2.37) {/tex} {tex} =2.71 {~V} {/tex} {tex} \Delta_{{rG}} {G}^0= -n {FE}^0 \text { cell } {/tex} {tex} = -2 \times 96500 \times 2.71 {/tex} {tex} = -523030 {Jmol}^{-1} \text { or }-523.03 {kJmol}^{-1} {/tex} Primary cell Maintains constant potential throughout its usage/ longer lifespan
solution_html:
1. {tex} \Lambda_m=\frac{K}{c} \times 1000 {/tex}
    {tex} \Lambda_m =\frac{2.48 \times 10^{-2}}{0.2} \times 1000 {/tex}
    {tex} =124 {Scm}^2 {~mol}^{-1} {/tex}
    {tex} =\lambda_{K^{+}}+\lambda_{{Cl}}{ }^{-} {/tex}
    {tex} \Lambda_m =73.5+76.5 {/tex}
    {tex} =150 {Scm} {mol}^{-1} {/tex}
    {tex} \alpha =\frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m} {/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{124}{150} {/tex}
    {tex} = 0.827 {/tex}
  2. {tex} \text {} {E}_{\text {cell }}^0 ={E}^0{ }_{\text {cathode }}-{E}^0 \text { anode } {/tex}
    {tex} =0.34-(-2.37) {/tex}
    {tex} =2.71 {~V} {/tex}
    {tex} \Delta_{{rG}} {G}^0= -n {FE}^0 \text { cell } {/tex}
    {tex} = -2 \times 96500 \times 2.71 {/tex}
    {tex} = -523030 {Jmol}^{-1} \text { or }-523.03 {kJmol}^{-1} {/tex}
  3. Primary cell Maintains constant potential throughout its usage/ longer lifespan

question_html:
Calculate emf of the following cell:
Zn (s) | Zn2+ (0.1 M) || Sn2+ (0.001 M) | Sn (s)
Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\frac {\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} }{ \mathrm{Zn}}}^0=-0 \cdot 76 \mathrm{~V},{/tex} {tex} \mathrm{E}_{\frac {\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} }{ \mathrm{Sn}}}^0=-0 \cdot 14 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}
[log 10 = 1]
question_text: Calculate emf of the following cell: Zn (s) | Zn 2+ (0.1 M) || Sn 2+ (0.001 M) | Sn (s) Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\frac {\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} }{ \mathrm{Zn}}}^0=-0 \cdot 76 \mathrm{~V},{/tex} {tex} \mathrm{E}_{\frac {\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} }{ \mathrm{Sn}}}^0=-0 \cdot 14 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} [log 10 = 1]
solution_html:
ECell = {tex}\left(E^o c^{-} E^o{ }_a\right)-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \left[\frac{Z n^{2+}}{S n^{2+}}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}[(-0 \cdot 14)-(-0 \cdot 76)]-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{0.1}{0.001}{/tex}
= {tex}+0.62-\frac{0.059}{2} \times 2{/tex}
= (0.62 - 0.059)V
= 0.561 V

question_text: When a certain conductivity cell was filled with 0.05 M KCl solution, it has a resistance of 100 ohm at 25 o C. When the same cell was filled with 0.02 M AgNO 3 solution, the resistance was 90 ohm. Calculate the conductivity and molar conductivity of AgNO 3 solution. solution: Cell constant(G*) = Conductivity {tex}\times{/tex} Resistance {tex} =1.35 \times 10^{-2} \times 100 {/tex} = 1.35 cm -1 Cell constant (G*) = Conductivity {tex}\times{/tex} Resistance {tex} 1.35 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}{/tex} = k {tex}\times{/tex} 90 {tex} \frac {1.35 }{ 90}=\mathrm{k} {/tex} k = 0.015 Scm -1 Molar conductivity {tex}\left(\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\right)=\frac {\mathrm{k} \times 1000 }{ \mathrm{C} }{/tex} {tex} =\frac{0.015 \times 1000}{0.02} {/tex} = 750 Scm 2 /mol

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Batteries and fuel cells are very useful forms of galvanic cell. Any battery or cell that we use as a source of electrical energy is basically a galvanic cell. However, for a battery to be of practical use it should be reasonably light, compact and its voltage should not vary appreciably during its use. There are mainly two types of batteries - primary batteries and secondary batteries.
In the primary batteries, the reaction occurs only once and after use over a period of time the battery becomes dead and cannot be reused again, whereas the secondary batteries are rechargeable.
Production of electricity by thermal plants is not a very efficient method and is a major source of pollution. To solve this problem, galvanic cells are designed in such a way that energy of combustion of fuels is directly converted into electrical energy, and these are known as fuel cells. One such fuel cell was used in the Apollo space programme.
Answer the following questions:

  1. How do primary batteries differ from secondary batteries? (1)
  2. The cell potential of Mercury cell is 1.35 V, and remains constant during its life. Give reason. (1)
  3. Write the reactions involved in the recharging of the lead storage battery. (2)

    OR

    Write two advantages of fuel cells over other galvanic cells. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Batteries and fuel cells are very useful forms of galvanic cell. Any battery or cell that we use as a source of electrical energy is basically a galvanic cell. However, for a battery to be of practical use it should be reasonably light, compact and its voltage should not vary appreciably during its use. There are mainly two types of batteries - primary batteries and secondary batteries. In the primary batteries, the reaction occurs only once and after use over a period of time the battery becomes dead and cannot be reused again, whereas the secondary batteries are rechargeable. Production of electricity by thermal plants is not a very efficient method and is a major source of pollution. To solve this problem, galvanic cells are designed in such a way that energy of combustion of fuels is directly converted into electrical energy, and these are known as fuel cells. One such fuel cell was used in the Apollo space programme. Answer the following questions: How do primary batteries differ from secondary batteries? (1) The cell potential of Mercury cell is 1.35 V, and remains constant during its life. Give reason. (1) Write the reactions involved in the recharging of the lead storage battery. (2) OR Write two advantages of fuel cells over other galvanic cells. (2)
explanation: Primary batteries are not rechargeable while secondary batteries are rechargeable. Overall reaction does not involve any ion in solution whose concentration can change during its lifetime. Cathode: PbSO 4 (s) + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Pb(s) + SO 4 2- (aq) Anode: PbSO 4 (s) + 2H 2 O (I) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} PbO 2 (s) + SO 4 2- (aq) + 4H + (aq) + 2e - OR (i) More efficiency (ii) Pollution free
solution: Primary batteries are not rechargeable while secondary batteries are rechargeable. Overall reaction does not involve any ion in solution whose concentration can change during its lifetime. Cathode: PbSO 4 (s) + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Pb(s) + SO 4 2- (aq) Anode: PbSO 4 (s) + 2H 2 O (I) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} PbO 2 (s) + SO 4 2- (aq) + 4H + (aq) + 2e - OR (i) More efficiency (ii) Pollution free
solution_html:
1. Primary batteries are not rechargeable while secondary batteries are rechargeable.
  2. Overall reaction does not involve any ion in solution whose concentration can change during its lifetime.
  3. Cathode: PbSO4(s) + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Pb(s) + SO42-(aq)
    Anode: PbSO4(s) + 2H2O (I) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} PbO2(s) + SO42-(aq) + 4H+(aq) + 2e-

    OR

    (i) More efficiency (ii) Pollution free

question_html:
1. The conductivity of 0.0025 mol L-1 acetic acid is 5.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 S cm-1. Calculate its degree of dissociation if {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} for acetic acid is 390 S cm2 mol-1.
  2. Write anode, cathode and overall reaction of lead storage battery.
question_text: The conductivity of 0.0025 mol L -1 acetic acid is 5.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 S cm -1 . Calculate its degree of dissociation if {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} for acetic acid is 390 S cm 2 mol -1 . Write anode, cathode and overall reaction of lead storage battery.
explanation: {tex} \Lambda_m=\frac{K}{M} \times 1000 {/tex} {tex} \Lambda_m=\frac{5.25 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0025} \times 1000 {/tex} {tex} =21 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \alpha=\frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^{\circ}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{21 \mathrm{~s} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{390 \mathrm{~s} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.053 Anode: Pb + SO 4 2- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} PbSO 4 + 2e - Cathode: PbO 2 + SO 4 2- + 4H + + 2e- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} PbSO 4 + 2H 2 O Overall: Pb + PbO 2 + 2H 2 SO 4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2PbSO 4 + 2H 2 O
solution: {tex} \Lambda_m=\frac{K}{M} \times 1000 {/tex} {tex} \Lambda_m=\frac{5.25 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0025} \times 1000 {/tex} {tex} =21 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \alpha=\frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^{\circ}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{21 \mathrm{~s} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{390 \mathrm{~s} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.053 Anode: Pb + SO 4 2- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} PbSO 4 + 2e - Cathode: PbO 2 + SO 4 2- + 4H + + 2e- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} PbSO 4 + 2H 2 O Overall: Pb + PbO 2 + 2H 2 SO 4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2PbSO 4 + 2H 2 O
solution_html:
1. {tex} \Lambda_m=\frac{K}{M} \times 1000 {/tex}
    {tex} \Lambda_m=\frac{5.25 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0025} \times 1000 {/tex}
    {tex} =21 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} {/tex}
    {tex} \alpha=\frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^{\circ}} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{21 \mathrm{~s} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{390 \mathrm{~s} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{/tex}
    = 0.053
  2. Anode: Pb + SO4 2- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} PbSO4 + 2e-
    Cathode: PbO2 + SO42- + 4H+ + 2e- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} PbSO4 + 2H2O
    Overall: Pb + PbO2 + 2H2SO4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2PbSO4 + 2H2O

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The conductivity of 0.2 M solution of KCl is 2.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 S cm-1. Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex}).
Given:
{tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{K}^{+}}^0{/tex} = 73.5 S cm2 mol-1
{tex}\lambda^0{ }_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}{/tex} = 76.5 S cm2 mol-1
question_text: The conductivity of 0.2 M solution of KCl is 2.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 S cm -1 . Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation ( {tex}\alpha{/tex} ). Given: {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{K}^{+}}^0{/tex} = 73.5 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\lambda^0{ }_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}{/tex} = 76.5 S cm 2 mol -1
explanation: {tex} \Delta_m=\frac {{k} }{ {C} }{/tex} {tex} \Lambda_m=\frac{{kx1000}}{{m}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1000 {~cm}^3 / {L} \times 2.48 \times 10^{-2}}{0.2 {~mol} {~L}^{-1}} {/tex} = 124 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex} \Lambda_m^{\circ}=\lambda^o_{+} + \lambda^0_-{/tex} = (73.5 + 76.5) S cm 2 mol -1 = 150 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha=\frac {\Lambda_m }{ \Lambda_m^{\circ} }{/tex} {tex} =\frac{124 {~S} {~cm}^2 {~mol}^{-1}}{150 {~S} {~cm}^2 {~mol}^{-1}} {/tex} = 0.826 (approx.)
solution: {tex} \Delta_m=\frac {{k} }{ {C} }{/tex} {tex} \Lambda_m=\frac{{kx1000}}{{m}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1000 {~cm}^3 / {L} \times 2.48 \times 10^{-2}}{0.2 {~mol} {~L}^{-1}} {/tex} = 124 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex} \Lambda_m^{\circ}=\lambda^o_{+} + \lambda^0_-{/tex} = (73.5 + 76.5) S cm 2 mol -1 = 150 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha=\frac {\Lambda_m }{ \Lambda_m^{\circ} }{/tex} {tex} =\frac{124 {~S} {~cm}^2 {~mol}^{-1}}{150 {~S} {~cm}^2 {~mol}^{-1}} {/tex} = 0.826 (approx.)
solution_html:
{tex} \Delta_m=\frac {{k} }{ {C} }{/tex}
{tex} \Lambda_m=\frac{{kx1000}}{{m}} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1000 {~cm}^3 / {L} \times 2.48 \times 10^{-2}}{0.2 {~mol} {~L}^{-1}} {/tex}
= 124 S cm2 mol-1
{tex} \Lambda_m^{\circ}=\lambda^o_{+} + \lambda^0_-{/tex}
= (73.5 + 76.5) S cm2 mol-1
= 150 S cm2 mol-1
{tex}\alpha=\frac {\Lambda_m }{ \Lambda_m^{\circ} }{/tex}
{tex} =\frac{124 {~S} {~cm}^2 {~mol}^{-1}}{150 {~S} {~cm}^2 {~mol}^{-1}} {/tex}
= 0.826 (approx.)

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Calculate emf of the following cell at 25oC:
    Zn (s) | Zn2+ (0.001M) || Cd2+ (0.1 M) | Cd (s)
    Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}^\text o_{\frac {\text {Zn}^{2+}}{\text {Zn}}}{/tex} = -0.76 V, {tex}\mathrm{E}^\text o_{\frac {\text {Cd}^{2+}}{\text {Cd}}}{/tex} = -0.40 V [log 10 = 1]
  2. State Faraday's second law of electrolysis. How will the pH of aqueous NaCl solution be affected when it is electrolysed?
question_text: Calculate emf of the following cell at 25 o C: Zn (s) | Zn 2+ (0.001M) || Cd 2+ (0.1 M) | Cd (s) Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}^\text o_{\frac {\text {Zn}^{2+}}{\text {Zn}}}{/tex} = -0.76 V, {tex}\mathrm{E}^\text o_{\frac {\text {Cd}^{2+}}{\text {Cd}}}{/tex} = -0.40 V [log 10 = 1] State Faraday's second law of electrolysis. How will the pH of aqueous NaCl solution be affected when it is electrolysed?
explanation: E o cell = E o cathode - E o anode = -0.40 - (-0.76)V = 0.36V E cell = E o Cell {tex}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[{Cd}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} {tex} =0.36 \frac{-0.059}{2} \log \frac{[0.001]}{[0 \cdot 1]} {/tex} {tex} =0.36 \frac{-0.059}{2} \log 10^{-2} {/tex} = 0.36 + 0.059 = 0.419 V When same amount of electricity is passed through the electrolytic solutions connected in series, weight of substance deposited or liberated at each electrode is directly proportional to its chemical equivalent weights. pH of solution will increase
solution: E o cell = E o cathode - E o anode = -0.40 - (-0.76)V = 0.36V E cell = E o Cell {tex}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[{Cd}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} {tex} =0.36 \frac{-0.059}{2} \log \frac{[0.001]}{[0 \cdot 1]} {/tex} {tex} =0.36 \frac{-0.059}{2} \log 10^{-2} {/tex} = 0.36 + 0.059 = 0.419 V When same amount of electricity is passed through the electrolytic solutions connected in series, weight of substance deposited or liberated at each electrode is directly proportional to its chemical equivalent weights. pH of solution will increase
solution_html:
1. Eocell = Eocathode - Eoanode
    = -0.40 - (-0.76)V
    = 0.36V
    Ecell = EoCell {tex}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[{Cd}^{2+}\right]}{/tex}
    {tex} =0.36 \frac{-0.059}{2} \log \frac{[0.001]}{[0 \cdot 1]} {/tex}
    {tex} =0.36 \frac{-0.059}{2} \log 10^{-2} {/tex}
    = 0.36 + 0.059
    = 0.419 V
  2. When same amount of electricity is passed through the electrolytic solutions connected in series, weight of substance deposited or liberated at each electrode is directly proportional to its chemical equivalent weights.
    pH of solution will increase

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Calculate the {tex}\Delta_\text r\text G^\text o{/tex} and log Kc for the following cell reaction:
    Fe (s) + Ag+ (aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Fe2+(aq) + Ag (s)
    Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}^\text o_{\frac {\text {Fe}^{2+}}{\text {Fe}}}{/tex} = -0.44 V, {tex}\mathrm{E}^\text o_{\frac {\text {Ag}^{+}}{\text {Ag}}}{/tex} = 0.80 V, 1 F = 96500 C mol-1
  2. Write any two advantages of the fuel cells over primary and secondary batteries?
  3. How many Faradays are required for the oxidation of 1 mole of H2O to O2?
question_text: Calculate the {tex}\Delta_\text r\text G^\text o{/tex} and log K c for the following cell reaction: Fe (s) + Ag + (aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Fe 2+ (aq) + Ag (s) Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}^\text o_{\frac {\text {Fe}^{2+}}{\text {Fe}}}{/tex} = -0.44 V, {tex}\mathrm{E}^\text o_{\frac {\text {Ag}^{+}}{\text {Ag}}}{/tex} = 0.80 V, 1 F = 96500 C mol -1 Write any two advantages of the fuel cells over primary and secondary batteries? How many Faradays are required for the oxidation of 1 mole of H 2 O to O 2 ?
explanation: E o cell = E o cathode - E o anode = 0.80V - (-0.44V) = 1.24 V {tex} \Delta {\text G}^{{\text o}}=-{\text {nFE}}_{\text {cell }}^{{\text o}} {/tex} = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.24 J mol -1 = -239320 J mol -1 {tex} \log {\text k}_{{\text c}}=\frac{{\text {nE}}_{\text {cell }}^{{o}}}{0 \cdot 059} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2 \times 1.24 {\text V}}{0.059} {/tex} = 42.0 More efficiency and Pollution free (Any other two correct advantages) 2F / 2 H 2 O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} O 2 + 4H + + 4e - 1 mole = 2e - {tex}\therefore{/tex} 2F
solution: E o cell = E o cathode - E o anode = 0.80V - (-0.44V) = 1.24 V {tex} \Delta {\text G}^{{\text o}}=-{\text {nFE}}_{\text {cell }}^{{\text o}} {/tex} = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.24 J mol -1 = -239320 J mol -1 {tex} \log {\text k}_{{\text c}}=\frac{{\text {nE}}_{\text {cell }}^{{o}}}{0 \cdot 059} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2 \times 1.24 {\text V}}{0.059} {/tex} = 42.0 More efficiency and Pollution free (Any other two correct advantages) 2F / 2 H 2 O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} O 2 + 4H + + 4e - 1 mole = 2e - {tex}\therefore{/tex} 2F
solution_html:
1. Eocell = Eocathode - Eoanode
    = 0.80V - (-0.44V)
    = 1.24 V
    {tex} \Delta {\text G}^{{\text o}}=-{\text {nFE}}_{\text {cell }}^{{\text o}} {/tex}
    = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.24 J mol-1
    = -239320 J mol-1
    {tex} \log {\text k}_{{\text c}}=\frac{{\text {nE}}_{\text {cell }}^{{o}}}{0 \cdot 059} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{2 \times 1.24 {\text V}}{0.059} {/tex}
    = 42.0
  2. More efficiency and Pollution free (Any other two correct advantages)
  3. 2F /
    2 H2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} O2 + 4H+ + 4e-
    1 mole = 2e- {tex}\therefore{/tex} 2F

1. The resistance of 0.05 M CH3COOH solution is found to be 100 ohm. If the cell constant is 0.0354 cm-1, calculate the molar conductivity of the acetic acid solution.
  2. State Faraday's first law of electrolysis. How much charge in Faraday is required for the reduction of 1 mol of {tex}\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}{/tex} to Mn2+?
question_text: The resistance of 0.05 M CH 3 COOH solution is found to be 100 ohm. If the cell constant is 0.0354 cm -1 , calculate the molar conductivity of the acetic acid solution. State Faraday's first law of electrolysis. How much charge in Faraday is required for the reduction of 1 mol of {tex}\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}{/tex} to Mn 2+ ?
explanation: {tex}{r}K=\frac{1}{R}\left(\frac{L}{A}\right){/tex} or {tex}\text k=\frac {\text G^* }{\text R}{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{1}{100}{/tex} (0.0354) = {tex}3.54 \times 10^{-4} \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_m=\frac{K}{M} \times 1000{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \cdot 54 \times 10^{-4}}{0 \cdot 05} \times 1000{/tex} = {tex}7.08 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2{/tex} mol -1 or 7.08 S cm 2 mol -1 The amount of chemical reaction which occurs at any electrode during electrolysis by a current is proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte. / m = ZIt where m = mass of the substance deposited , Z = electrochemical equivalent, I = current, t = time. 5F
solution: {tex}{r}K=\frac{1}{R}\left(\frac{L}{A}\right){/tex} or {tex}\text k=\frac {\text G^* }{\text R}{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{1}{100}{/tex} (0.0354) = {tex}3.54 \times 10^{-4} \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_m=\frac{K}{M} \times 1000{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \cdot 54 \times 10^{-4}}{0 \cdot 05} \times 1000{/tex} = {tex}7.08 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2{/tex} mol -1 or 7.08 S cm 2 mol -1 The amount of chemical reaction which occurs at any electrode during electrolysis by a current is proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte. / m = ZIt where m = mass of the substance deposited , Z = electrochemical equivalent, I = current, t = time. 5F
solution_html:
1. {tex}{r}K=\frac{1}{R}\left(\frac{L}{A}\right){/tex}
    or
    {tex}\text k=\frac {\text G^* }{\text R}{/tex}
    {tex}k=\frac{1}{100}{/tex} (0.0354)
    = {tex}3.54 \times 10^{-4} \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}{/tex}
    {tex}\Lambda_m=\frac{K}{M} \times 1000{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{3 \cdot 54 \times 10^{-4}}{0 \cdot 05} \times 1000{/tex}
    = {tex}7.08 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2{/tex} mol-1 or 7.08 S cm2 mol-1
  2. The amount of chemical reaction which occurs at any electrode during electrolysis by a current is proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte. / m = ZIt where m = mass of the substance deposited , Z = electrochemical equivalent, I = current, t = time.
    5F

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
In a galvanic cell, chemical energy of a redox reaction is converted into electrical energy, whereas in an electrolytic cell the redox reaction occurs on passing electricity. The simplest galvanic cell is in which Zn rod is placed in a solution of {tex}{ZnSO}_{4}{/tex} and Cu rod is placed in a solution of {tex}{CuSO}_{4}{/tex}. The two rods are connected by a metallic wire through a voltmeter. The two solutions are joined by a salt bridge. The difference between the two electrode potentials of the two electrodes is known as electromotive force. In the process of electrolysis, the decomposition of a substance takes place by passing an electric current. One mole of electric charge when passed through a cell will discharge half a mole of a divalent metal ion such as {tex}{Cu}^{2+}{/tex}. This was first formulated by Faraday in the form of laws of electrolysis.
Answer the following questions:

  1. What is the function of a salt bridge in a galvanic cell? (1)
  2. When does galvanic cell behave like an electrolytic cell? (1)
  3. Can copper sulphate solution be stored in a pot made of zinc? Explain with the help of the value of {tex}{E}^{\circ}{/tex} cell. (2)
    ({tex}{E}^{\circ} {Cu}^{2+} / {Cu}=0.34 {~V}{/tex})
    ({tex}{E}^{\circ} {Zn}^{2+} / {Zn}=-0.76 {~V}{/tex})

    OR

    How much charge in terms of Faraday is required for the following: (2)
    1. 1 mol of {tex}{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex}to {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex}
    2. 1 mol of {tex}{H}_{2} {O}{/tex} to {tex}{O}_{2}{/tex}
question_text: In a galvanic cell, chemical energy of a redox reaction is converted into electrical energy, whereas in an electrolytic cell the redox reaction occurs on passing electricity. The simplest galvanic cell is in which Zn rod is placed in a solution of {tex}{ZnSO}_{4}{/tex} and Cu rod is placed in a solution of {tex}{CuSO}_{4}{/tex} . The two rods are connected by a metallic wire through a voltmeter. The two solutions are joined by a salt bridge. The difference between the two electrode potentials of the two electrodes is known as electromotive force. In the process of electrolysis, the decomposition of a substance takes place by passing an electric current. One mole of electric charge when passed through a cell will discharge half a mole of a divalent metal ion such as {tex}{Cu}^{2+}{/tex} . This was first formulated by Faraday in the form of laws of electrolysis. Answer the following questions: What is the function of a salt bridge in a galvanic cell? (1) When does galvanic cell behave like an electrolytic cell? (1) Can copper sulphate solution be stored in a pot made of zinc? Explain with the help of the value of {tex}{E}^{\circ}{/tex} cell. (2) ( {tex}{E}^{\circ} {Cu}^{2+} / {Cu}=0.34 {~V}{/tex} ) ( {tex}{E}^{\circ} {Zn}^{2+} / {Zn}=-0.76 {~V}{/tex} ) OR How much charge in terms of Faraday is required for the following: (2) 1 mol of {tex}{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} to {tex}{Mn}^{2+}{/tex} 1 mol of {tex}{H}_{2} {O}{/tex} to {tex}{O}_{2}{/tex}
explanation: It allows flow of ions and the circuit is completed / it maintains the electrical neutrality. When {tex}{E}_{\text {ext }} \gt {E}_{\text {cell }}{/tex} {tex}{E}^{{o}}{ }_{\text {cell }}={E}^{{o}} {Cu}^{2+} / {Cu}-{E}^{{o}} {Zn}^{2+} / {Zn}{/tex} {tex}=0 \cdot 34-(-0 \cdot 76)=1 \cdot 10 {~V}{/tex} As {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{0}=+v e{/tex} , the reaction takes place, so copper sulphate cannot be stored ina zinc pot. OR 5 F 2 F
solution: It allows flow of ions and the circuit is completed / it maintains the electrical neutrality. When {tex}{E}_{\text {ext }} \gt {E}_{\text {cell }}{/tex} {tex}{E}^{{o}}{ }_{\text {cell }}={E}^{{o}} {Cu}^{2+} / {Cu}-{E}^{{o}} {Zn}^{2+} / {Zn}{/tex} {tex}=0 \cdot 34-(-0 \cdot 76)=1 \cdot 10 {~V}{/tex} As {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{0}=+v e{/tex} , the reaction takes place, so copper sulphate cannot be stored ina zinc pot. OR 5 F 2 F
solution_html:
1. It allows flow of ions and the circuit is completed / it maintains the electrical neutrality.
  2. When {tex}{E}_{\text {ext }} \gt {E}_{\text {cell }}{/tex}
  3. {tex}{E}^{{o}}{ }_{\text {cell }}={E}^{{o}} {Cu}^{2+} / {Cu}-{E}^{{o}} {Zn}^{2+} / {Zn}{/tex}
    {tex}=0 \cdot 34-(-0 \cdot 76)=1 \cdot 10 {~V}{/tex}
    As {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{0}=+v e{/tex}, the reaction takes place, so copper sulphate cannot be stored ina zinc pot.

    OR

    1. 5 F
    2. 2 F

solution: {tex}{R}^{=} \rho \frac{l}{A}{/tex} Resistivity: {tex}\rho=R \frac{A}{l}{/tex} {tex}\rho=\frac{5.4 \times 10^{3}}{60}{/tex} {tex}\rho=0.09 \times 10^{3} \Omega {~cm}{/tex} or {tex}90 \Omega {~cm}{/tex} Conductivity: {tex}k=1 / \rho{/tex} = 1/ 90 {tex}k=0.011 \Omega^{-1} {~cm}^{-1}{/tex} or {tex}0.011 {~S} {~cm}^{-1}{/tex} Molar Conductivity {tex}\Lambda_{{m}}=\frac{k}{c} \times 1000{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.011}{0.05} \times 1000{/tex} {tex}=220 \Omega^{-1} \ {cm}^{2} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} or {tex}220 {~S} \ {cm}^{2} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}

  2. Oxidation of metal to undesirable products when exposed to air and moisture. / Metallic corrosion is loss of metals by a redox process in which metals are oxidized by air and moisture.

solution: Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. {tex}=110+100-105 {~S} \ {cm}^{2} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}=105 {~S} {~cm}^{2} {~mol}^{-1} {/tex} {tex} {E}_{\text {cell }}=E^o_{\text {cathode }}- E^{o} \text { anode }{/tex} {tex}=0.34-(-0.76 {~V}){/tex} {tex}=1.10 {~V}{/tex} {tex} \Delta_r G^{\underline o}=-n F E_{\text {cell }}^{\underline o} {/tex} {tex}=-2 \times 96500 \times 1.10{/tex} {tex}=-212,300 {~J} {~mol}^{-1} \text { or }{/tex} {tex}-212.3 {~kJ} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}

    [Molar mass of {tex}{Ag}=108 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}, 1 {~F}=96500 \ {C} \ {mol}^{-1}{/tex}]
question_text: What type of battery is the lead storage battery? Write the anode and the cathode reactions and the overall reaction occurring in a lead storage battery when current is drawn from it. Calculate the time to deposit 1.5 g of silver at cathode when a current of 1.5 A is passed through the solution of {tex}{AgNO}_{3}{/tex} . [Molar mass of {tex}{Ag}=108 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}, 1 {~F}=96500 \ {C} \ {mol}^{-1}{/tex} ]
explanation: Secondary cell /Battery Reaction at Cathode: {tex}{Ag}^{+}({aq})+{e}^{-} \longrightarrow {Ag}({~s}) {/tex} {tex} 108 {~g} \text { of } {Ag} \text { required } {/tex} = 96500 C {tex} 1.5 {~g} \text { of } {Ag} \text { required }=\frac{96500}{108} \times \frac{1.5}{1}=1340.27 {C} {/tex} {tex} \text { Time }=\frac{Q}{I}=\frac{1340.27}{1.5} {/tex} {tex} =893.51 {~s} \text { or } 14.85 {~min} . {/tex}
solution: Secondary cell /Battery Reaction at Cathode: {tex}{Ag}^{+}({aq})+{e}^{-} \longrightarrow {Ag}({~s}) {/tex} {tex} 108 {~g} \text { of } {Ag} \text { required } {/tex} = 96500 C {tex} 1.5 {~g} \text { of } {Ag} \text { required }=\frac{96500}{108} \times \frac{1.5}{1}=1340.27 {C} {/tex} {tex} \text { Time }=\frac{Q}{I}=\frac{1340.27}{1.5} {/tex} {tex} =893.51 {~s} \text { or } 14.85 {~min} . {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Secondary cell /Battery
  2. Reaction at Cathode:
    {tex}{Ag}^{+}({aq})+{e}^{-} \longrightarrow {Ag}({~s}) {/tex}
    {tex} 108 {~g} \text { of } {Ag} \text { required } {/tex}= 96500 C
    {tex} 1.5 {~g} \text { of } {Ag} \text { required }=\frac{96500}{108} \times \frac{1.5}{1}=1340.27 {C} {/tex}
    {tex} \text { Time }=\frac{Q}{I}=\frac{1340.27}{1.5} {/tex}
    {tex} =893.51 {~s} \text { or } 14.85 {~min} . {/tex}

question_html: Resistance of a conductivity cell filled with {tex}0.2 {~mol} \ {L}^{-1} {KCl}{/tex} solution is {tex}200 \Omega{/tex}. If the resistance of the same cell when filled with {tex}0.05 {~mol} \ {L}^{-1} {KCl}{/tex} solution is {tex}620 \Omega{/tex}, calculate the conductivity and molar conductivity of {tex}0.05 {~mol} \ {L}^{-1} {KCl}{/tex} solution. The conductivity of {tex}0.2 {~mol} \ {L}^{-1} {KCl}{/tex} solution is {tex}0.0248 {~S} \ {cm}^{-1}{/tex}.
question_text: Resistance of a conductivity cell filled with {tex}0.2 {~mol} \ {L}^{-1} {KCl}{/tex} solution is {tex}200 \Omega{/tex} . If the resistance of the same cell when filled with {tex}0.05 {~mol} \ {L}^{-1} {KCl}{/tex} solution is {tex}620 \Omega{/tex} , calculate the conductivity and molar conductivity of {tex}0.05 {~mol} \ {L}^{-1} {KCl}{/tex} solution. The conductivity of {tex}0.2 {~mol} \ {L}^{-1} {KCl}{/tex} solution is {tex}0.0248 {~S} \ {cm}^{-1}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\text {Cell constant}=G^*={conductivity \times resistance}{/tex} {tex}=0.0248 {~S} / {cm} \times 200 {ohm}=4.96 \ {cm}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\text { Conductivity of } 0.05 {~mol} \ {L}^{-1} {KCl} \text { solution }{/tex} {tex}=\text { cell constant } / \text { resistance }{/tex} {tex}=* G / R=4.96 / 620=0.008 {~S} {~cm}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\text { Molar conductivity }=\Lambda_{{m}}=\frac{k \times 1000}{c}{/tex} {tex}=0.008 \times 1000 / 0.05=160 {S} \ {cm}^2 {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\text {Cell constant}=G^*={conductivity \times resistance}{/tex} {tex}=0.0248 {~S} / {cm} \times 200 {ohm}=4.96 \ {cm}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\text { Conductivity of } 0.05 {~mol} \ {L}^{-1} {KCl} \text { solution }{/tex} {tex}=\text { cell constant } / \text { resistance }{/tex} {tex}=* G / R=4.96 / 620=0.008 {~S} {~cm}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\text { Molar conductivity }=\Lambda_{{m}}=\frac{k \times 1000}{c}{/tex} {tex}=0.008 \times 1000 / 0.05=160 {S} \ {cm}^2 {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\text {Cell constant}=G^*={conductivity \times resistance}{/tex} {tex}=0.0248 {~S} / {cm} \times 200 {ohm}=4.96 \ {cm}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Conductivity of } 0.05 {~mol} \ {L}^{-1} {KCl} \text { solution }{/tex} {tex}=\text { cell constant } / \text { resistance }{/tex}
{tex}=* G / R=4.96 / 620=0.008 {~S} {~cm}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Molar conductivity }=\Lambda_{{m}}=\frac{k \times 1000}{c}{/tex}
{tex}=0.008 \times 1000 / 0.05=160 {S} \ {cm}^2 {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Four half reactions I to IV are shown below:

  1. 2Cl- {tex}\to{/tex} Cl2 + 2e-
  2. 4OH- {tex}\to{/tex} O2 + 2H2O + 2e-
  3. Na+ + e- {tex}\to{/tex} Na
  4. 2H+ + 2e- {tex}\to{/tex} H2

Which two of these reactions are most likely to occur when concentrated brine is electrolysed?
question_text: Four half reactions I to IV are shown below: 2Cl - {tex}\to{/tex} Cl 2 + 2e - 4OH - {tex}\to{/tex} O 2 + 2H 2 O + 2e - Na + + e - {tex}\to{/tex} Na 2H + + 2e - {tex}\to{/tex} H 2 Which two of these reactions are most likely to occur when concentrated brine is electrolysed?

options.explanation: On electrolysis of brine solution at anode 2Cl - → Cl 2 + 2e - (oxidation) at cathode Na + + e - → Na

explanation: On electrolysis of brine solution at anode 2Cl - → Cl 2 + 2e - (oxidation) at cathode Na + + e - → Na

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. State Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions. Write an expression for the molar conductivity of acetic acid at infinite dilution according to Kohlrausch's law.
  2. Calculate the maximum work and log Kc for the given reaction at 298 K:
    Ni(s) + 2Ag+(aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Ni2+(aq) + 2Ag(s)
    Given: {tex}\mathrm E_{\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}/\mathrm{Ni}}^{\mathrm o}{/tex} = -0.25 V, {tex}\mathrm E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+}/\mathrm{Ag}}^{\mathrm o}{/tex} = 0.80 V
    1 F = 96500 C mol-1
question_text: State Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions. Write an expression for the molar conductivity of acetic acid at infinite dilution according to Kohlrausch's law. Calculate the maximum work and log K c for the given reaction at 298 K: Ni(s) + 2Ag + (aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Ni 2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s) Given: {tex}\mathrm E_{\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}/\mathrm{Ni}}^{\mathrm o}{/tex} = -0.25 V, {tex}\mathrm E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+}/\mathrm{Ag}}^{\mathrm o}{/tex} = 0.80 V 1 F = 96500 C mol -1
explanation: Kohlrausch law of independent migration of ion - Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} (CH 3 COOH) = {tex}\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}+\lambda^{\circ} {H}^{+}{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{nFE}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} Maximum work = {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{nFE}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C mol -1 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.80 + 0.25)V = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.05 J mol -1 = 202,650 J mol -1 or 202.65 kJ mol -1 log K c = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{nE}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}}{0.059}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 1.05}{0.059}{/tex} = 35.6
solution: Kohlrausch law of independent migration of ion - Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} (CH 3 COOH) = {tex}\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}+\lambda^{\circ} {H}^{+}{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{nFE}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} Maximum work = {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{nFE}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C mol -1 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.80 + 0.25)V = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.05 J mol -1 = 202,650 J mol -1 or 202.65 kJ mol -1 log K c = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{nE}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}}{0.059}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 1.05}{0.059}{/tex} = 35.6
solution_html:
1. Kohlrausch law of independent migration of ion - Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte.
    {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} (CH3COOH) = {tex}\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}+\lambda^{\circ} {H}^{+}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{nFE}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex}
    Maximum work = {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{nFE}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex}
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.80 + 0.25)V
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.05 J mol-1
    = 202,650 J mol-1 or 202.65 kJ mol-1
    log Kc = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{nE}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}}{0.059}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2 \times 1.05}{0.059}{/tex} = 35.6

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. State Faraday's first law of electrolysis. How much charge, in terms of Faraday, is required for the reduction of 1 mol Cu2+ to Cu?
  2. Calculate emf of the following cell at 298 K for
    Mg(s) | Mg2+ (0.1 M) || Cu2+ (0.01 M) | Cu(s)
    [{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = +2.71 V, 1 F = 96500 C mol-1, log 10 = 1]
question_text: State Faraday's first law of electrolysis. How much charge, in terms of Faraday, is required for the reduction of 1 mol Cu 2+ to Cu? Calculate emf of the following cell at 298 K for Mg(s) | Mg 2+ (0.1 M) || Cu 2+ (0.01 M) | Cu(s) [ {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = +2.71 V, 1 F = 96500 C mol -1 , log 10 = 1]
explanation: Faraday's first law of electrolysis - states that the mass of a substance deposited /liberated at the electrodes is directly proportional to the charge/quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte. 2F charge is required. {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} = 2.71 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{0 \cdot 1}{0.01}{/tex} = 2.71 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex} log 10 = 2.71 V - 0.0295 = 2.68 V
solution: Faraday's first law of electrolysis - states that the mass of a substance deposited /liberated at the electrodes is directly proportional to the charge/quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte. 2F charge is required. {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} = 2.71 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{0 \cdot 1}{0.01}{/tex} = 2.71 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex} log 10 = 2.71 V - 0.0295 = 2.68 V
solution_html:
1. Faraday's first law of electrolysis -  states that the mass of a substance deposited /liberated at the electrodes is directly proportional to the charge/quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.
    2F charge is required.
  2. {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex}
    = 2.71 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{0 \cdot 1}{0.01}{/tex}
    = 2.71 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex} log 10
    = 2.71 V - 0.0295
    = 2.68 V

question_text: Calculate {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} for the cell reaction at 25 C: Zn | Zn 2+ || Cd 2+ | Cd Given that: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V, {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cd}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cd}}^2{/tex} = 0.40 V 1 F = 96500 C mol -1

{tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\mathrm{o}}=-\mathrm{nF}^{\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}}{/tex}
= -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.16 V
= -223,880 J mol-1/-223.88 kJ mol-1

  2. Calculate the {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} and log Kc for the given reaction at 298 K:
    {tex}\mathrm{Ni}_{(\mathrm{s})}+2 \mathrm{Ag}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}{ }_{(\mathrm{aq})}+2 \mathrm{Ag}_{(\mathrm{s})}{/tex}
    Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{i}}}^{\circ}{ }^{2+} / \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{i}}=-0.25 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}=+0.80 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}
    1 F = 96500 C mol-1.
question_text: Conductivity of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M methanoic acid is 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 S cm -1 . Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation if {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^0{/tex} for methanoic acid is 404 S cm 2 mol -1 . Calculate the {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} and log K c for the given reaction at 298 K: {tex}\mathrm{Ni}_{(\mathrm{s})}+2 \mathrm{Ag}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}{ }_{(\mathrm{aq})}+2 \mathrm{Ag}_{(\mathrm{s})}{/tex} Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{i}}}^{\circ}{ }^{2+} / \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{i}}=-0.25 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}=+0.80 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} 1 F = 96500 C mol -1 .
explanation: Molar conductivity of methanoic acid {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{k}{c} \times{/tex} 1000 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}=\frac{8 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \times{/tex} 1000 S cm 2 mol -1 = 40 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha=\frac{\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}}{\wedge_{\mathrm{m}^{\circ}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{40}{404}{/tex} = 0.099 {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{nF} E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C mol -1 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.80 + 0.25) V = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.05 J mol -1 = -202,650 J mol -1 or -202.65 kJ mol -1 log K c {tex}=\frac{\mathrm{nE}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}}{0.059}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 1.05}{0.059}{/tex} = 35.6
solution: Molar conductivity of methanoic acid {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{k}{c} \times{/tex} 1000 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}=\frac{8 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \times{/tex} 1000 S cm 2 mol -1 = 40 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha=\frac{\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}}{\wedge_{\mathrm{m}^{\circ}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{40}{404}{/tex} = 0.099 {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{nF} E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C mol -1 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.80 + 0.25) V = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.05 J mol -1 = -202,650 J mol -1 or -202.65 kJ mol -1 log K c {tex}=\frac{\mathrm{nE}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}}{0.059}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 1.05}{0.059}{/tex} = 35.6
solution_html:
1. Molar conductivity of methanoic acid {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{k}{c} \times{/tex} 1000 S cm2 mol-1
    {tex}=\frac{8 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \times{/tex} 1000 S cm2 mol-1
    = 40 S cm2 mol-1
    {tex}\alpha=\frac{\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}}{\wedge_{\mathrm{m}^{\circ}}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{40}{404}{/tex}
    = 0.099
  2. {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{nF} E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex}
    = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.80 + 0.25) V
    = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.05 J mol-1
    = -202,650 J mol-1 or -202.65 kJ mol-1
    log Kc {tex}=\frac{\mathrm{nE}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}}{0.059}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2 \times 1.05}{0.059}{/tex} = 35.6
