subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Calculate the emf of the following cell at 25oC:
    Zn(s) | Zn2+ (0.1 M) || H+ (0.01 M) | H2 (g) (1 bar), Pt (s)
    [Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V, {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_2}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.00 V, log 10 = 1]
  2. State Kohlrausch law of independent migration of ions. Why does the conductivity of a solution decrease with dilution?
question_text: Calculate the emf of the following cell at 25 o C: Zn(s) | Zn 2+ (0.1 M) || H + (0.01 M) | H 2 (g) (1 bar), Pt (s) [Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V, {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_2}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.00 V, log 10 = 1] State Kohlrausch law of independent migration of ions. Why does the conductivity of a solution decrease with dilution?
explanation: E cell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^2}{/tex} = 0.76 V - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{[0 \cdot 1]}{[0 \cdot 01]^2}{/tex} = 0.76 V - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log 10 3 = 0.76 V - {tex}\frac{0.059 \times 3}{2}{/tex} E cell = 0.671 V Kohlrausch law of independent migration of ion - Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is equal to the sum of individual contributions of cation and anion of the electrolyte. Conductivity of the solution decrease with dilution because no. of ions per unit volume decreases.
solution: E cell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^2}{/tex} = 0.76 V - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{[0 \cdot 1]}{[0 \cdot 01]^2}{/tex} = 0.76 V - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log 10 3 = 0.76 V - {tex}\frac{0.059 \times 3}{2}{/tex} E cell = 0.671 V Kohlrausch law of independent migration of ion - Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is equal to the sum of individual contributions of cation and anion of the electrolyte. Conductivity of the solution decrease with dilution because no. of ions per unit volume decreases.
solution_html:
1. Ecell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^2}{/tex}
    = 0.76 V - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{[0 \cdot 1]}{[0 \cdot 01]^2}{/tex}
    = 0.76 V - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log 103
    = 0.76 V - {tex}\frac{0.059 \times 3}{2}{/tex}
    Ecell = 0.671 V
  2. Kohlrausch law of independent migration of ion - Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is equal to the sum of individual contributions of cation and anion of the electrolyte.
    Conductivity of the solution decrease with dilution because no. of ions per unit volume decreases.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Calculate the emf of the following cell at 298 K:
    Al (s) | Al3+ (0.001 M) || Ni2+ (0.1 M) | Ni (s)
    [Given" {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Al}^{3+} / \mathrm{Al}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -1.66 V, {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ni}^{2+} / \mathrm{Ni}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.25 V, log 10 = 1]
  2. With the help of a graph explain why it is not possible to determine {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} for a weak electrolyte by extrapolating the molar conductivity {tex}\left(\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\right){/tex} versus {tex}\mathrm{C}^{1 / 2}{/tex} curve as for strong electrolyte.
question_text: Calculate the emf of the following cell at 298 K: Al (s) | Al 3+ (0.001 M) || Ni 2+ (0.1 M) | Ni (s) [Given" {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Al}^{3+} / \mathrm{Al}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -1.66 V, {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ni}^{2+} / \mathrm{Ni}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.25 V, log 10 = 1] With the help of a graph explain why it is not possible to determine {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} for a weak electrolyte by extrapolating the molar conductivity {tex}\left(\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\right){/tex} versus {tex}\mathrm{C}^{1 / 2}{/tex} curve as for strong electrolyte.
explanation: Emf of the cell = E cell = {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right]^3}{/tex} E cell = [-0.25 + 1.66] - {tex}\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{[0.001]^2}{[0 \cdot 1]^3}{/tex} = 1.41 - {tex}\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} log 10 -6+3 = 1.41 + {tex}\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3 E cell = 1.41 + 0.0295 E cell = 1.4395 V As seen from the curve, it runs parallel to the y-axis. So, even on extrapolation, it will not intercept, hence {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} cannot be obtained.
solution: Emf of the cell = E cell = {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right]^3}{/tex} E cell = [-0.25 + 1.66] - {tex}\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{[0.001]^2}{[0 \cdot 1]^3}{/tex} = 1.41 - {tex}\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} log 10 -6+3 = 1.41 + {tex}\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3 E cell = 1.41 + 0.0295 E cell = 1.4395 V As seen from the curve, it runs parallel to the y-axis. So, even on extrapolation, it will not intercept, hence {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} cannot be obtained.
solution_html:
1. Emf of the cell = Ecell = {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right]^3}{/tex}
    Ecell = [-0.25 + 1.66] - {tex}\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{[0.001]^2}{[0 \cdot 1]^3}{/tex}
    = 1.41 - {tex}\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} log 10-6+3
    = 1.41 + {tex}\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3
    Ecell = 1.41 + 0.0295
    Ecell = 1.4395 V

  2. As seen from the curve, it runs parallel to the y-axis. So, even on extrapolation, it will not intercept, hence {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} cannot be obtained.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. The molar conductivities of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}{/tex} and Cl- ion are 73.8 S cm2 mol-1 and 76.2 S cm2 mol-1 respectively. The conductivity of 0.1 M NH4Cl is 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 S cm-1. Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation.
  2. Calculate the half-cell potential at 298 K for the reaction
    Zn2+ + 2e- {tex}\to{/tex} Zn
    if [Zn2+] = 0.1 M and {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V.
question_text: The molar conductivities of {tex}\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}{/tex} and Cl - ion are 73.8 S cm 2 mol -1 and 76.2 S cm 2 mol -1 respectively. The conductivity of 0.1 M NH 4 Cl is 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 S cm -1 . Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation. Calculate the half-cell potential at 298 K for the reaction Zn 2+ + 2e - {tex}\to{/tex} Zn if [Zn 2+ ] = 0.1 M and {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V.
explanation: {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{ }^{\circ}{/tex} (NH 4 Cl) = 73.8 + 76.2 = 150.0 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{c}} \times{/tex} 1000 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{1.29 \times 10^{-2}}{0.1} \times{/tex} 1000 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 = 129 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^{\circ}}{/tex} degree of dissociation {tex}\alpha=\frac{129}{150}{/tex} = 0.86 {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} \mathrm{/}_{\mathrm{Zn}}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{1}{0.1}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V - 0.0295 = -0.7895 V
solution: {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{ }^{\circ}{/tex} (NH 4 Cl) = 73.8 + 76.2 = 150.0 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{c}} \times{/tex} 1000 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{1.29 \times 10^{-2}}{0.1} \times{/tex} 1000 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 = 129 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^{\circ}}{/tex} degree of dissociation {tex}\alpha=\frac{129}{150}{/tex} = 0.86 {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} \mathrm{/}_{\mathrm{Zn}}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{1}{0.1}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V - 0.0295 = -0.7895 V
solution_html:
1. {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{ }^{\circ}{/tex} (NH4Cl) = 73.8 + 76.2 = 150.0 S cm2 mol-1
    {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{c}} \times{/tex} 1000 S cm2 mol-1
    {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{1.29 \times 10^{-2}}{0.1} \times{/tex} 1000 S cm2 mol-1
    {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 102 = 129 S cm2 mol-1
    {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^{\circ}}{/tex}                                     degree of dissociation 
    {tex}\alpha=\frac{129}{150}{/tex} = 0.86
  2. {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} \mathrm{/}_{\mathrm{Zn}}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{1}{0.1}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V - 0.0295
    = -0.7895 V

question_html:
Calculate the emf of the following cell at 298 K:
Fe(s) | Fe2+ (0.01 M) || H+(1 M) | H2(g) (1 bar), Pt(s)
Given {tex}\mathrm{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {Cell }}{/tex} = 0.44 V.
question_text: Calculate the emf of the following cell at 298 K: Fe (s) | Fe 2+ (0.01 M) || H + (1 M) | H 2(g) (1 bar), Pt (s) Given {tex}\mathrm{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {Cell }}{/tex} = 0.44 V.
explanation: E cell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[F e^{2+}(a q)\right]}{\left[H^{+}(a q .)\right]^2}{/tex} = 0.44 - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{(0.01)}{(1)^2}{/tex} = 0.44 - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log 10 -2 = 0.499 V
solution: E cell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[F e^{2+}(a q)\right]}{\left[H^{+}(a q .)\right]^2}{/tex} = 0.44 - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{(0.01)}{(1)^2}{/tex} = 0.44 - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log 10 -2 = 0.499 V
solution_html:
Ecell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[F e^{2+}(a q)\right]}{\left[H^{+}(a q .)\right]^2}{/tex}
= 0.44 - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{(0.01)}{(1)^2}{/tex}
= 0.44 - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} log 10-2
= 0.499 V

explanation: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {Cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = +ve & {tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} = -ve It states that the mass of a substance deposited/liberated at the electrodes is directly proportional to the charge/quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.

question_html:
Rahul set-up an experiment to find resistance of aqueous KCl solution for different concentrations at 298 K using a conductivity cell connected to a Wheatstone bridge. He fed the Wheatstone bridge with a.c. power in the audio frequency range 550 to 5000 cycles per second. Once the resistance was calculated from null point he also calculated the conductivity K and molar conductivity {tex}\wedge_m{/tex} and recorded his readings in tabular form.

S.No. Conc.(M) k S cm-1 {tex}\wedge_m{/tex} S cm2 mol-1
1. 1.00 111.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 111.3
2. 0.10 12.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 129.0
3. 0.01 1.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 141.0

Why does conductivity decrease with dilution?
question_text: Rahul set-up an experiment to find resistance of aqueous KCl solution for different concentrations at 298 K using a conductivity cell connected to a Wheatstone bridge. He fed the Wheatstone bridge with a.c. power in the audio frequency range 550 to 5000 cycles per second. Once the resistance was calculated from null point he also calculated the conductivity K and molar conductivity {tex}\wedge_m{/tex} and recorded his readings in tabular form. S.No. Conc.(M) k S cm -1 {tex}\wedge_m{/tex} S cm 2 mol -1 1. 1.00 111.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 111.3 2. 0.10 12.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 129.0 3. 0.01 1.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 141.0 Why does conductivity decrease with dilution?
explanation: The conductivity of a solution is defined as the conductance of ions in a unit volume of the solution. When a solution is diluted, the number of ions (responsible for carrying current) drops. As a result, solution conductivity diminishes with dilution.
solution: The conductivity of a solution is defined as the conductance of ions in a unit volume of the solution. When a solution is diluted, the number of ions (responsible for carrying current) drops. As a result, solution conductivity diminishes with dilution.
solution_html: The conductivity of a solution is defined as the conductance of ions in a unit volume of the solution. When a solution is diluted, the number of ions (responsible for carrying current) drops. As a result, solution conductivity diminishes with dilution.

question_text: Rahul set-up an experiment to find resistance of aqueous KCl solution for different concentrations at 298 K using a conductivity cell connected to a Wheatstone bridge. explanation: {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^0{/tex} (KCl) = 150.0 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} (0.01 M KCl) = 141 S cm 2 /mol As we know {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^0}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{141}{150}{/tex} = 0.94
solution: {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^0{/tex} (KCl) = 150.0 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} (0.01 M KCl) = 141 S cm 2 /mol As we know {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^0}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{141}{150}{/tex} = 0.94

{tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^0{/tex} (KCl) = 150.0 S cm2 mol-1
{tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} (0.01 M KCl) = 141 S cm2/mol
As we know
{tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^0}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{141}{150}{/tex}
= 0.94

question_html:
1. State Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions.
  2. How much electricity in terms of Faraday is required to produce 40 g of Al from molten Al2O3?
    (Given : atomic mass of Al = 27 u)
  3. Calculate log Kc for the following reaction at 298 K:
    Zn (s) + Cu2+ (aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Zn2+ (aq) + Cu (s)
    Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0·76 V, {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0·34 V
question_text: State Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions. How much electricity in terms of Faraday is required to produce 40 g of Al from molten Al 2 O 3 ? (Given : atomic mass of Al = 27 u) Calculate log K c for the following reaction at 298 K: Zn (s) + Cu 2+ (aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + Cu (s) Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0·76 V, {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0·34 V
explanation: Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. AI 3+ + 3e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} & \text { Al } \\ & 27 \mathrm{~g} \end{aligned}{/tex} Electricity required to produce 27 g of Al = 3 F Therefore, electricity required to produce 40 g of Al = {tex}\frac{3 \times 40}{27} \mathrm{~F}{/tex} = 4.44 F log Kc = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{nE}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}}{0.059}{/tex} E° cathode= 0.34V E° anode= -0.76V {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = E°cathode - E°anode = 0.34 - (-0.76) V = 1.10 V n = 2 log Kc = {tex}\frac{2 \times 1.10}{0.059}{/tex} = 37.22
solution: Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. AI 3+ + 3e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} & \text { Al } \\ & 27 \mathrm{~g} \end{aligned}{/tex} Electricity required to produce 27 g of Al = 3 F Therefore, electricity required to produce 40 g of Al = {tex}\frac{3 \times 40}{27} \mathrm{~F}{/tex} = 4.44 F log Kc = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{nE}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}}{0.059}{/tex} E° cathode= 0.34V E° anode= -0.76V {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = E°cathode - E°anode = 0.34 - (-0.76) V = 1.10 V n = 2 log Kc = {tex}\frac{2 \times 1.10}{0.059}{/tex} = 37.22
solution_html:
1. Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte.
  2. AI3+ + 3e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} & \text { Al } \\ & 27 \mathrm{~g} \end{aligned}{/tex}   Electricity required to produce 27 g of Al = 3 F
  3. Therefore, electricity required to produce 40 g of Al = {tex}\frac{3 \times 40}{27} \mathrm{~F}{/tex} = 4.44 F
  4. log Kc = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{nE}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}}{0.059}{/tex}E° cathode= 0.34V E° anode= -0.76V
    {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = E°cathode - E°anode = 0.34 - (-0.76) V
    = 1.10 V
    n = 2
    log Kc = {tex}\frac{2 \times 1.10}{0.059}{/tex}
    = 37.22

question_text: A cell and its emf is given below: Pt (s) | H 2 (g, 1 bar) | H + (aq, 1 M) || Cu 2+ (aq, 1M) | Cu (s) emf of the cell = + 0.34 V. Write the reduction half-reaction at cathode. How is standard Gibbs energy for a reaction related to equilibrium constant? Calculate emf of the given cell: Mg (s) | Mg 2+ (0·1 M) || Cu 2+ (1·0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M) | Cu (s) Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0·34 V, {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Mg}^{2+} / \mathrm{Mg}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -2.37 V (log 100 = 2)
explanation: cell reaction - {tex} \left.P_t(s)\left|H_2(g,(b a l)) H^{+}\left(a q_1 \mid M\right)\right| \mid {cu}^{2+} / a q_2 / M\right) \mid {Cu}{/tex} Reduction half reaction at cathode - {tex} \underbrace{{Cu}^{2+}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow {Cu}}_{\text {Reduction }}{/tex} Fol a cell - {tex} m\left|m^{m+1}\right|\left(N^{m+} \mid N\right.{/tex} Relation between Gibbs free energy and emp of cell - {tex} \Delta_r G=-n F E_{(c u l)}^{\circ}{/tex} But, at equilibrium - {tex} \epsilon_{\text {cell }}^0=\frac{R T}{n+} \ln \frac{\left[N^{n+}\right]}{\left[N^{n+}\right]} {/tex} {tex} \left.\Delta_R h=-n F+\frac{R T}{n+} \ln \frac{\left[M^{n+}\right]}{\left[N^{n+}\right]}=K_C\right) {/tex} {tex} \left.\Delta_R L^{\circ}=R T \ln \right] {/tex} {tex}\checkmark{/tex} Relation b/w standard Gibbs energy and equiliseinm constant {tex}{Cell} \rightarrow{/tex} {tex} {Mg}({~s}) \mid {Mg}^{2+}(0.1 {M}) \| {cu}^{2 t}\left(1 \cdot 10^{-3} {M}\right) {Cu}({~s}{/tex} {tex} \text { Cell equation - } {/tex} {tex} {Mg}+{Cu}^{2+} \longrightarrow {Mg}^{2+}+{Cu}({n}=2) {/tex} {tex} E_{{Cu}^2 / {cu}}^0=0.34 {~V} E_{{Mg}}^{21} / {Mg}=-237 {~V} {/tex} {tex} E_{\text {cell }}^c= E_{\text {sd }}^c-E_{\text {bx }}^{-} {/tex} {tex} 0.34-(-2.37) {/tex} {tex} 0.34+2.37 {/tex} {tex} 2.71 {~V} {/tex} According to Nernst equation- {tex} \begin{aligned} & E_{\text {cell }}=E_{\text {cell }}^0-\frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{\left[{mg}_{{g}}^{2+]}\right.}{\left\{{Cu}^{2+1}\right.} \\ & E_{\text {cell }}=2.71-\frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{[0.1]}{\left[1 \times 10^{-3}\right]} \\ & E_{\text {cell }}=2.71-\frac{0.059}{2} \log 10^2 \\ & E_{\text {cell }}=2.71-\frac{0.059}{2} \times 3 \\ & E_{\text {cell }}=2.651 {~V} \end{aligned}{/tex}

    {tex} \left.P_t(s)\left|H_2(g,(b a l)) H^{+}\left(a q_1 \mid M\right)\right| \mid {cu}^{2+} / a q_2 / M\right) \mid {Cu}{/tex}

question_text: Rahul set-up an experiment to find resistance of aqueous KCl solution for different concentrations at 298 K using a conductivity cell connected to a Wheatstone bridge. He fed the Wheatstone bridge with a.c. power in the audio frequency range 550 to 5000 cycles per second. Once the resistance was calculated from null point he also calculated the conductivity K and molar conductivity {tex}\wedge_m{/tex} and recorded his readings in tabular form. S.No. Conc.(M) k S cm -1 {tex}\wedge_m{/tex} S cm 2 mol -1 1. 1.00 111.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 111.3 2. 0.10 12.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 129.0 3. 0.01 1.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 141.0 If Rahul had used HCl instead to KCl then would you expect the {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} values to be more or less than those per KCl for a given concentration. Justify.
explanation: If Rahul had used HCl instead of KCl, the {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} value for a given concentration would have been greater. The explanation for the increase in molar conductivity of H + over K + is that H + has the maximum molar conductance in an aqueous state because the proton is transported from one water molecule to another in the hydrogen-linked chain of water molecules.
solution: If Rahul had used HCl instead of KCl, the {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} value for a given concentration would have been greater. The explanation for the increase in molar conductivity of H + over K + is that H + has the maximum molar conductance in an aqueous state because the proton is transported from one water molecule to another in the hydrogen-linked chain of water molecules.

question_html: Define Molar conductivity ({tex}\Lambda_m{/tex}).
question_text: Define Molar conductivity ( {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} ).
explanation: Molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is the conductance of the volume V of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with area of cross section A and distance of unit length. Therefore, {tex}\Lambda_m\;=\;\frac{\kappa A}l=\kappa V{/tex} Its unit is given below: {tex}\Lambda_m(\;S\;cm^2\;mol^{-1})=\frac{\kappa\;(\;S\;cm^{-1})}{1000\;L\;m^{-3}\;\times\;molariity\;(mol\;L^{-1})}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_m(\;S\;cm^2\;mol^{-1})=\frac{\kappa\;(\;S\;cm^{-1})\;\times\;1000\;(cm^3/L)}{\;molariity\;(mol\;L^{-1})} {/tex}
solution: Molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is the conductance of the volume V of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with area of cross section A and distance of unit length. Therefore, {tex}\Lambda_m\;=\;\frac{\kappa A}l=\kappa V{/tex} Its unit is given below: {tex}\Lambda_m(\;S\;cm^2\;mol^{-1})=\frac{\kappa\;(\;S\;cm^{-1})}{1000\;L\;m^{-3}\;\times\;molariity\;(mol\;L^{-1})}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_m(\;S\;cm^2\;mol^{-1})=\frac{\kappa\;(\;S\;cm^{-1})\;\times\;1000\;(cm^3/L)}{\;molariity\;(mol\;L^{-1})} {/tex}
solution_html:
Molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is the conductance of the volume V of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with area of cross section A and distance of unit length. Therefore,
{tex}\Lambda_m\;=\;\frac{\kappa A}l=\kappa V{/tex}
Its unit is given below:
{tex}\Lambda_m(\;S\;cm^2\;mol^{-1})=\frac{\kappa\;(\;S\;cm^{-1})}{1000\;L\;m^{-3}\;\times\;molariity\;(mol\;L^{-1})}{/tex}

{tex}\Lambda_m(\;S\;cm^2\;mol^{-1})=\frac{\kappa\;(\;S\;cm^{-1})\;\times\;1000\;(cm^3/L)}{\;molariity\;(mol\;L^{-1})} {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Oxidation-reduction reactions are commonly known as redox reactions. They involve transfer of electrons from one species to another. In a spontaneous reaction, energy is released which can be used to do useful work. The reaction is split into two half-reactions. Two different containers are used and a wire is used to drive the electrons from one side to the other and a Voltaic/Galvanic cell is created. It is an electrochemical cell that uses spontaneous redox reactions to generate electricity. A salt bridge also connects to the half-cells. The reading of the voltmeter gives the cell voltage or cell potential or electromotive force. If Ecell0 is positive the reaction is spontaneous and if it is negative the reaction is non-spontaneous and is referred to as electrolytic cell. Electrolysis refers to the decomposition of a substance by an electric current. One mole of electric charge when passed through a cell will discharge half a mole of a divalent metal ion such as Cu2+. This was first formulated by Faraday in the form of laws of electrolysis.
The conductance of material is the property of materials due to which a material allows the flow of ions through itself and thus conducts electricity. Conductivity is represented by k and it depends upon nature and concentration of electrolyte, temperature, etc. A more common term molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is conductance of the volume of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with the unit area of cross-section and distance of unit length. Limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolytes cannot be obtained graphically.

What will happen if the salt bridge is removed?
question_text: Oxidation-reduction reactions are commonly known as redox reactions. They involve transfer of electrons from one species to another. Limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolytes cannot be obtained graphically. What will happen if the salt bridge is removed?
explanation: Salt bridge permits the flow of current by completing the circuit as well as it maintains the charge balance between anode and cathode by movement of electrons. If the salt bridge is removed no current will flow in the circuit and the voltage will drop to zero.
solution: Salt bridge permits the flow of current by completing the circuit as well as it maintains the charge balance between anode and cathode by movement of electrons. If the salt bridge is removed no current will flow in the circuit and the voltage will drop to zero.

When does electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell?
question_text: Oxidation-reduction reactions are commonly known as redox reactions. They involve transfer of electrons from one species to another. In a spontaneous reaction, energy is released which can be used to do useful work. The reaction is split into two half-reactions. Two different containers are used and a wire is used to drive the electrons from one side to the other and a Voltaic/Galvanic cell is created. It is an electrochemical cell that uses spontaneous redox reactions to generate electricity. A salt bridge also connects to the half-cells. The reading of the voltmeter gives the cell voltage or cell potential or electromotive force. If E cell 0 is positive the reaction is spontaneous and if it is negative the reaction is non-spontaneous and is referred to as electrolytic cell. Electrolysis refers to the decomposition of a substance by an electric current. One mole of electric charge when passed through a cell will discharge half a mole of a divalent metal ion such as Cu 2+ . This was first formulated by Faraday in the form of laws of electrolysis. The conductance of material is the property of materials due to which a material allows the flow of ions through itself and thus conducts electricity. Conductivity is represented by k and it depends upon nature and concentration of electrolyte, temperature, etc. A more common term molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is conductance of the volume of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with the unit area of cross-section and distance of unit length. Limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolytes cannot be obtained graphically. When does electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell?
explanation: An electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell when there is an application of an external opposite potential on the galvanic cell and reaction is not inhibited until the opposing voltage reaches the value 1.1 V. At this stage, no current flows through the cell and on increasing the external potential any further the reaction will function in the opposite direction. E ext > E cell
solution: An electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell when there is an application of an external opposite potential on the galvanic cell and reaction is not inhibited until the opposing voltage reaches the value 1.1 V. At this stage, no current flows through the cell and on increasing the external potential any further the reaction will function in the opposite direction. E ext > E cell
solution_html:
An electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell when there is an application of an external opposite potential on the galvanic cell and reaction is not inhibited until the opposing voltage reaches the value 1.1 V. At this stage, no current flows through the cell and on increasing the external potential any further the reaction will function in the opposite direction.
Eext > Ecell

  1. What will happen to the concentration of Zn2+ and Ag+ when Ecell = 0.
  2. Why does conductivity of a solution decreases with dilution?
question_text: Oxidation-reduction reactions are commonly known as redox reactions. They involve transfer of electrons from one species to another. In a spontaneous reaction, energy is released which can be used to do useful work. The reaction is split into two half-reactions. Two different containers are used and a wire is used to drive the electrons from one side to the other and a Voltaic/Galvanic cell is created. It is an electrochemical cell that uses spontaneous redox reactions to generate electricity. A salt bridge also connects to the half-cells. The reading of the voltmeter gives the cell voltage or cell potential or electromotive force. If E cell 0 is positive the reaction is spontaneous and if it is negative the reaction is non-spontaneous and is referred to as electrolytic cell. Electrolysis refers to the decomposition of a substance by an electric current. One mole of electric charge when passed through a cell will discharge half a mole of a divalent metal ion such as Cu 2+ . This was first formulated by Faraday in the form of laws of electrolysis. The conductance of material is the property of materials due to which a material allows the flow of ions through itself and thus conducts electricity. Conductivity is represented by k and it depends upon nature and concentration of electrolyte, temperature, etc. A more common term molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is conductance of the volume of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with the unit area of cross-section and distance of unit length. Limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolytes cannot be obtained graphically. What will happen to the concentration of Zn 2+ and Ag + when E cell = 0. Why does conductivity of a solution decreases with dilution?
explanation: When E cell = 0, an equilibrium condition is reached and the concentration of Zn 2+ and Ag + remains same. Conductivity of a solution defined as the conductance of ions present in a unit volume of the solution. On dilution, the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Thus, the conductivity of the solution decreases on dilution.
solution: When E cell = 0, an equilibrium condition is reached and the concentration of Zn 2+ and Ag + remains same. Conductivity of a solution defined as the conductance of ions present in a unit volume of the solution. On dilution, the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Thus, the conductivity of the solution decreases on dilution.
solution_html:
1. When Ecell = 0, an equilibrium condition is reached and the concentration of Zn2+ and Ag+ remains same.
  2. Conductivity of a solution defined as the conductance of ions present in a unit volume of the solution. On dilution, the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Thus, the conductivity of the solution decreases on dilution.

The molar conductivity of a 1.5 M solution of an electrolyte is found to be 138.9 Scm2mol-1. Calculate the conductivity of this solution.
question_text: Oxidation-reduction reactions are commonly known as redox reactions. They involve transfer of electrons from one species to another. In a spontaneous reaction, energy is released which can be used to do useful work. The reaction is split into two half-reactions. Two different containers are used and a wire is used to drive the electrons from one side to the other and a Voltaic/Galvanic cell is created. It is an electrochemical cell that uses spontaneous redox reactions to generate electricity. A salt bridge also connects to the half-cells. The reading of the voltmeter gives the cell voltage or cell potential or electromotive force. If E cell 0 is positive the reaction is spontaneous and if it is negative the reaction is non-spontaneous and is referred to as electrolytic cell. Electrolysis refers to the decomposition of a substance by an electric current. One mole of electric charge when passed through a cell will discharge half a mole of a divalent metal ion such as Cu 2+ . This was first formulated by Faraday in the form of laws of electrolysis. The conductance of material is the property of materials due to which a material allows the flow of ions through itself and thus conducts electricity. Conductivity is represented by k and it depends upon nature and concentration of electrolyte, temperature, etc. A more common term molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is conductance of the volume of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with the unit area of cross-section and distance of unit length. Limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolytes cannot be obtained graphically. The molar conductivity of a 1.5 M solution of an electrolyte is found to be 138.9 Scm 2 mol -1 . Calculate the conductivity of this solution.
explanation: Molar Conductivity, k = {tex}\frac{\text { Conductivity }}{\text { Concentration }}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_m=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{\mathrm{c}}{/tex} Conductivity {tex}=\Lambda_m \times \mathrm{C}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{138.9\left(\mathrm{Scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right) \times 1.5(\mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L})}{1000\left(\mathrm{CM}^3 / \mathrm{L}\right)}{/tex} = 0.208 Scm -1
solution: Molar Conductivity, k = {tex}\frac{\text { Conductivity }}{\text { Concentration }}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_m=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{\mathrm{c}}{/tex} Conductivity {tex}=\Lambda_m \times \mathrm{C}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{138.9\left(\mathrm{Scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right) \times 1.5(\mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L})}{1000\left(\mathrm{CM}^3 / \mathrm{L}\right)}{/tex} = 0.208 Scm -1
solution_html:
Molar Conductivity, k = {tex}\frac{\text { Conductivity }}{\text { Concentration }}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_m=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{\mathrm{c}}{/tex}
Conductivity {tex}=\Lambda_m \times \mathrm{C}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{138.9\left(\mathrm{Scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right) \times 1.5(\mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L})}{1000\left(\mathrm{CM}^3 / \mathrm{L}\right)}{/tex}
= 0.208 Scm-1

question_html: The conductivity of 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M methanoic acid is 5.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 S cm-1 and its {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^0{/tex} has a value 400 S cm2 mol-1. Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation.
question_text: The conductivity of 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 M methanoic acid is 5.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 S cm -1 and its {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^0{/tex} has a value 400 S cm 2 mol -1 . Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation.
explanation: {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1000 \times \mathrm{K}}{\mathrm{M}}{/tex} S cm 2 mol -1 {tex} \Lambda_m{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1000 \times 5.25 \times 10^{-5}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} - S cm 2 mol -1 = 210 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex} \wedge_m^0 \mathrm{HCOOH}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{HCOO}^{-}+\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{H}^{+}{/tex} = (50.5 + 349.5) S cm 2 mol -1 = 400 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\Lambda_m }{\Lambda_m^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{210}{400}{/tex} = 0.525
solution: {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1000 \times \mathrm{K}}{\mathrm{M}}{/tex} S cm 2 mol -1 {tex} \Lambda_m{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1000 \times 5.25 \times 10^{-5}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} - S cm 2 mol -1 = 210 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex} \wedge_m^0 \mathrm{HCOOH}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{HCOO}^{-}+\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{H}^{+}{/tex} = (50.5 + 349.5) S cm 2 mol -1 = 400 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\Lambda_m }{\Lambda_m^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{210}{400}{/tex} = 0.525
solution_html:
{tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1000 \times \mathrm{K}}{\mathrm{M}}{/tex} S cm2 mol-1
{tex} \Lambda_m{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1000 \times 5.25 \times 10^{-5}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} - S cm2 mol-1
= 210 S cm2mol-1
{tex} \wedge_m^0 \mathrm{HCOOH}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{HCOO}^{-}+\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{H}^{+}{/tex}
= (50.5 + 349.5) S cm2mol-1
= 400 S cm2mol-1
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\Lambda_m }{\Lambda_m^{\circ}}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{210}{400}{/tex}
= 0.525

Write the Nernst equation for the following cell reaction:
Zn(s) + Cu2+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ (aq) + Cu(s)
How will the Ecell be affected when concentration of

  1. Cu2+ ions is increased and
  2. Zn2+ ions is increased?
question_text: Write the Nernst equation for the following cell reaction: Zn(s) + Cu 2+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + Cu(s) How will the E cell be affected when concentration of Cu 2+ ions is increased and Zn 2+ ions is increased?
explanation: Zn + Cu 2+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ + Cu E cell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} E cell increases when concentration of Cu 2+ ions increases. E cell decreases when concentration of Zn 2+ ions increases.
solution: Zn + Cu 2+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ + Cu E cell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} E cell increases when concentration of Cu 2+ ions increases. E cell decreases when concentration of Zn 2+ ions increases.
solution_html:
Zn + Cu2+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ + Cu
Ecell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex}- {tex}\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex}

  1. Ecell increases when concentration of Cu2+ ions increases.
  2. Ecell decreases when concentration of Zn2+ ions increases.

    Calculate {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} for the reaction. (Given: 1F = 96500 mol-1)
  2. Calculate {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^o{/tex} for MgCl2 if {tex}\lambda^o{/tex} values for Mg2+ ion and Cl- ion are 106 S cm2mol-1 and 76.3 S cm2mol-1 respectively.
question_text: The standard Gibbs energy {tex}\left(\Delta \mathrm{rG}^{\circ}\right){/tex} for the following cell reaction is -300 kJ mol -1 : Zn(s) + 2Ag + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s) Calculate {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} for the reaction. explanation: Zn(s) + 2Ag + (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s) The number of moles of electrons transferred (n) is 2, as two moles of electrons are involved in the reduction of two moles of {tex}\mathrm{Ag}^{\wedge}{/tex} + ions. subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. State Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions.
  2. Calculate the degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) of CH3COOH if {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} and {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^o{/tex} of CH3COOH are 48 S cm2 mol-1 and 400 S cm2 mol-1 respectively.
question_text: State Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions. Calculate the degree of dissociation ( {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) of CH 3 COOH if {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} and {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^o{/tex} of CH 3 COOH are 48 S cm 2 mol -1 and 400 S cm 2 mol -1 respectively.
explanation: Limiting molar conductivity is equal to the sum of individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\wedge_m}{\wedge_{m^{\circ}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{48}{400}{/tex} = 0.12
solution: Limiting molar conductivity is equal to the sum of individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\wedge_m}{\wedge_{m^{\circ}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{48}{400}{/tex} = 0.12
solution_html:
1. Limiting molar conductivity is equal to the sum of individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte.
  2. {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\wedge_m}{\wedge_{m^{\circ}}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{48}{400}{/tex} = 0.12

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the emf of the following cell:
Zn (s) | Zn2+ (0·01 M) || (0·001 M) Ag+ | Ag (s)
Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -0·76 V and
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = +0·80 V
[log 2 = 0·3010, log 3 = 0·4771, log 10 = 1]
question_text: Calculate the emf of the following cell: Zn (s) | Zn 2+ (0·01 M) || (0·001 M) Ag+ | Ag (s) Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -0·76 V and {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = +0·80 V [log 2 = 0·3010, log 3 = 0·4771, log 10 = 1]
explanation: The balanced cell reaction is: Zn(s) + 2Ag + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ + 2Ag(s) E o for the cell is 0.80 V (given for {tex}\frac{Ag^+}{ Ag}{/tex} ) and -0.76 V (given for {tex}\frac{Zn^{2+}}{Zn}{/tex} ). n the number of moles of electrons transferred, which is 2 in this case. [A] is the concentration of Zn 2+ , which is 0.01 M. [B] is the concentration of Ag + , which is 0.001 M. E = {tex}E^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log {/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{[A]^m}{[B]^n}\right){/tex} Now, substitute these values into the Nernst equation: E = 0.80 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\left(\frac{0.01}{0.001}\right){/tex} Calculate the logarithmic term: log {tex}\left(\frac{0.01}{0.001}\right){/tex} = log(10) = 1 Now, substitute this value back into the equation: E = 0.80 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2} \cdot{/tex} 1 = 0.80 V - 0.02955 V = 0.77045 V So, the emf of the given cell is approximately 0.770 V.
solution: The balanced cell reaction is: Zn(s) + 2Ag + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ + 2Ag(s) E o for the cell is 0.80 V (given for {tex}\frac{Ag^+}{ Ag}{/tex} ) and -0.76 V (given for {tex}\frac{Zn^{2+}}{Zn}{/tex} ). n the number of moles of electrons transferred, which is 2 in this case. [A] is the concentration of Zn 2+ , which is 0.01 M. [B] is the concentration of Ag + , which is 0.001 M. E = {tex}E^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log {/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{[A]^m}{[B]^n}\right){/tex} Now, substitute these values into the Nernst equation: E = 0.80 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\left(\frac{0.01}{0.001}\right){/tex} Calculate the logarithmic term: log {tex}\left(\frac{0.01}{0.001}\right){/tex} = log(10) = 1 Now, substitute this value back into the equation: E = 0.80 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2} \cdot{/tex} 1 = 0.80 V - 0.02955 V = 0.77045 V So, the emf of the given cell is approximately 0.770 V.
solution_html:
The balanced cell reaction is:
Zn(s) + 2Ag+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ + 2Ag(s)
Eo for the cell is 0.80 V (given for {tex}\frac{Ag^+}{ Ag}{/tex}) and -0.76 V (given for {tex}\frac{Zn^{2+}}{Zn}{/tex}).
n the number of moles of electrons transferred, which is 2 in this case.
[A] is the concentration of Zn2+, which is 0.01 M.
[B] is the concentration of Ag+, which is 0.001 M.
E = {tex}E^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log {/tex}{tex}\left(\frac{[A]^m}{[B]^n}\right){/tex}
Now, substitute these values into the Nernst equation:
E = 0.80 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex}log {tex}\left(\frac{0.01}{0.001}\right){/tex}
Calculate the logarithmic term:
log {tex}\left(\frac{0.01}{0.001}\right){/tex} = log(10) = 1
Now, substitute this value back into the equation:
E = 0.80 V - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2} \cdot{/tex} 1 = 0.80 V - 0.02955 V = 0.77045 V
So, the emf of the given cell is approximately 0.770 V.

question_text: The resistance and conductivity of a conductivity cell containing 0·001 M KCl solution at 298 K are 1200 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and 1·5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 S cm -1 . Calculate its cell constant and molar conductivity.

solution: k = {tex}\frac{1}{R}\left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex} {tex}1.5 \times 10^{-4} {/tex} Scm -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{1200} \Omega\left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex} {tex} \left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1200 cm -1 {tex}\left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex} = 0.18 cm -1 {tex} \Lambda_m {/tex} = {tex}\frac{\kappa}{C} \times 1000 \Omega^{-1} {/tex} cm 2 mol -1 = {tex}\frac{1 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-4}}{0 \cdot 001} \times 1000 \Omega^{-1}{/tex} cm 2 mol -1 {tex} \wedge_m{/tex} = 150 {tex}\Omega^{-1} {/tex} cm 2 mol -1 or 150 S cm 2 mol -1

k = {tex}\frac{1}{R}\left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex}
{tex}1.5 \times 10^{-4} {/tex} Scm-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{1200} \Omega\left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex}
{tex} \left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex}10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1200 cm-1
{tex}\left(\frac{l}{A}\right){/tex} = 0.18 cm-1
{tex} \Lambda_m {/tex} = {tex}\frac{\kappa}{C} \times 1000 \Omega^{-1} {/tex} cm2 mol-1
= {tex}\frac{1 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-4}}{0 \cdot 001} \times 1000 \Omega^{-1}{/tex} cm2 mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Why on dilution the {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} of CH3COOH increases very fast, while that of CH3COONa increases gradually?
  2. What happens if external potential applied becomes greater than {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} of electrochemical cell?
question_text: Why on dilution the {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} of CH 3 COOH increases very fast, while that of CH 3 COONa increases gradually? What happens if external potential applied becomes greater than {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} of electrochemical cell?
explanation: In the case of CH 3 COOH, which is a weak electrolyte, the number of ions increase on dilution due to an increase in the degree of dissociation. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} {/tex} {tex}\rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}(\mathrm{aq} .)+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}{/tex} In the case of strong electrolyte such as CH 3 COONa, the number of ions remains the same but the interionic attraction between ions decreases on the dilution. The chemical reaction of the cell is reversed and current flows in the opposite direction when opposing emf is slightly greater than that of the cell.
solution: In the case of CH 3 COOH, which is a weak electrolyte, the number of ions increase on dilution due to an increase in the degree of dissociation. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} {/tex} {tex}\rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}(\mathrm{aq} .)+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}{/tex} In the case of strong electrolyte such as CH 3 COONa, the number of ions remains the same but the interionic attraction between ions decreases on the dilution. The chemical reaction of the cell is reversed and current flows in the opposite direction when opposing emf is slightly greater than that of the cell.
solution_html:
1. In the case of CH3COOH, which is a weak electrolyte, the number of ions increase on dilution due to an increase in the degree of dissociation.
    {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} {/tex}{tex}\rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}(\mathrm{aq} .)+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}{/tex}
    In the case of strong electrolyte such as CH3COONa, the number of ions remains the same but the interionic attraction between ions decreases on the dilution.
  2. The chemical reaction of the cell is reversed and current flows in the opposite direction when opposing emf is slightly greater than that of the cell.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} and log Kc for the following cell:
Ni(s) + 2Ag+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ni2+(aq) + 2Ag(s)
Given that {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 1.05V, IF = 96,500 Cmol-1.
question_text: Calculate {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} and log K c for the following cell: Ni(s) + 2Ag + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ni 2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s) Given that {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 1.05V, IF = 96,500 Cmol -1 .
explanation: According to the equation, Ni + 2Ag + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Ni 2+ + 2Ag {tex} \Delta{/tex} G = -nFE o Where {tex} \Delta{/tex} G = Gibb's free energy {tex} \Delta{/tex} G = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.05 N = No. of electrons gain or lost = 2 {tex} \Delta{/tex} G = -202.650 kJ F = Faraday's constant = 96500 E o = Standard emf = 1.05V The relation between Gibb's free energy and Equilibrium constant is given by the equation {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.0591}{n} \log K_c{/tex} log K C = {tex}-\frac{1.05 \times 2}{0.0591}{/tex} = 35.3 K C = 3.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 35
solution: According to the equation, Ni + 2Ag + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Ni 2+ + 2Ag {tex} \Delta{/tex} G = -nFE o Where {tex} \Delta{/tex} G = Gibb's free energy {tex} \Delta{/tex} G = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.05 N = No. of electrons gain or lost = 2 {tex} \Delta{/tex} G = -202.650 kJ F = Faraday's constant = 96500 E o = Standard emf = 1.05V The relation between Gibb's free energy and Equilibrium constant is given by the equation {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.0591}{n} \log K_c{/tex} log K C = {tex}-\frac{1.05 \times 2}{0.0591}{/tex} = 35.3 K C = 3.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 35
solution_html:
According to the equation,
Ni + 2Ag+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Ni2+ + 2Ag
{tex} \Delta{/tex}G = -nFEo
Where {tex} \Delta{/tex}G = Gibb's free energy
{tex} \Delta{/tex}G = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.05
N = No. of electrons gain or lost = 2
{tex} \Delta{/tex}G = -202.650 kJ
F = Faraday's constant = 96500
Eo = Standard emf = 1.05V
The relation between Gibb's free energy and Equilibrium constant is given by the equation
{tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.0591}{n} \log K_c{/tex}
log KC = {tex}-\frac{1.05 \times 2}{0.0591}{/tex} = 35.3
KC = 3.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 1035

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the e.m.f. of the following cell at 298K:
Fe(s) | Fe2+ (0.001 M) || H+ (0.01 M) | H2(g) (1 bar) | Pt(s)
Given that {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = +0.44V
[log 2 = 0.3010 log 3 = 0.4771 log 10 = 1]
question_text: Calculate the e.m.f. of the following cell at 298K: Fe(s) | Fe 2+ (0.001 M) || H + (0.01 M) | H 2 (g) (1 bar) | Pt(s) Given that {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = +0.44V [log 2 = 0.3010 log 3 = 0.4771 log 10 = 1]
explanation: According to the equation, Fe(s) + 2H + (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Fe 2+ (aq) + H 2 (g) {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-E_{\text {anode }}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0 - (-0.44) V {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0.44V By applying Nernst Equation E cell = {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[F e^{2+}\right]}{\left[H^{+}\right]^2}{/tex} E cell = {tex}0.44 \frac{-0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.001}{1^2}{/tex} E cell = {tex}0.44-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log 10^{-3}{/tex} E cell = 0.44 + 0.089V E cell = +0.53V
solution: According to the equation, Fe(s) + 2H + (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Fe 2+ (aq) + H 2 (g) {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-E_{\text {anode }}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0 - (-0.44) V {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0.44V By applying Nernst Equation E cell = {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[F e^{2+}\right]}{\left[H^{+}\right]^2}{/tex} E cell = {tex}0.44 \frac{-0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.001}{1^2}{/tex} E cell = {tex}0.44-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log 10^{-3}{/tex} E cell = 0.44 + 0.089V E cell = +0.53V
solution_html:
According to the equation,
Fe(s) + 2H+ (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Fe2+ (aq) + H2(g)
{tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-E_{\text {anode }}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0 - (-0.44) V
{tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0.44V
By applying Nernst Equation
Ecell = {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[F e^{2+}\right]}{\left[H^{+}\right]^2}{/tex}
Ecell = {tex}0.44 \frac{-0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.001}{1^2}{/tex}
Ecell = {tex}0.44-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log 10^{-3}{/tex}
Ecell = 0.44 + 0.089V
Ecell = +0.53V

question_html: In a plot of {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} against the square root of concentration (C1/2) for strong and weak electrolyte, the value of limiting molar conductivity of a weak electrolyte cannot be obtained graphically. Suggest a way to obtain this value. Also state the related law, if any.
question_text: In a plot of {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} against the square root of concentration (C 1/2 ) for strong and weak electrolyte, the value of limiting molar conductivity of a weak electrolyte cannot be obtained graphically. Suggest a way to obtain this value. Also state the related law, if any.
explanation: Limiting molar conductivity for weak electrolyte can be obtained by using Kohlrausch law. It states that limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of conductivity of constituent ions.
solution: Limiting molar conductivity for weak electrolyte can be obtained by using Kohlrausch law. It states that limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of conductivity of constituent ions.

Limiting molar conductivity for weak electrolyte can be obtained by using Kohlrausch law.
It states that limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of conductivity of constituent ions.

Write the Nernst equation and calculate emf of the following cell at 298 K:
Cr | Cr3+ (0·1 M) || Fe2+ (0·01 M) | Fe
Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cr}^{3+} / \mathrm{Cr}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -0·75 V
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -0·45 V
(log 10 = 1)
question_text: Write the Nernst equation and calculate emf of the following cell at 298 K: Cr | Cr 3+ (0·1 M) || Fe 2+ (0·01 M) | Fe Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cr}^{3+} / \mathrm{Cr}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -0·75 V {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -0·45 V (log 10 = 1)
explanation: {tex}E_{c e l l} {/tex} = {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^0-\frac{0.059}{6} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\right]^3} \\ {/tex} {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^0{/tex} = {tex}-0.45-(-0.75){/tex} = 0.30 V {tex}E_{\text {cell }}{/tex} = {tex}0.3-\frac{0.059}{6} \log \frac{(0-1)^2}{(0.01)^3}{/tex} = {tex}0 \cdot 3-.00985 \log \frac{\left(10^{-1}\right)^2}{\left(10^{-2}\right)^3}{/tex} = 0.3 - .00985 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 log10 = 0.3 - 0.0394 = 0.2606 V

question_text: Answer the following questions on the basis of the given figure: Define the process depicted in the above figure. Can this process be used in the coagulation of the lyophobic sols? What is coagulation?
explanation: The movement of colloidal particles under an applied electric potential. Yes this process be used in the coagulation of the lyophobic sols. The process of settling colloidal particles is coagulation.
solution: The movement of colloidal particles under an applied electric potential. Yes this process be used in the coagulation of the lyophobic sols. The process of settling colloidal particles is coagulation.

  2. Yes this process be used in the coagulation of the lyophobic sols.
  3. The process of settling colloidal particles is coagulation.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Define molar conductivity for the solution of an electrolyte. How does it vary with concentration?
question_text: Define molar conductivity for the solution of an electrolyte. How does it vary with concentration?
explanation: Molar conductivity is the conductivity of solution observed in 1 M solution. Molar conductivity decreases with increase in concentration or molar conductivity increases with dilution.
solution: Molar conductivity is the conductivity of solution observed in 1 M solution. Molar conductivity decreases with increase in concentration or molar conductivity increases with dilution.
solution_html:
Molar conductivity is the conductivity of solution observed in 1 M solution.
Molar conductivity decreases with increase in concentration or molar conductivity increases with dilution.

question_text: Define conductivity for the solution of an electrolyte. Why does the conductivity of a solution decrease with dilution?
explanation: Conductivity is the conductance of one unit volume of solution kept between two platinum electrodes with unit area of cross-section and at a distance of unit length. Because of decrease in the number of ions per unit volume.
solution: Conductivity is the conductance of one unit volume of solution kept between two platinum electrodes with unit area of cross-section and at a distance of unit length. Because of decrease in the number of ions per unit volume.

Conductivity is the conductance of one unit volume of solution kept between two platinum electrodes with unit area of cross-section and at a distance of unit length.
Because of decrease in the number of ions per unit volume.

question_text: The conductivity of 0.001M acetic acid is 7.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 S cm -1 . Calculate its degree of dissociation if {tex}\wedge^{\circ}_\mathrm{m}{/tex} for acetic acid is 390 S cm 2 mol -1 .
explanation: {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{k}}{C} \times 1000 \Omega^{-1} {/tex} cm 2 mol -1 = {tex}\frac{7.8 \times 10^{-5}}{0.001} {/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}1000 \Omega^{-1}{/tex} cm 2 mol -1 = {tex}78 \Omega^{-1}{/tex} cm 2 mol -1 or 78 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha =\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{78}{390}{/tex} = 0.2
solution: {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{k}}{C} \times 1000 \Omega^{-1} {/tex} cm 2 mol -1 = {tex}\frac{7.8 \times 10^{-5}}{0.001} {/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}1000 \Omega^{-1}{/tex} cm 2 mol -1 = {tex}78 \Omega^{-1}{/tex} cm 2 mol -1 or 78 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha =\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{78}{390}{/tex} = 0.2

{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{k}}{C} \times 1000 \Omega^{-1} {/tex} cm2 mol-1
= {tex}\frac{7.8 \times 10^{-5}}{0.001} {/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}1000 \Omega^{-1}{/tex} cm2 mol-1
= {tex}78 \Omega^{-1}{/tex}cm2 mol-1 or 78 S cm2 mol-1

On dilution, why does the molar conductivity of HCOOH increase drastically, while that of HCOONa increases gradually?
explanation: E cell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^0{/tex} - {tex}\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]^3}{/tex} E cell = 2 - {tex}\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{\left[10^{-1}\right]^2}{\left[10^{-2}\right]^3}{/tex} E cell = 2 - {tex}\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} log 10 4 E cell = 2 - {tex}\frac{0.059}{6}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 4 log 10 E cell = 2 - 0.039 E cell = 1.961 V Molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is the conductance of the volume V of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with area of cross section A and distance of unit length. solution: A = {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = {tex}\frac{22}{7}{/tex} {tex}\times {/tex} (0.7) 2 = 1.54 cm 2 {tex}\ell{/tex} = 44 cm {tex}\frac{\ell}{A}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{44}{1.54}{/tex} cm -1 {tex}\frac{1}{\rho}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{R} \frac{l}{A}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{5 \times 10^3} \times \frac{44}{1.54}{/tex} cm -1 {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 \times 10^3}{44}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1.54 {tex}\rho{/tex} = 175 {tex}\Omega{/tex} cm k = {tex}\frac{1}{ \rho}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{175} {/tex} k = 0.0057 {tex}\Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{k}{c}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 = {tex}\frac{0.0057}{0.02}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 = 285 Scm 2 mol -1 Ni (s) | Ni 2+ (aq.) || Ag + (aq.) | Ag (s) Ag electrons

    {tex}\frac{\ell}{A}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{44}{1.54}{/tex} cm-1

question_html: Conductivity of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M methanoic acid is 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 S cm–1. Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation if {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} for methanoic acid is 404 S cm2 mol-1.
question_text: Conductivity of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M methanoic acid is 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 S cm –1 . Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation if {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} for methanoic acid is 404 S cm 2 mol -1 .
explanation: Concentration of methanoid acid = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M The conductivity of methanoic acid = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 S cm –1 {tex}\left(\lambda_{m}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1000 \times \text { Conductivity }(k)}{\text { Concentration }(c)}{/tex} So, {tex}\left(\lambda_{m}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1000 \times 8 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 40 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\wedge_{m}}{{\wedge}_{m}^o}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{40}{404}{/tex} = 0.099
solution: Concentration of methanoid acid = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M The conductivity of methanoic acid = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 S cm –1 {tex}\left(\lambda_{m}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1000 \times \text { Conductivity }(k)}{\text { Concentration }(c)}{/tex} So, {tex}\left(\lambda_{m}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1000 \times 8 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 40 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\wedge_{m}}{{\wedge}_{m}^o}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{40}{404}{/tex} = 0.099
solution_html:
Concentration of methanoid acid = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M 
The conductivity of methanoic acid = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 S cm–1 
{tex}\left(\lambda_{m}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1000 \times \text { Conductivity }(k)}{\text { Concentration }(c)}{/tex} 
So, 
 {tex}\left(\lambda_{m}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1000 \times 8 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 40 S cm2 mol-1 
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\wedge_{m}}{{\wedge}_{m}^o}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{40}{404}{/tex} 
= 0.099

question_html: In a lead storage battery:
question_text: In a lead storage battery:

options.text: PbO 2 is reduced to PbSO 4 at the cathode.
options.html: PbO2 is reduced to PbSO4 at the cathode.

options.text: Pb is oxidised to PbSO 4 at the anode.
options.html: Pb is oxidised to PbSO4 at the anode.

options.text: Both electrodes are immersed in the same aqueous solution of H 2 SO 4 .
options.html: Both electrodes are immersed in the same aqueous solution of H2SO4.

options.explanation: In a lead storage battery, all of these statements are true. Explanation: The lead storage battery is a rechargeable battery . This battery has a pure lead as anode; lead(IV) oxide as a cathode and dilute sulfuric acid as an electrolyte. When the battery is being used, lead(IV) oxide is reduced to PdSO 4 and lead is oxidized to PdSO 4 . When the battery is being charged, PdSO₄ is again converted into PbO 2 and Pb as cathode and anode respectively.

explanation: In a lead storage battery, all of these statements are true. Explanation: The lead storage battery is a rechargeable battery . When the battery is being charged, PdSO₄ is again converted into PbO 2 and Pb as cathode and anode respectively.

question_html:
When a steady current of 2A was passed through two electrolytic cells A and B containing electrolytes ZnSO4 and CuSO4 connected in series, 2g of Cu were deposited at the cathode of cell B. How long did the current flow? What mass of Zn was deposited at cathode of cell A?
[Atomic mass: Cu = 63.5 g mol-1, Zn = 65 g mol-1; 1F = 96500 C mol-1]
question_text: When a steady current of 2A was passed through two electrolytic cells A and B containing electrolytes ZnSO 4 and CuSO 4 connected in series, 2g of Cu were deposited at the cathode of cell B. How long did the current flow? What mass of Zn was deposited at cathode of cell A? [Atomic mass: Cu = 63.5 g mol -1 , Zn = 65 g mol -1 ; 1F = 96500 C mol -1 ]
explanation: Steady current = 2A copper deposited at cathode of cell B = 2g Cell B contains CuSO 4 and reaction may be represented as, {tex}CuS{O_4} \to C{u^{2 + }} + SO_4^{2 - }{/tex} The reaction happens at cathode as, Cu 2+ + 2e - {tex}\to{/tex} Cu So, 1 mol Cu deposited by 2F charge 63.5 g Cu {tex}\to{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 2g Cu {tex}\to{/tex} x x = {tex}\frac{2 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.5}{/tex} = 6078.74 C we know formula Q = It 6078.74 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} t t = {tex}\frac{6078.74}{2}{/tex} = 3039.37 Sec From faraday's second law of electrolysis, {tex}\frac{\omega t \text { of } C u}{\omega t \text { of } Z n}=\frac{E q \omega t C u}{E q \cdot \omega t Z n}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{x}=\frac{63.5 / 2}{65 / 2}{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{2}{0.9769}{/tex} = 2.047 g weight of Zn deposited = 2.047 g
solution: Steady current = 2A copper deposited at cathode of cell B = 2g Cell B contains CuSO 4 and reaction may be represented as, {tex}CuS{O_4} \to C{u^{2 + }} + SO_4^{2 - }{/tex} The reaction happens at cathode as, Cu 2+ + 2e - {tex}\to{/tex} Cu So, 1 mol Cu deposited by 2F charge 63.5 g Cu {tex}\to{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 2g Cu {tex}\to{/tex} x x = {tex}\frac{2 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.5}{/tex} = 6078.74 C we know formula Q = It 6078.74 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} t t = {tex}\frac{6078.74}{2}{/tex} = 3039.37 Sec From faraday's second law of electrolysis, {tex}\frac{\omega t \text { of } C u}{\omega t \text { of } Z n}=\frac{E q \omega t C u}{E q \cdot \omega t Z n}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{x}=\frac{63.5 / 2}{65 / 2}{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{2}{0.9769}{/tex} = 2.047 g weight of Zn deposited = 2.047 g
solution_html:
Steady current = 2A
copper deposited at cathode of cell B = 2g
Cell B contains CuSO4 and reaction may be represented as,
{tex}CuS{O_4} \to C{u^{2 + }} + SO_4^{2 - }{/tex} 
The reaction happens at cathode as,
Cu2+ + 2e- {tex}\to{/tex} Cu
So, 1 mol Cu deposited by 2F charge
63.5 g Cu {tex}\to{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500
2g Cu {tex}\to{/tex} x 
x = {tex}\frac{2 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.5}{/tex} = 6078.74 C 
we know formula
Q = It
6078.74 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} t 
t = {tex}\frac{6078.74}{2}{/tex} = 3039.37 Sec
From faraday's second law of electrolysis,
{tex}\frac{\omega t \text { of } C u}{\omega t \text { of } Z n}=\frac{E q \omega t C u}{E q \cdot \omega t Z n}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{2}{x}=\frac{63.5 / 2}{65 / 2}{/tex} 
x = {tex}\frac{2}{0.9769}{/tex} = 2.047 g 
weight of Zn deposited = 2.047 g

1. Out of the following pairs, predict with reason which pair will allow greater conduction of electricity:
    1. Silver wire at 30oC or silver wire at 60oC.
    2. 0.1 M CH3COOH solution or 1 M CH3COOH solution.
    3. KCl solution at 20oC or KCl solution at 50oC.
  2. Give two points of differences between electrochemical and electrolytic cells.
question_text: Out of the following pairs, predict with reason which pair will allow greater conduction of electricity: Silver wire at 30 o C or silver wire at 60 o C. 0.1 M CH 3 COOH solution or 1 M CH 3 COOH solution. KCl solution at 20 o C or KCl solution at 50 o C. Give two points of differences between electrochemical and electrolytic cells.
explanation: Silver wire at 30 o C will allow greater conduction of electricity because as temperature decreases, resistance decreases Hence, conduction increases. 0.1 M CH 3 COOH will allow greater conduction of electricity, because on dilution degree of ionization increases hence conduction increases. KCl solution at 50 o C will allow greater conduction of electricity, because at high temperature mobility of ions increases and hence conductance increases. Electrochemical Cell Electrolytic Cell Anode -ve Cathode +ve Anode +ve Cathode -ve Convert chemical Energy to electrical energy Convert electrical Energy to chemical energy
solution: Silver wire at 30 o C will allow greater conduction of electricity because as temperature decreases, resistance decreases Hence, conduction increases. 0.1 M CH 3 COOH will allow greater conduction of electricity, because on dilution degree of ionization increases hence conduction increases. KCl solution at 50 o C will allow greater conduction of electricity, because at high temperature mobility of ions increases and hence conductance increases. Electrochemical Cell Electrolytic Cell Anode -ve Cathode +ve Anode +ve Cathode -ve Convert chemical Energy to electrical energy Convert electrical Energy to chemical energy

1. Silver wire at 30oC will allow greater conduction of electricity because as temperature decreases, resistance decreases Hence, conduction increases.
    2. 0.1 M CH3COOH will allow greater conduction of electricity, because on dilution degree of ionization increases hence conduction increases.
    3. KCl solution at 50oC will allow greater conduction of electricity, because at high temperature mobility of ions increases and hence conductance increases.

    Anode -ve Cathode +ve Anode +ve Cathode -ve

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Calculate e.m.f. of the following cell:
    Zn(s)/Zn2+ (0.1 M) || (0.01 M) Ag+/Ag(s)
    Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = –0.76 V, {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0.80 V
    [Given : log 10 = 1]
  2. X and Y are two electrolytes. On dilution molar conductivity of ‘X’ increases 2.5 times while that Y increases 25 times. Which of the two is a weak electrolyte and why?
question_text: Calculate e.m.f. of the following cell: Zn(s)/Zn 2+ (0.1 M) || (0.01 M) Ag + /Ag(s) Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = –0.76 V, {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0.80 V [Given : log 10 = 1] X and Y are two electrolytes. On dilution molar conductivity of ‘X’ increases 2.5 times while that Y increases 25 times. Which of the two is a weak electrolyte and why?
explanation: Given, we have two half cells, Ag +1 (aq) + e -1 {tex}\to{/tex} Ag(s) reduction E 0 = 0.80 V Zn +2 (aq) + 2e -1 {tex}\to{/tex} Zn(s) oxidation E 0 = -0.76 The net cell reaction is 2 Ag +1 (aq) + Zn(s) {tex}\to{/tex} 2Ag(s) + Zn +2 (aq) As, reduction potential of Zn is lower, it undergoes oxidation. We know, E 0 ell = E 0 reduction - E 0 oxidation = 0.80 - (-0.76) = 1.56 V Now. we know EMF = E 0 cell - (0.0591 / n) log[anode]/[cathode], where n is number of electrons transferred EMF = 1.56 - (0.0591 /2) log0.1/(0.01) 2 = 1.56 - (0.0591/2) log10 3 EMF = 1.56 - (0.0591/2)*3 = 1.56 - 0.08865 = 1.471 V Strong electrolyte has already completely dissociated in the solution, so on dilution only less amount of its molar conductivity increases. Whereas, for weak dilution on dilution its dissociation increases, leading to steep increase in its molar conductivity.
solution: Given, we have two half cells, Ag +1 (aq) + e -1 {tex}\to{/tex} Ag(s) reduction E 0 = 0.80 V Zn +2 (aq) + 2e -1 {tex}\to{/tex} Zn(s) oxidation E 0 = -0.76 The net cell reaction is 2 Ag +1 (aq) + Zn(s) {tex}\to{/tex} 2Ag(s) + Zn +2 (aq) As, reduction potential of Zn is lower, it undergoes oxidation. We know, E 0 ell = E 0 reduction - E 0 oxidation = 0.80 - (-0.76) = 1.56 V Now. we know EMF = E 0 cell - (0.0591 / n) log[anode]/[cathode], where n is number of electrons transferred EMF = 1.56 - (0.0591 /2) log0.1/(0.01) 2 = 1.56 - (0.0591/2) log10 3 EMF = 1.56 - (0.0591/2)*3 = 1.56 - 0.08865 = 1.471 V Strong electrolyte has already completely dissociated in the solution, so on dilution only less amount of its molar conductivity increases. Whereas, for weak dilution on dilution its dissociation increases, leading to steep increase in its molar conductivity.
solution_html:
1. Given, we have two half cells,
    Ag+1(aq) + e-1 {tex}\to{/tex} Ag(s) reduction E0 = 0.80 V
    Zn+2(aq) + 2e-1 {tex}\to{/tex} Zn(s) oxidation E0 = -0.76
    The net cell reaction is 2 Ag+1(aq) + Zn(s) {tex}\to{/tex} 2Ag(s) + Zn+2(aq)
    As, reduction potential of Zn is lower, it undergoes oxidation.
    We know, E0ell = E0 reduction - E0 oxidation = 0.80 - (-0.76) = 1.56 V
    Now. we know EMF = E0cell - (0.0591 / n) log[anode]/[cathode], where n is number of electrons transferred
    EMF = 1.56 - (0.0591 /2) log0.1/(0.01)2 = 1.56 - (0.0591/2) log103
    EMF = 1.56 - (0.0591/2)*3 = 1.56 - 0.08865 = 1.471 V
  2. Strong electrolyte has already completely dissociated in the solution, so on dilution only less amount of its molar conductivity increases.
    Whereas, for weak dilution on dilution its dissociation increases, leading to steep increase in its molar conductivity.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: An electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell when
question_text: An electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell when

options.explanation: If an external opposite potential is applied on the electrochemical cell, the reaction continues to take place till the opposite voltage reaches the value 1.1 V. At this stage, no current flow through the cell and if there is any further increase in the external potential (E external ), then reaction starts functioning in opposite direction i.e. an electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell. {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {external }}>\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}{/tex}

explanation: If an external opposite potential is applied on the electrochemical cell, the reaction continues to take place till the opposite voltage reaches the value 1.1 V. At this stage, no current flow through the cell and if there is any further increase in the external potential (E external ), then reaction starts functioning in opposite direction i.e. an electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell. {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {external }}>\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. The electrical resistance of a column of 0.05 M KOH solution of length 50 cm and area of cross-section 0.625 cm2 is 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 ohm. Calculate its resistivity, conductivity and molar conductivity.
  2. Predict the products of electrolysis of an aqueous solution of CuCl2 with platinum electrodes.
    (Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = +0.34 V, {tex}E^o _{(1/2 Cl_2 Cl^-)}{/tex} = +1.36 V
    {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{g}), \mathrm{Pt}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = 0.00 V, {tex}E^o _{(1/2 O_2/H_2O)}{/tex} = +1.23 V)
question_text: The electrical resistance of a column of 0.05 M KOH solution of length 50 cm and area of cross-section 0.625 cm 2 is 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 ohm. Calculate its resistivity, conductivity and molar conductivity. Predict the products of electrolysis of an aqueous solution of CuCl 2 with platinum electrodes. (Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = +0.34 V, {tex}E^o _{(1/2 Cl_2 Cl^-)}{/tex} = +1.36 V {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{g}), \mathrm{Pt}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = 0.00 V, {tex}E^o _{(1/2 O_2/H_2O)}{/tex} = +1.23 V)
explanation: {tex}R=\frac{\rho l}{a}=\frac{5 \times 10^3 \times 0.625}{50}=62.5 \Omega{/tex} Condutivity = {tex}\kappa = \frac{1}{\rho}=\frac{1}{62.5}=0.016 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}{/tex} Molar - conductivity = {tex}\Lambda_m=\frac{\kappa \times 1000}{c}=320 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}{/tex} Given {tex}E_{C u^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}=+0.34 V \cdot E_{\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2 / C l^{-}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} = +1.36 V At cathode : Cu 2+ + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu Because {tex}E_{C u^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ} > E_{H^{+} / \mathrm{H_2}}{/tex} At anode: H 2 O {tex}\rightarrow \frac12{/tex} O 2 + 2H + + 2e - This reaction should occur at anode but due to over-potential of O 2 , oxidation of C l- is preferred. Hence the reaction is 2Cl - {tex}\to{/tex} Cl 2 + 2e -
solution: {tex}R=\frac{\rho l}{a}=\frac{5 \times 10^3 \times 0.625}{50}=62.5 \Omega{/tex} Condutivity = {tex}\kappa = \frac{1}{\rho}=\frac{1}{62.5}=0.016 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}{/tex} Molar - conductivity = {tex}\Lambda_m=\frac{\kappa \times 1000}{c}=320 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}{/tex} Given {tex}E_{C u^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}=+0.34 V \cdot E_{\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2 / C l^{-}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} = +1.36 V At cathode : Cu 2+ + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu Because {tex}E_{C u^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ} > E_{H^{+} / \mathrm{H_2}}{/tex} At anode: H 2 O {tex}\rightarrow \frac12{/tex} O 2 + 2H + + 2e - This reaction should occur at anode but due to over-potential of O 2 , oxidation of C l- is preferred. Hence the reaction is 2Cl - {tex}\to{/tex} Cl 2 + 2e -
solution_html:
1. {tex}R=\frac{\rho l}{a}=\frac{5 \times 10^3 \times 0.625}{50}=62.5 \Omega{/tex}
    Condutivity = {tex}\kappa = \frac{1}{\rho}=\frac{1}{62.5}=0.016 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}{/tex}
    Molar - conductivity = {tex}\Lambda_m=\frac{\kappa \times 1000}{c}=320 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
  2. Given
    {tex}E_{C u^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}=+0.34 V \cdot E_{\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2 / C l^{-}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} = +1.36 V
    At cathode : Cu2+ + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu
    Because {tex}E_{C u^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ} > E_{H^{+} / \mathrm{H_2}}{/tex}
    At anode:
    H2O {tex}\rightarrow \frac12{/tex} O2 + 2H+ + 2e-
    This reaction should occur at anode but due to over-potential of O2, oxidation of Cl- is preferred.
    Hence the reaction is
    2Cl- {tex}\to{/tex} Cl2 + 2e-

subject: Chemistry
question_html: State Kohlrausch’s law. Calculate the molar conductance of Sr(NO3)2. The molar ionic conductance of Sr2+ and {tex}\mathrm{NO}_3^{-}{/tex} ions are 119 S cm2 mol-1 and 72 S cm2 mol-1 respectively.
question_text: State Kohlrausch’s law. Calculate the molar conductance of Sr(NO 3 ) 2 . The molar ionic conductance of Sr 2+ and {tex}\mathrm{NO}_3^{-}{/tex} ions are 119 S cm 2 mol -1 and 72 S cm 2 mol -1 respectively.
explanation: Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. Molar conductivity of Sr(NO 3 ) 2 = {tex}\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Sr}^{2+}+2 \lambda^{\mathrm{o}} N \mathrm{O}_3^{-}{/tex} = 119 + (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 72) = 263 Scm 2 /mol Hence, molar conductance of Sr(NO 3 ) 2 will be 263 Scm 2 /mol.
solution: Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. Molar conductivity of Sr(NO 3 ) 2 = {tex}\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Sr}^{2+}+2 \lambda^{\mathrm{o}} N \mathrm{O}_3^{-}{/tex} = 119 + (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 72) = 263 Scm 2 /mol Hence, molar conductance of Sr(NO 3 ) 2 will be 263 Scm 2 /mol.
solution_html:
Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte.
Molar conductivity of Sr(NO3)2 = {tex}\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Sr}^{2+}+2 \lambda^{\mathrm{o}} N \mathrm{O}_3^{-}{/tex}
= 119 + (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 72)
= 263 Scm2/mol
Hence, molar conductance of Sr(NO3)2 will be 263 Scm2/mol.
