question_text: State Kohlrausch’s law. Calculate the molar conductance of Ba(OH) 2 . The molar ionic conductance of Ba 2+ and OH - ions are 127 and 199 S cm 2 mol -1 respectively.
explanation: Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. Molar conductivity of Ba(OH) 2 = {tex}\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}+2 \lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex} = 127 + (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 199) = 525 Scm 2 /mol Hence, molar conductance of Ba(OH) 2 will be 525 Scm 2 /mol.
solution: Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. Molar conductivity of Ba(OH) 2 = {tex}\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}+2 \lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex} = 127 + (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 199) = 525 Scm 2 /mol Hence, molar conductance of Ba(OH) 2 will be 525 Scm 2 /mol.
solution_html:
Limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte.
Molar conductivity of Ba(OH)2 = {tex}\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}+2 \lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex}
= 127 + (2 {tex}\times{/tex}199)
= 525 Scm2/mol
Hence, molar conductance of Ba(OH)2 will be 525 Scm2/mol.

question_html:
Calculate {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex}for the following reaction at 298 K:
2Cr(s) + 3Fe2+(0.01M) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr3+(0.01M) + 3Fe(s)
Given: Ecell = 0.261 V
question_text: Calculate {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} for the following reaction at 298 K: 2Cr(s) + 3Fe 2+ (0.01M) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ (0.01M) + 3Fe(s) Given: E cell = 0.261 V
explanation: According to the Nernst equation- {tex}\mathrm{E}_{(\text {cell })}=\mathrm{E}_{(\text {cell })}^0 - \frac {0.059}{ 6} \log \frac {\left[\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\right]^3}{/tex} {tex}0.261=E_{(\text {cell })}^0 - \frac {0.059}{ 6} \log \frac {[0.01]^2}{[0.01]^3}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}^0 _{(cell)}{/tex} = 0.261 + 0.0983(2) {tex}\mathrm{E}^0 _{(cell)}{/tex} = 0.261+ 0.1966 {tex}\mathrm{E}^0_{ (cell)}{/tex} = 0.4576 V
solution: According to the Nernst equation- {tex}\mathrm{E}_{(\text {cell })}=\mathrm{E}_{(\text {cell })}^0 - \frac {0.059}{ 6} \log \frac {\left[\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\right]^3}{/tex} {tex}0.261=E_{(\text {cell })}^0 - \frac {0.059}{ 6} \log \frac {[0.01]^2}{[0.01]^3}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{E}^0 _{(cell)}{/tex} = 0.261 + 0.0983(2) {tex}\mathrm{E}^0 _{(cell)}{/tex} = 0.261+ 0.1966 {tex}\mathrm{E}^0_{ (cell)}{/tex} = 0.4576 V
solution_html:
According to the Nernst equation-
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{(\text {cell })}=\mathrm{E}_{(\text {cell })}^0 - \frac {0.059}{ 6} \log \frac {\left[\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\right]^3}{/tex}
{tex}0.261=E_{(\text {cell })}^0 - \frac {0.059}{ 6} \log \frac {[0.01]^2}{[0.01]^3}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{E}^0 _{(cell)}{/tex} = 0.261 + 0.0983(2)
{tex}\mathrm{E}^0 _{(cell)}{/tex} = 0.261+ 0.1966
{tex}\mathrm{E}^0_{ (cell)}{/tex} = 0.4576 V

question_text: Following reactions may occur at cathode and anode during electrolysis of aqueous sodium chloride. What products will be held at anode and cathode? Use given E o values to justify your answer. Cathode: Na + (aq) + e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Na(s) E o = -2.71 V H + (aq) + e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} H 2 (g) E o = 0.00 V Anode: Cl - (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} Cl 2 (g) + e - E o = +1.36 V 2H 2 O(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} O 2 (g) + 4H + + 4e - E o = +1.23 V
explanation: At cathode- H 2 (g) is produced due to greater E o value of H + ion At anode- Cl 2 (g) is produced due to over voltage /over potential of oxygen
solution: At cathode- H 2 (g) is produced due to greater E o value of H + ion At anode- Cl 2 (g) is produced due to over voltage /over potential of oxygen

question_text: Calculate {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} and log K c for the following reaction: Cd +2 (aq) + Zn(s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + Cd(s) Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cd}}^{\mathrm{o}} 2+/ \mathrm{_C}_{d}{/tex} = -0.403 V {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}}^{\mathrm{O}} 2+/ \mathrm{_Z}{_n}{/tex} = -0.763 V
explanation: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^o{/tex} = E cathode - E anode = -0.403 - (-0.763) = 0.360 V {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^o{/tex} = {tex}-\mathrm{nFE}{ }_{\text {cell }}^o{/tex} = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} f0.360 = -69480 J mol-1 or -69.480 kJ mol -1 Hence, {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} for the given reaction is -69.480 kJ mol -1 log K c = {tex}\mathrm{nE}^o_ {cell }{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.360 / 0.059 log K c = 12.20
solution: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^o{/tex} = E cathode - E anode = -0.403 - (-0.763) = 0.360 V {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^o{/tex} = {tex}-\mathrm{nFE}{ }_{\text {cell }}^o{/tex} = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} f0.360 = -69480 J mol-1 or -69.480 kJ mol -1 Hence, {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} for the given reaction is -69.480 kJ mol -1 log K c = {tex}\mathrm{nE}^o_ {cell }{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.360 / 0.059 log K c = 12.20
solution_html:
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^o{/tex} = Ecathode - Eanode
= -0.403 - (-0.763) = 0.360 V
{tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^o{/tex} = {tex}-\mathrm{nFE}{ }_{\text {cell }}^o{/tex}
= -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} f0.360
= -69480 J mol-1 or -69.480 kJ mol-1
Hence, {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} for the given reaction is  -69.480 kJ mol-1
log Kc = {tex}\mathrm{nE}^o_ {cell }{/tex}

log Kc = 12.20

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Chromium metal is electroplated using an acidic solution containing CrO3 according to the following equation:
CrO3(aq) + 6H+ + 6e- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cr(s) + 3H2O
Calculate how many grams of chromium will be electroplated by 24,000 coulombs. How long will it take to electroplate 1.5 g chromium using 12.5 A current?
[Atomic mass of Cr = 52 g mol-1, 1 F = 96500 C mol-1]
question_text: Chromium metal is electroplated using an acidic solution containing CrO 3 according to the following equation: CrO 3 (aq) + 6H + + 6e - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cr(s) + 3H 2 O Calculate how many grams of chromium will be electroplated by 24,000 coulombs. How long will it take to electroplate 1.5 g chromium using 12.5 A current? [Atomic mass of Cr = 52 g mol -1 , 1 F = 96500 C mol -1 ]
explanation: 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C deposit 1 mole Cr = 52 g 24000 C will deposit 52 {tex}\times{/tex} 24000 / 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 = 2.155 g Hence, 2.155 g grams of chromium will be electroplated by 24,000 coulombs. 52 g of Cr deposited by 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C 1.5 g Cr deposited by 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 / 52 = 16701.9 C Q = I {tex}\times{/tex} t t = Q/I = 16701.9 C / 12.5 A = 1336 s
solution: 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C deposit 1 mole Cr = 52 g 24000 C will deposit 52 {tex}\times{/tex} 24000 / 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 = 2.155 g Hence, 2.155 g grams of chromium will be electroplated by 24,000 coulombs. 52 g of Cr deposited by 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C 1.5 g Cr deposited by 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 / 52 = 16701.9 C Q = I {tex}\times{/tex} t t = Q/I = 16701.9 C / 12.5 A = 1336 s
solution_html:
6 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C deposit 1 mole Cr = 52 g
24000 C will deposit 52 {tex}\times{/tex} 24000 / 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 = 2.155 g
Hence, 2.155 g grams of chromium will be electroplated by 24,000 coulombs.
52 g of Cr deposited by 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C
1.5 g Cr deposited by 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 / 52 = 16701.9 C
Q = I {tex}\times{/tex} t
t = Q/I = 16701.9 C / 12.5 A = 1336 s

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the emf of the following cell at 25oC:
Al (s) | Al3+ (0.001 M) || (0.1) Ni2+ | Ni (s)
Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\left(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+} / \mathrm{Ni}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = -0.25 V
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\left(\mathrm{Al}^{3+} / \mathrm{Al}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = -1.66 V
[log 2 = 0.3010, log 3 = 0.4771]
question_text: Calculate the emf of the following cell at 25 o C: Al (s) | Al 3+ (0.001 M) || (0.1) Ni 2+ | Ni (s) Given: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\left(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+} / \mathrm{Ni}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = -0.25 V {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\left(\mathrm{Al}^{3+} / \mathrm{Al}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = -1.66 V [log 2 = 0.3010, log 3 = 0.4771]
explanation: n = 6 E o = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cathode }}^0-\mathrm{E}_{\text {anode }}^0{/tex} = (-0.25) - (-1.66) = 1.41 V E cell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell- }}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} (0.059/n) log [Al +3 ] 2 /[Ni +2 ] 3 E cell = 1.41-(0.059/6)log(10 -3 ) 2 /(0.1) 3 E cell = 1.4395 V Hence, the emf of the following cell at 25 o C is 1.4395 V
solution: n = 6 E o = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cathode }}^0-\mathrm{E}_{\text {anode }}^0{/tex} = (-0.25) - (-1.66) = 1.41 V E cell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell- }}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} (0.059/n) log [Al +3 ] 2 /[Ni +2 ] 3 E cell = 1.41-(0.059/6)log(10 -3 ) 2 /(0.1) 3 E cell = 1.4395 V Hence, the emf of the following cell at 25 o C is 1.4395 V
solution_html:
n = 6
Eo = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cathode }}^0-\mathrm{E}_{\text {anode }}^0{/tex}
= (-0.25) - (-1.66)
= 1.41 V
Ecell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell- }}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} (0.059/n) log [Al+3]2 /[Ni+2]3
Ecell = 1.41-(0.059/6)log(10-3)2 /(0.1)3
Ecell = 1.4395 V
Hence, the emf of the following cell at 25oC is 1.4395 V

solution: {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\kappa \times 1000}{C}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.65 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \times 1000 \mathrm{~cm}^3 L^{-1}}{0.01 \mathrm{molL}^{-1}}{/tex} = 16.5 S cm 2 mol -1

question_html:
Consider the following reaction:
Cu(s) + 2Ag+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Ag(s) + Cu2+(aq)

  1. Depict the galvanic cell in which the given reaction takes place.
  2. Give the direction of flow of current.
  3. Write the half-cell reactions taking place at cathode and anode.
question_text: Consider the following reaction: Cu(s) + 2Ag + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Ag(s) + Cu 2+ (aq) Depict the galvanic cell in which the given reaction takes place. Give the direction of flow of current. Write the half-cell reactions taking place at cathode and anode.
explanation: Cu(s) | Cu 2+ (aq) || Ag + (aq) | Ag(s) Current will flow from silver to copper electrode in the external circuit. Cathode: 2Ag + (aq) + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Ag(s) Anode: Cu(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu 2+ (aq) + 2e -
solution: Cu(s) | Cu 2+ (aq) || Ag + (aq) | Ag(s) Current will flow from silver to copper electrode in the external circuit. Cathode: 2Ag + (aq) + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Ag(s) Anode: Cu(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu 2+ (aq) + 2e -
solution_html:
1. Cu(s) | Cu2+(aq) || Ag+(aq) | Ag(s)
  2. Current will flow from silver to copper electrode in the external circuit.
  3. Cathode: 2Ag+(aq) + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Ag(s)
    Anode: Cu(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu2+(aq) + 2e-

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Write the name of the cell which is generally used in transistors. Write the reactions taking place at the anode and the cathode of this cell.
question_text: Write the name of the cell which is generally used in transistors. Write the reactions taking place at the anode and the cathode of this cell.
explanation: Leclanchecell is commonly used in transistors. The electrode reactions of Leclanchecell can be written as At anode Zn(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ + 2e - At cathode {tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{2}+\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{MnO}(\mathrm{OH})+\mathrm{NH}_{3}{/tex} NH 3 formed in reaction at cathode combines with Zn 2+ to form the complex like [Zn(NH 3 ) 4 ] 2+ .
solution: Leclanchecell is commonly used in transistors. The electrode reactions of Leclanchecell can be written as At anode Zn(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ + 2e - At cathode {tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{2}+\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{MnO}(\mathrm{OH})+\mathrm{NH}_{3}{/tex} NH 3 formed in reaction at cathode combines with Zn 2+ to form the complex like [Zn(NH 3 ) 4 ] 2+ .
solution_html:
Leclanchecell is commonly used in transistors. The electrode reactions of Leclanchecell can be written as
At anode Zn(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ + 2e-
At cathode
{tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{2}+\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{MnO}(\mathrm{OH})+\mathrm{NH}_{3}{/tex}
NH3 formed in reaction at cathode combines with Zn2+ to form the complex like [Zn(NH3)4]2+.

question_html:
1. Calculate the mass of Ag deposited at cathode when a current of 2A was passed through a solution of AgNO3 for 15 min.
    (Given: Molar mass of Ag = 108g mol-1, 1 F = 96500 C mol-1).
  2. Define fuel cell.
question_text: Calculate the mass of Ag deposited at cathode when a current of 2A was passed through a solution of AgNO 3 for 15 min. (Given: Molar mass of Ag = 108g mol -1 , 1 F = 96500 C mol -1 ). Define fuel cell.
explanation: Given, current (I) = 2A, time (t) = 15 min Quantity of electricity passed will be Q = It = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 1800 C Electrolysis of AgNO 3 Ag + + e {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} Ag(s) (Atomic mass of Ag = 108 g mol -1 ) As, 96500 C deposit 108 g of Ag {tex}\therefore{/tex} 1800 C will deposit = {tex}\frac{108 \times 1800}{96500}{/tex} = 2.014g of Ag Fuel cell: The galvanic cells in which chemical energy of combustion of fuels like hydrogen, methane, etc. is converted into electrical energy is called fuel cell. The overall reaction of H 2 -O 2 cell is {tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l){/tex}
solution: Given, current (I) = 2A, time (t) = 15 min Quantity of electricity passed will be Q = It = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 1800 C Electrolysis of AgNO 3 Ag + + e {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} Ag(s) (Atomic mass of Ag = 108 g mol -1 ) As, 96500 C deposit 108 g of Ag {tex}\therefore{/tex} 1800 C will deposit = {tex}\frac{108 \times 1800}{96500}{/tex} = 2.014g of Ag Fuel cell: The galvanic cells in which chemical energy of combustion of fuels like hydrogen, methane, etc. is converted into electrical energy is called fuel cell. The overall reaction of H 2 -O 2 cell is {tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l){/tex}
solution_html:
1. Given, current (I) = 2A, time (t) = 15 min
    Quantity of electricity passed will be
    Q = It = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 1800 C
    Electrolysis of AgNO3
    Ag+ + e {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} Ag(s) (Atomic mass of Ag = 108 g mol-1)
    As, 96500 C deposit 108 g of Ag
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} 1800 C will deposit = {tex}\frac{108 \times 1800}{96500}{/tex} = 2.014g of Ag
  2. Fuel cell: The galvanic cells in which chemical energy of combustion of fuels like hydrogen, methane, etc. is converted into electrical energy is called fuel cell.
    The overall reaction of H2-O2 cell is
    {tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l){/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Calculate the degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) of acetic acid if its molar conductivity ({tex}\Lambda_m{/tex}) is 39.05 S cm mol-1. Given {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 349.6 S cm mol-1 and {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^0{/tex} = 40.9 S cm2 mol-1.
question_text: Calculate the degree of dissociation ( {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) of acetic acid if its molar conductivity ( {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} ) is 39.05 S cm mol -1 . Given {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 349.6 S cm mol -1 and {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^0{/tex} = 40.9 S cm 2 mol -1 .
explanation: According to the question Given, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 39.05 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right){/tex} = 349.6 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right){/tex} = 40.9 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)=\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)+\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right){/tex} = 349.6 S cm 2 mol -1 + 40.9 S cm 2 mol -1 = 390.5 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}}=\frac{39.05 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{390.5 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.1
solution: According to the question Given, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 39.05 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right){/tex} = 349.6 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right){/tex} = 40.9 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)=\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)+\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right){/tex} = 349.6 S cm 2 mol -1 + 40.9 S cm 2 mol -1 = 390.5 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}}=\frac{39.05 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{390.5 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.1
solution_html:
According to the question
Given, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 39.05 S cm2 mol-1
{tex}\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right){/tex} = 349.6 S cm2 mol-1
{tex}\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right){/tex}= 40.9 S cm2 mol-1
{tex}\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)=\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)+\Lambda^{\circ}_\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right){/tex}
= 349.6 S cm2 mol-1 + 40.9 S cm2 mol-1
= 390.5 S cm2 mol-1
{tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}}=\frac{39.05 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{390.5 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Write the name of the cell which is generally used in hearing aids. Write the reactions taking place at the anode and the cathode of this cell.
question_text: Write the name of the cell which is generally used in hearing aids. Write the reactions taking place at the anode and the cathode of this cell.
explanation: Mercury cell is suitable for low current devices like hearing aids, watches, etc. The electrode reactions of a mercury cell can be written as At anode {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 e^{-}{/tex} At cathode {tex}\mathrm{HgO}(s)+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hg}(l)+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex} The overall reaction of the cell is written as {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})+\mathrm{HgO}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}(s)+\mathrm{Hg}(l){/tex}
solution: Mercury cell is suitable for low current devices like hearing aids, watches, etc. The electrode reactions of a mercury cell can be written as At anode {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 e^{-}{/tex} At cathode {tex}\mathrm{HgO}(s)+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hg}(l)+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex} The overall reaction of the cell is written as {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})+\mathrm{HgO}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}(s)+\mathrm{Hg}(l){/tex}
solution_html:
Mercury cell is suitable for low current devices like hearing aids, watches, etc.
The electrode reactions of a mercury cell can be written as
At anode
{tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 e^{-}{/tex}
At cathode
{tex}\mathrm{HgO}(s)+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hg}(l)+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex}
The overall reaction of the cell is written as
{tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})+\mathrm{HgO}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}(s)+\mathrm{Hg}(l){/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. The cell in which the following reaction occurs:
    2Fe3+(aq) + 2I-(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Fe2+(aq) + l2(s) has {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=0.236 \mathrm{V}{/tex} at 298 K.
    Calculate the standard Gibbs energy of the cell reaction. (Given, 1F = 96500 C mol-1)
  2. How many electrons flow through a metallic wire if a current of 0.5 A is passed for 2 h? (given, 1F = 96500 C mol-1).
question_text: The cell in which the following reaction occurs: 2Fe 3+ (aq) + 2I - (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Fe 2+ (aq) + l 2 (s) has {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=0.236 \mathrm{V}{/tex} at 298 K. Calculate the standard Gibbs energy of the cell reaction. (Given, 1F = 96500 C mol -1 ) How many electrons flow through a metallic wire if a current of 0.5 A is passed for 2 h? (given, 1F = 96500 C mol -1 ).
explanation: Standard Gibbs Free energy is given by {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} ... (i) where, n = number of moles of electrons transferred, F = Faraday's constant = 96500 C mol -1 E o cell = Cell potential Two half-reactions for the given redox reaction can be written as {tex}2 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(a q)+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(a q){/tex} {tex}2 \mathrm{I}^{-}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2}(s)+2 e^{-}{/tex} 2 moles of electrons are involved in the reaction, hence n = 2 Therefore, by substituting all the values in Eq. we get (i) {tex}\therefore \Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-(2 \mathrm{mol}) \times\left(9 \% 500 \mathrm{C} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right) \times(0.236 \mathrm{V}){/tex} = -45548 J {tex}\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-4555 \mathrm{kJ}{/tex} Given, current (I) = 0.5A, time (t) = 2h Quantity of charge (Q) passed= Current (I) {tex}\times{/tex} time (t) = (0.5A) {tex}\times{/tex} (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 60s) = 3600C Again, Q = ne - where, n = number of electrons e - = charge on electron n = {tex}\frac{Q}{e^{-}}=\frac{3600 \mathrm{C}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}}=2250 \times 10^{19}{/tex} Thus, number of electrons= 2.250 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 22 .
solution: Standard Gibbs Free energy is given by {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} ... (i) where, n = number of moles of electrons transferred, F = Faraday's constant = 96500 C mol -1 E o cell = Cell potential Two half-reactions for the given redox reaction can be written as {tex}2 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(a q)+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(a q){/tex} {tex}2 \mathrm{I}^{-}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2}(s)+2 e^{-}{/tex} 2 moles of electrons are involved in the reaction, hence n = 2 Therefore, by substituting all the values in Eq. we get (i) {tex}\therefore \Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-(2 \mathrm{mol}) \times\left(9 \% 500 \mathrm{C} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right) \times(0.236 \mathrm{V}){/tex} = -45548 J {tex}\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-4555 \mathrm{kJ}{/tex} Given, current (I) = 0.5A, time (t) = 2h Quantity of charge (Q) passed= Current (I) {tex}\times{/tex} time (t) = (0.5A) {tex}\times{/tex} (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 60s) = 3600C Again, Q = ne - where, n = number of electrons e - = charge on electron n = {tex}\frac{Q}{e^{-}}=\frac{3600 \mathrm{C}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}}=2250 \times 10^{19}{/tex} Thus, number of electrons= 2.250 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 22 .
solution_html:
1. Standard Gibbs Free energy is given by
    {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}{/tex}... (i)
    where, n = number of moles of electrons transferred, F = Faraday's constant = 96500 C mol-1 
    Eocell = Cell potential
    Two half-reactions for the given redox reaction can be written as
    {tex}2 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(a q)+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(a q){/tex}
    {tex}2 \mathrm{I}^{-}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2}(s)+2 e^{-}{/tex}
    2 moles of electrons are involved in the reaction, hence n = 2
    Therefore, by substituting all the values in Eq. we get (i)
    {tex}\therefore \Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-(2 \mathrm{mol}) \times\left(9 \% 500 \mathrm{C} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right) \times(0.236 \mathrm{V}){/tex}
    = -45548 J
    {tex}\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-4555 \mathrm{kJ}{/tex}
  2. Given, current (I) = 0.5A, time (t) = 2h Quantity of charge (Q) passed= Current (I) {tex}\times{/tex} time (t) = (0.5A) {tex}\times{/tex} (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 60s) = 3600C
    Again, Q = ne-
    where, n = number of electrons
    e- = charge on electron
    n = {tex}\frac{Q}{e^{-}}=\frac{3600 \mathrm{C}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}}=2250 \times 10^{19}{/tex}
    Thus, number of electrons= 2.250 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The electrical resistance of a column of 0.05 M KOH solution of diameter 1 cm and length 45.5 cm is 4.55 {tex}\times{/tex}103 ohm. Calculate its molar conductivity.
question_text: The electrical resistance of a column of 0.05 M KOH solution of diameter 1 cm and length 45.5 cm is 4.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 ohm. Calculate its molar conductivity.
explanation: A = {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 cm 2 = 0.785 cm 2 l = 45.5 cm G* = {tex}\frac{l}{A}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{45.5 cm}{ 0.785cm^2}{/tex} = 57.96 cm -1 {tex}\kappa{/tex} = {tex}\frac{G^*}{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{57.96 cm^{-1}}{4.55 \times 10^3 \Omega}{/tex} = 1.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 S cm {tex}\Lambda_m {/tex} = {tex}\kappa \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left[1.27 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\right] \times 1000}{0.05 \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{cm}^3}{/tex} = 254.77 S cm 2 mol -1
solution: A = {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 cm 2 = 0.785 cm 2 l = 45.5 cm G* = {tex}\frac{l}{A}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{45.5 cm}{ 0.785cm^2}{/tex} = 57.96 cm -1 {tex}\kappa{/tex} = {tex}\frac{G^*}{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{57.96 cm^{-1}}{4.55 \times 10^3 \Omega}{/tex} = 1.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 S cm {tex}\Lambda_m {/tex} = {tex}\kappa \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left[1.27 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\right] \times 1000}{0.05 \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{cm}^3}{/tex} = 254.77 S cm 2 mol -1
solution_html:
A = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2
 = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 cm2
= 0.785 cm2
l = 45.5 cm
G* = {tex}\frac{l}{A}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{45.5 cm}{ 0.785cm^2}{/tex}
= 57.96 cm-1
{tex}\kappa{/tex} = {tex}\frac{G^*}{\mathrm{R}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{57.96 cm^{-1}}{4.55 \times 10^3 \Omega}{/tex}
= 1.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 S cm
{tex}\Lambda_m {/tex} = {tex}\kappa \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\left[1.27 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\right] \times 1000}{0.05 \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{cm}^3}{/tex}
= 254.77 S cm2 mol-1

How will concentration of Zn 2+ ions and Ag + ions be affected when the cell functions?
explanation: Zinc to silver Concentration of Zn 2+ ions will increase and Ag + ions will decrease.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the emf of the following cell at 298 K.
2Cr(s) + 3Fe2+(0.1M) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr3+(0.01M) + 3Fe(s)
Given, {tex}E_{c{r^{3 + }}/cr}^o{/tex} =-0.74V, {tex}E_{F{e^{2 + }}/Fe}^o{/tex} = -0.44 V
question_text: Calculate the emf of the following cell at 298 K. 2Cr(s) + 3Fe 2+ (0.1M) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ (0.01M) + 3Fe(s) Given, {tex}E_{c{r^{3 + }}/cr}^o{/tex} =-0.74V, {tex}E_{F{e^{2 + }}/Fe}^o{/tex} = -0.44 V
explanation: Since oxidation of Cr is taking place in the given reaction, the chromium electrode is anode and as Fe is reduced in the reaction, Fe electrode is the cathode. The half-cell reactions are as follows. At anode Cr {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cr 3+ + 3e - ] {tex}\times{/tex} 2 At cathode Fe 2+ + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe] {tex}\times{/tex} 3 Overall reaction 2Cr + 3Fe 2+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ + 3Fe Eo = E cathode - E anode = -0.44 - (-0.74) = 0.3V E = E o - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{n}\log \frac{{{{[C{r^{3 + }}]}^2}}}{{{{[F{e^{2 + }}]}^3}}}{/tex} Here, n = number of electrons transferred, i.e. equal to 6. = 0.3 - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{6}\log \frac{{{{[0.01]}^2}}}{{{{[0.1]}^3}}}{/tex} = 0.309 {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.31
solution: Since oxidation of Cr is taking place in the given reaction, the chromium electrode is anode and as Fe is reduced in the reaction, Fe electrode is the cathode. The half-cell reactions are as follows. At anode Cr {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cr 3+ + 3e - ] {tex}\times{/tex} 2 At cathode Fe 2+ + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe] {tex}\times{/tex} 3 Overall reaction 2Cr + 3Fe 2+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ + 3Fe Eo = E cathode - E anode = -0.44 - (-0.74) = 0.3V E = E o - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{n}\log \frac{{{{[C{r^{3 + }}]}^2}}}{{{{[F{e^{2 + }}]}^3}}}{/tex} Here, n = number of electrons transferred, i.e. equal to 6. = 0.3 - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{6}\log \frac{{{{[0.01]}^2}}}{{{{[0.1]}^3}}}{/tex} = 0.309 {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.31
solution_html:
Since oxidation of Cr is taking place in the given reaction, the chromium electrode is anode and as Fe is reduced in the reaction, Fe electrode is the cathode. The half-cell reactions are as follows.
At anode Cr {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cr3+ + 3e-] {tex}\times{/tex} 2 
At cathode Fe2+ + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe] {tex}\times{/tex} 3 
Overall reaction 
2Cr + 3Fe2+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr3+ + 3Fe
Eo = Ecathode - Eanode = -0.44 - (-0.74) = 0.3V
E = Eo - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{n}\log \frac{{{{[C{r^{3 + }}]}^2}}}{{{{[F{e^{2 + }}]}^3}}}{/tex}
Here, n = number of electrons transferred, i.e. equal to 6.
= 0.3 - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{6}\log \frac{{{{[0.01]}^2}}}{{{{[0.1]}^3}}}{/tex}
= 0.309 {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.31

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. The conductivity of 0.001 mol L-1 solution of CH3COOH is 3.905 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 S cm-1. Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex}). Given, {tex}\lambda^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)=349.6 \mathrm{Scm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}and {tex}\lambda^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right)=40.9 \mathrm{Scm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} 
  2. Define electrochemical cell. What happens if external potential applied becomes greater than {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} of electrochemical cell?
question_text: The conductivity of 0.001 mol L -1 solution of CH 3 COOH is 3.905 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 S cm -1 . Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation ( {tex}\alpha{/tex} ). Given, {tex}\lambda^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)=349.6 \mathrm{Scm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right)=40.9 \mathrm{Scm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} Define electrochemical cell. What happens if external potential applied becomes greater than {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} of electrochemical cell?
explanation: Given: Conductivity of CH 3 COOH solution, {tex}\kappa=3.905 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} and [CH 3 COOH], M = 0.001 mol L -1 = 0.001 M Now, Molar conductivity of solution can be calculated as {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\kappa \times \frac{1000}{M}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(3.905 \times 10^{-5}) \times(1000)}{0.001 }{/tex} = 39.05 S cm 2 mol -1 Also, Molar conductivity at infinite dilution ( {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{0}{/tex} ) for CH 3 COOH = {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ} \mathrm({CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH})=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{0}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 40.9 + 349.6 = 390.5 S cm 2 mol -1 We know that, Degree of dissociation, {tex}\alpha=\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{39.05 }{390.5 }{/tex} = 0.1 i.e. 10% dissociated. Electrochemical cell: A device used to convert the chemical energy produced in a spontaneous redox reaction into electrical energy is called an electrochemical cell. Electrochemical cells are divided into two main types: (a) Electrolytic cell (b) Galvanic or voltaic cell. When external potential applied becomes greater than {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} of an electrochemical cell, electrons flow from cathode to anode, i.e. electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell.
solution: Given: Conductivity of CH 3 COOH solution, {tex}\kappa=3.905 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} and [CH 3 COOH], M = 0.001 mol L -1 = 0.001 M Now, Molar conductivity of solution can be calculated as {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\kappa \times \frac{1000}{M}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(3.905 \times 10^{-5}) \times(1000)}{0.001 }{/tex} = 39.05 S cm 2 mol -1 Also, Molar conductivity at infinite dilution ( {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{0}{/tex} ) for CH 3 COOH = {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ} \mathrm({CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH})=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{0}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 40.9 + 349.6 = 390.5 S cm 2 mol -1 We know that, Degree of dissociation, {tex}\alpha=\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{39.05 }{390.5 }{/tex} = 0.1 i.e. 10% dissociated. Electrochemical cell: A device used to convert the chemical energy produced in a spontaneous redox reaction into electrical energy is called an electrochemical cell. Electrochemical cells are divided into two main types: (a) Electrolytic cell (b) Galvanic or voltaic cell. When external potential applied becomes greater than {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} of an electrochemical cell, electrons flow from cathode to anode, i.e. electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell.
solution_html:
1. Given: Conductivity of CH3COOH solution,{tex}\kappa=3.905 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} and [CH3COOH], M = 0.001 mol L-1 = 0.001 M
    Now, Molar conductivity of solution can be calculated as {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\kappa \times \frac{1000}{M}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(3.905 \times 10^{-5}) \times(1000)}{0.001 }{/tex} = 39.05 S cm2mol-1
    Also, Molar conductivity at infinite dilution ({tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{0}{/tex}) for CH3COOH = {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ} \mathrm({CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH})=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{0}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 40.9 + 349.6 = 390.5 S cm2 mol-1
    We know that, Degree of dissociation, {tex}\alpha=\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{39.05 }{390.5 }{/tex} = 0.1 i.e. 10% dissociated.
  2. Electrochemical cell: A device used to convert the chemical energy produced in a spontaneous redox reaction into electrical energy is called an electrochemical cell. Electrochemical cells are divided into two main types: (a) Electrolytic cell (b) Galvanic or voltaic cell. When external potential applied becomes greater than {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} of an electrochemical cell, electrons flow from cathode to anode, i.e. electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Calculate {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} for the following reaction at 298 K:
    {tex}2 \mathrm{Al}(s)+3 \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}){/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}(0.01 \mathrm{M})+3 \mathrm{Cu}(s){/tex}
    Given, Ecell = 1.98 V
  2. Using the Eo values of A and B, predict which is better for coating the surface of iron
    {tex}\left[ {{E^o}\left( {\frac{{F{e^{2 + }}}}{{Fe}}} \right) = - 0.44V} \right]{/tex} to prevent corrosion and why?
    Given : {tex}{E^o}\left( {\frac{{{A^{2 + }}}}{A}} \right){/tex} = -2.37V,
    {tex}{E^o}\left( {\frac{{{B^{2 + }}}}{B}} \right){/tex} = -0.14V.
question_text: Calculate {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} for the following reaction at 298 K: {tex}2 \mathrm{Al}(s)+3 \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}){/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}(0.01 \mathrm{M})+3 \mathrm{Cu}(s){/tex} Given, E cell = 1.98 V Using the E o values of A and B, predict which is better for coating the surface of iron {tex}\left[ {{E^o}\left( {\frac{{F{e^{2 + }}}}{{Fe}}} \right) = - 0.44V} \right]{/tex} to prevent corrosion and why? Given : {tex}{E^o}\left( {\frac{{{A^{2 + }}}}{A}} \right){/tex} = -2.37V, {tex}{E^o}\left( {\frac{{{B^{2 + }}}}{B}} \right){/tex} = -0.14V.
explanation: {tex}2 \mathrm{Al}(s)+3 \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}){/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}(0.01 \mathrm{M})+3 \mathrm{Cu}(s){/tex} ApplyingNernst equation for the given cell reaction, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]^{3}}{/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}+\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]^{3}}{/tex} Here, n = 6 (n = number of electrons transferred) = {tex}1.98+\frac{0.0591}{6} \log \frac{(0.01)^{2}}{(0.01)^{3}}{/tex} = {tex}1.98+\frac{0.0591}{6} \log 100{/tex} = {tex}1.98+\frac{0.0591}{6} \times 2{/tex} {tex}\left[\log 100=\log 10^{2}=2 \log 10=2]\right.{/tex} , as log 10 = 1] = 1.9997 V {tex}\approx{/tex} 2.0 V As reduction potential of A is lower than that of iron, therefore A would be used for coating the surface of iron to prevent it from corrosion. Thisis becauselower the reduction potential of an element, more reactive is the element. Therefore,A would be more reactive than iron as well as B and hence, A protects the iron metal from corrosion.
solution: {tex}2 \mathrm{Al}(s)+3 \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}){/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}(0.01 \mathrm{M})+3 \mathrm{Cu}(s){/tex} ApplyingNernst equation for the given cell reaction, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]^{3}}{/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}+\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]^{3}}{/tex} Here, n = 6 (n = number of electrons transferred) = {tex}1.98+\frac{0.0591}{6} \log \frac{(0.01)^{2}}{(0.01)^{3}}{/tex} = {tex}1.98+\frac{0.0591}{6} \log 100{/tex} = {tex}1.98+\frac{0.0591}{6} \times 2{/tex} {tex}\left[\log 100=\log 10^{2}=2 \log 10=2]\right.{/tex} , as log 10 = 1] = 1.9997 V {tex}\approx{/tex} 2.0 V As reduction potential of A is lower than that of iron, therefore A would be used for coating the surface of iron to prevent it from corrosion. Thisis becauselower the reduction potential of an element, more reactive is the element. Therefore,A would be more reactive than iron as well as B and hence, A protects the iron metal from corrosion.
solution_html:
1. {tex}2 \mathrm{Al}(s)+3 \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}){/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}(0.01 \mathrm{M})+3 \mathrm{Cu}(s){/tex}
    ApplyingNernst equation for the given cell reaction,
    {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]^{3}}{/tex}
    {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}+\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]^{3}}{/tex}
    Here, n = 6 (n = number of electrons transferred)
    = {tex}1.98+\frac{0.0591}{6} \log \frac{(0.01)^{2}}{(0.01)^{3}}{/tex}
    = {tex}1.98+\frac{0.0591}{6} \log 100{/tex}
    = {tex}1.98+\frac{0.0591}{6} \times 2{/tex}
    {tex}\left[\log 100=\log 10^{2}=2 \log 10=2]\right.{/tex}, as log 10 = 1]
    = 1.9997 V {tex}\approx{/tex} 2.0 V
  2. As reduction potential of A is lower than that of iron, therefore A would be used for coating the surface of iron to prevent it from corrosion. Thisis becauselower the reduction potential of an element, more reactive is the element. Therefore,A would be more reactive than iron as well as B and hence, A protects the iron metal from corrosion.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the emf of the cell Mg(s) {tex}\|{/tex} Mg2+(0.1 M) || Cu2+ (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M) | Cu(s)
Given : E0(Cu2+/Cu) = +0.34V
E0(Mg2+/Mg) = -2.37V
question_text: Calculate the emf of the cell Mg(s) {tex}\|{/tex} Mg 2+ (0.1 M) || Cu 2+ (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 M) | Cu(s) Given : E 0 (Cu 2+ /Cu) = +0.34V E 0 (Mg 2+ /Mg) = -2.37V
solution_html:
{tex}{E_{cell}} = E_{cell}^0 - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{{\left[ {M{g^{2 + }}} \right]}}{{\left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]}}{/tex}

{tex}E_{cell}^0 = \left[ {{E^0}\frac{(C{u^{2 + }})}{(Cu)} - {E^0}(M{g^{2 + }}/Mg)} \right]{/tex}
{tex}{E_{cell}} = \left[ { + 0.34V - \left( { - 2.37V} \right)} \right] - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log {10^2}{/tex}
= (0.271 V - 0.0591)V
= 2.65 V

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Write the cell formulation and calculate the standard cell potential of the galvanic cell in which the following reaction takes place:
Fe2+(aq) + Ag+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe3+(aq) + Ag(s)
Calculate {tex}{\Delta _r}{G^0}{/tex} for the above reaction
[Given: {tex}E_{A{g^ + }/Ag}^0 = + 0.80V{/tex}
{tex}E_{F{e^{3 + }}/Fe}^0 = + 0.77V{/tex}
[1 F = 96500 C mol-1]
question_text: Write the cell formulation and calculate the standard cell potential of the galvanic cell in which the following reaction takes place: Fe 2+ (aq) + Ag + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe 3+ (aq) + Ag(s) Calculate {tex}{\Delta _r}{G^0}{/tex} for the above reaction [Given: {tex}E_{A{g^ + }/Ag}^0 = + 0.80V{/tex} {tex}E_{F{e^{3 + }}/Fe}^0 = + 0.77V{/tex} [1 F = 96500 C mol -1 ]
solution_html:
{tex}E_{cell}^0 = E_{A{g^ + }/Ag}^0 - E_{F{e^{3 + }}/Fe}^0{/tex}
= +0.80 V - (+0.77) V
= 0.03 V
{tex}{\Delta _r}{G^0} = - nF{E^o}{/tex}
{tex} = - 2 \times 0.03V \times 96500{/tex}
= -5790 J mol-1
= 57.90 KJ mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the emf of the following cell at 25oC.
Zn | Zn2+ (0.001 M) || H+ (0.01 M) | H2(g) (1 bar) | Pt(s)
Eo(Zn2+/Zn) = -0.76 V, {tex}E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^o{/tex} = 0.00V
question_text: Calculate the emf of the following cell at 25 o C. Zn | Zn 2+ (0.001 M) || H + (0.01 M) | H 2 (g) (1 bar) | Pt(s) E o (Zn 2+ /Zn) = -0.76 V, {tex}E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^o{/tex} = 0.00V
explanation: At anode Zn(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + 2e - At cathode [H + (aq) + e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H 2 (g)] {tex}\times{/tex} 2 Net equation Zn(s) + 2H + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + H 2 (g) E cell = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} E cell = {tex}0.76-\frac{0.0591}{2}=0.731 \mathrm{V}{/tex} .
solution: At anode Zn(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + 2e - At cathode [H + (aq) + e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H 2 (g)] {tex}\times{/tex} 2 Net equation Zn(s) + 2H + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + H 2 (g) E cell = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} E cell = {tex}0.76-\frac{0.0591}{2}=0.731 \mathrm{V}{/tex} .
solution_html:
At anode Zn(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+(aq) + 2e-
At cathode [H+ (aq) + e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2(g)] {tex}\times{/tex} 2
Net equation
Zn(s) + 2H+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+(aq) + H2(g)
Ecell = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
Ecell = {tex}0.76-\frac{0.0591}{2}=0.731 \mathrm{V}{/tex}.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Conductivity of 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M methanoic acid is 5.25{tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 S cm-1, Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation.
Given: {tex}\lambda^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right){/tex} = 349.5 S cm2mol-1 and {tex}\lambda^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H} \operatorname{COO}^{-}\right){/tex}= 50.5 S cm2mol-1.
question_text: Conductivity of 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 M methanoic acid is 5.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 S cm -1 , Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation. Given: {tex}\lambda^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right){/tex} = 349.5 S cm 2 mol -1 and {tex}\lambda^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H} \operatorname{COO}^{-}\right){/tex} = 50.5 S cm 2 mol -1 .
explanation: Molar conductivity of 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 M solution is {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{C}=\frac{\kappa \times 1000}{M}=\frac{5.25 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{C}=210 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}(\mathrm{HCOOH})=\lambda_{\left(\mathrm{HCOO}^{-}\right)}^{0}+\lambda_{\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{0}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}{/tex} = 50.0 + 349.5 {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}{/tex} = 399.5 {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 400 cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{c}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{210}{400}{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.525
solution: Molar conductivity of 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 M solution is {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{C}=\frac{\kappa \times 1000}{M}=\frac{5.25 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{C}=210 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}(\mathrm{HCOOH})=\lambda_{\left(\mathrm{HCOO}^{-}\right)}^{0}+\lambda_{\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{0}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}{/tex} = 50.0 + 349.5 {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}{/tex} = 399.5 {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 400 cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{c}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{210}{400}{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.525
solution_html:
Molar conductivity of 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 M solution is
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{C}=\frac{\kappa \times 1000}{M}=\frac{5.25 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{C}=210 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}(\mathrm{HCOOH})=\lambda_{\left(\mathrm{HCOO}^{-}\right)}^{0}+\lambda_{\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{0}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}{/tex} = 50.0 + 349.5
{tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}{/tex} = 399.5
{tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}{/tex} {tex}\approx{/tex} 400 cm2 mol-1
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{c}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{210}{400}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.525

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the time to deposit 1.27 g of copper at cathode when a current of 2A was passed through the solution of CuSO4.
(Molar mass of Cu= 63.5g mol-1, 1F = 96500 C mol-1)
question_text: Calculate the time to deposit 1.27 g of copper at cathode when a current of 2A was passed through the solution of CuSO 4 . (Molar mass of Cu= 63.5g mol -1 , 1F = 96500 C mol -1 )
explanation: Given, m = 1.27g. I = 2A Molarmass of Cu= 63.5g mol -1 , Z = {tex}\frac{\text { Equivalent weight }}{F}{/tex} F = 96500 C mol -1 m = Z {tex}\times{/tex} I {tex}\times{/tex} t t = {tex}\frac{m}{Z \times I}=\frac{1.27 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.5 \times 2}{/tex} t = 1930 s = 32.1 min
solution: Given, m = 1.27g. I = 2A Molarmass of Cu= 63.5g mol -1 , Z = {tex}\frac{\text { Equivalent weight }}{F}{/tex} F = 96500 C mol -1 m = Z {tex}\times{/tex} I {tex}\times{/tex} t t = {tex}\frac{m}{Z \times I}=\frac{1.27 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.5 \times 2}{/tex} t = 1930 s = 32.1 min
solution_html:
Given, m = 1.27g. I = 2A
Molarmass of Cu= 63.5g mol-1,
Z = {tex}\frac{\text { Equivalent weight }}{F}{/tex}
F = 96500 C mol-1
m = Z {tex}\times{/tex} I {tex}\times{/tex} t
t = {tex}\frac{m}{Z \times I}=\frac{1.27 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.5 \times 2}{/tex}
t = 1930 s
= 32.1 min

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
State the following laws:

  1. Faraday's first law of electrolysis.
  2. Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions.
question_text: State the following laws: Faraday's first law of electrolysis. Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions.
explanation: Faraday's first law of electrolysis: The amount of substance deposited during electrolysis is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed. {tex}m \propto Q{/tex} , {tex}m \propto It{/tex} , m = ZIt, where Z is electrochemical equivalent, I is current in ampere, t is a time in seconds and Q is charge in coulomb. Electrochemical equivalent (Z) It is the amount of substance deposited in grams when 1-ampere current is passed for 1 second, i.e. when 1 coulomb of charge is passed. Its Unit is g C -1 . Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions states that the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be expressed as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and the cation of the electrolyte, e.g. {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)}^{\circ}=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex} The conductivity of a solution is related to the number of ions present per unit volume of the solution. When the solution is diluted, the number of ions per unit volume decreases Hence, conductivity or specific conductance of the solution decreases.
solution: Faraday's first law of electrolysis: The amount of substance deposited during electrolysis is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed. {tex}m \propto Q{/tex} , {tex}m \propto It{/tex} , m = ZIt, where Z is electrochemical equivalent, I is current in ampere, t is a time in seconds and Q is charge in coulomb. Electrochemical equivalent (Z) It is the amount of substance deposited in grams when 1-ampere current is passed for 1 second, i.e. when 1 coulomb of charge is passed. Its Unit is g C -1 . Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions states that the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be expressed as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and the cation of the electrolyte, e.g. {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)}^{\circ}=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex} The conductivity of a solution is related to the number of ions present per unit volume of the solution. When the solution is diluted, the number of ions per unit volume decreases Hence, conductivity or specific conductance of the solution decreases.
solution_html:
1. Faraday's first law of electrolysis: The amount of substance deposited during electrolysis is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed.
    {tex}m \propto Q{/tex}, {tex}m \propto It{/tex}, m = ZIt,
    where Z is electrochemical equivalent, I is current in ampere, t is a time in seconds and Q is charge in coulomb.
    Electrochemical equivalent (Z) It is the amount of substance deposited in grams when 1-ampere current is passed for 1 second, i.e. when 1 coulomb of charge is passed. Its Unit is g C-1.
  2. Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions states that the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be expressed as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and the cation of the electrolyte, e.g.
    {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)}^{\circ}=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex}
    The conductivity of a solution is related to the number of ions present per unit volume of the solution. When the solution is diluted, the number of ions per unit volume decreases Hence, conductivity or specific conductance of the solution decreases.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Define the following terms:

  1. Molar conductivity (Am)
  2. Secondary batteries
  3. Fuel cell
question_text: Define the following terms: Molar conductivity (Am) Secondary batteries Fuel cell
explanation: Molar Conductivity is defined as the conductance of the solution which contains one mole of the electrolyte such that entire solution is in between the two electrodes kept one centimetre apart, and large enough to contain all the electrolytes. Molar conductivity, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} ,= {tex}\frac{k}{C}{/tex} . Secondary batteries After use, they can be recharged by passing current through it in the opposite direction so that it can be used again. The secondary cell undergoes a large number of discharging and charging cycles. The most important secondary cell is the lead storage battery. Other example includes nickel-cadmium cell. Nickel-cadmium cell is a secondary cell. It has a longer life than the lead storage cell but more expensive to manufacture. The overall reaction during discharging is Cd(s) + 2Ni(OH) 3 (s) CdO(s) + 2Ni(OH) 2 (s) + H2O(l)
solution: Molar Conductivity is defined as the conductance of the solution which contains one mole of the electrolyte such that entire solution is in between the two electrodes kept one centimetre apart, and large enough to contain all the electrolytes. Molar conductivity, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} ,= {tex}\frac{k}{C}{/tex} . Secondary batteries After use, they can be recharged by passing current through it in the opposite direction so that it can be used again. The secondary cell undergoes a large number of discharging and charging cycles. The most important secondary cell is the lead storage battery. Other example includes nickel-cadmium cell. Nickel-cadmium cell is a secondary cell. It has a longer life than the lead storage cell but more expensive to manufacture. The overall reaction during discharging is Cd(s) + 2Ni(OH) 3 (s) CdO(s) + 2Ni(OH) 2 (s) + H2O(l)
solution_html:
1. Molar Conductivity is defined as the conductance of the solution which contains one mole of the electrolyte such that entire solution is in between the two electrodes kept one centimetre apart, and large enough to contain all the electrolytes.
    Molar conductivity, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex},= {tex}\frac{k}{C}{/tex}.
  2. Secondary batteries After use, they can be recharged by passing current through it in the opposite direction so that it can be used again. The secondary cell undergoes a large number of discharging and charging cycles. The most important secondary cell is the lead storage battery. Other example includes nickel-cadmium cell.
  3. Nickel-cadmium cell is a secondary cell. It has a longer life than the lead storage cell but more expensive to manufacture. The overall reaction during discharging is 
    Cd(s) + 2Ni(OH)3(s)  CdO(s) + 2Ni(OH)2(s) + H2O(l)

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the {tex}\Delta_rG^o{/tex} and emf (E) that can be obtained from the following cell under the standard conditions at 25oC.
{tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q) \| \mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q)\right| \operatorname{Sn}(s){/tex}
[Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V; {tex}E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{s}_{\mathrm{n}}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.14 V and F = 96500 Cmol-1].
question_text: Calculate the {tex}\Delta_rG^o{/tex} and emf (E) that can be obtained from the following cell under the standard conditions at 25 o C. {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q) \| \mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q)\right| \operatorname{Sn}(s){/tex} [Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V; {tex}E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{s}_{\mathrm{n}}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.14 V and F = 96500 Cmol -1 ].
explanation: At anode: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-}{/tex} At cathode: {tex}\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Sn}(s){/tex} Overall reaction: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)+\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)+\mathrm{Sn}(s){/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Sn}}^{\circ}-\left(-E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{0}\right){/tex} = -0.14 + 0.76 = 0.62 V {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}=-2 \times 96500 \times 0.62{/tex} {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-119660\; \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution: At anode: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-}{/tex} At cathode: {tex}\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Sn}(s){/tex} Overall reaction: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)+\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)+\mathrm{Sn}(s){/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Sn}}^{\circ}-\left(-E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{0}\right){/tex} = -0.14 + 0.76 = 0.62 V {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}=-2 \times 96500 \times 0.62{/tex} {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-119660\; \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
At anode: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-}{/tex}
At cathode: {tex}\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Sn}(s){/tex}
Overall reaction:
{tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)+\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)+\mathrm{Sn}(s){/tex}
{tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Sn}}^{\circ}-\left(-E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{0}\right){/tex}
= -0.14 + 0.76 = 0.62 V
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}=-2 \times 96500 \times 0.62{/tex}
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-119660\; \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the emf of the following cell at 25°C.
{tex}\text { Fe } | \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(0.001 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{H}^{+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}){/tex}{tex}| \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{g})(1 \text { bar }) | \mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{s}){/tex}
Given that, {tex}E_{F{e^{2 + }}/Fe}^o{/tex}= -0.44 V; {tex}E_{H{^{ + }}/H_2}^o{/tex}= 0.00 V
question_text: Calculate the emf of the following cell at 25°C. {tex}\text { Fe } | \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(0.001 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{H}^{+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}){/tex} {tex}| \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{g})(1 \text { bar }) | \mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{s}){/tex} Given that, {tex}E_{F{e^{2 + }}/Fe}^o{/tex} = -0.44 V; {tex}E_{H{^{ + }}/H_2}^o{/tex} = 0.00 V
explanation: At anode, Fe(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe 2+ (aq) + 2e - At cathode, [H + (aq) + e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} H 2 (g)] {tex}\times{/tex} 2 Overall reaction: Fe(s) + 2H + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe 2+ (aq) + H 2 (g) Given, {tex}E_{F{e^{2 + }}/Fe}^o{/tex} = -0.44 V and {tex}E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^o{/tex} = 0.00 V E o (cell) = E o (cathode) - E o (anode) = [0.00- (-0.44)] = 0.44V Now, Applying Nernst equation: E cell = {tex}E_{cell}^o{/tex} - {tex}\frac{{0.00591}}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{{[F{e^{2 + }}]}}{{{{[{H^ + }]}^2}}}{/tex} = 0.44 - {tex}\frac{{0.00591}}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{{{{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{{{({{10}^{ - 2}})}^2}}}{/tex} = 0.44 - {tex}\frac{{0.00591}}{2}{/tex} = 0.4104 V
solution: At anode, Fe(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe 2+ (aq) + 2e - At cathode, [H + (aq) + e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} H 2 (g)] {tex}\times{/tex} 2 Overall reaction: Fe(s) + 2H + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe 2+ (aq) + H 2 (g) Given, {tex}E_{F{e^{2 + }}/Fe}^o{/tex} = -0.44 V and {tex}E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^o{/tex} = 0.00 V E o (cell) = E o (cathode) - E o (anode) = [0.00- (-0.44)] = 0.44V Now, Applying Nernst equation: E cell = {tex}E_{cell}^o{/tex} - {tex}\frac{{0.00591}}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{{[F{e^{2 + }}]}}{{{{[{H^ + }]}^2}}}{/tex} = 0.44 - {tex}\frac{{0.00591}}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{{{{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{{{({{10}^{ - 2}})}^2}}}{/tex} = 0.44 - {tex}\frac{{0.00591}}{2}{/tex} = 0.4104 V
solution_html:
At anode, Fe(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe2+(aq) + 2e- 
At cathode, [H+ (aq) + e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}H2(g)] {tex}\times{/tex} 2 
Overall reaction:
Fe(s) + 2H+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe2+(aq) + H2(g)
Given, {tex}E_{F{e^{2 + }}/Fe}^o{/tex} = -0.44 V and {tex}E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^o{/tex} = 0.00 V
Eo(cell) = Eo(cathode) - Eo(anode) = [0.00- (-0.44)] = 0.44V
Now, Applying Nernst equation:
Ecell = {tex}E_{cell}^o{/tex} - {tex}\frac{{0.00591}}{2}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{{[F{e^{2 + }}]}}{{{{[{H^ + }]}^2}}}{/tex}= 0.44 - {tex}\frac{{0.00591}}{2}{/tex} log{tex}\frac{{{{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{{{({{10}^{ - 2}})}^2}}}{/tex} 
= 0.44 - {tex}\frac{{0.00591}}{2}{/tex} = 0.4104 V

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Define the term degree of dissociation. Write an expression that relates the molar conductivity of a weak electrolyte to its degree of dissociation.
  2. For the cell reaction,
    {tex}\mathrm{Ni}(s)\left|\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(a q) \| \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q)\right| \mathrm{Ag}(s){/tex} 
    Calculate the equilibrium constant at 25oC. How much maximum work would be obtained by operation of this cell? {tex}E_{\mathrm{N}_{1}^{2+} / \mathrm{N}_{1}}^{\circ}=0.25 \mathrm{V}{/tex} and {tex}E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}=0.80 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
question_text: Define the term degree of dissociation. Write an expression that relates the molar conductivity of a weak electrolyte to its degree of dissociation. For the cell reaction, {tex}\mathrm{Ni}(s)\left|\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(a q) \| \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q)\right| \mathrm{Ag}(s){/tex} Calculate the equilibrium constant at 25 o C. How much maximum work would be obtained by operation of this cell? {tex}E_{\mathrm{N}_{1}^{2+} / \mathrm{N}_{1}}^{\circ}=0.25 \mathrm{V}{/tex} and {tex}E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}=0.80 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
explanation: Degree of dissociation: The ratio of molar conductivity {tex}\left(\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\right){/tex} at a specific concentration C to the molar conductivity at infinite dilution, i.e. limiting molar conductivity, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} , is known as the degree of dissociation. At any concentration C, if {tex}\alpha{/tex} is the degree of dissociation, then for a weak electrolyte, {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}{/tex} Oxidation half cell reaction: {tex}\mathrm{Ni}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-}{/tex} (At anode) Reduction half cell reaction: {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}(s)\right] \times 2{/tex} (At cathode) Overall cell reaction: {tex}\mathrm{Ni}(s)+2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{Ag}(s){/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{Ni}^{2+} / \mathrm{N} 1}^{\circ}=0.80-0.25{/tex} = 0.55 According to the Nernst equation, {tex}E_{cell}\;={E^o}_{cell}\;-\frac{0.0591}n\;\log\;Q{/tex} at 25 o C At equilibrium, E cell = 0 and Q = K Equilibrium constant(K) = {tex}\text {antilog }\left(\frac{n E^{\circ}}{0.0591}\right)=\text { antilog }\left(2 \times \frac{0.55}{0.0591}\right){/tex} Thus, K = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 18 Therefore, Maximum work {tex}\left(\Delta G^{\circ}\right){/tex} = -nFE o = {tex}-2 \times 96500 \times 0.55=-106150\; \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution: Degree of dissociation: The ratio of molar conductivity {tex}\left(\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\right){/tex} at a specific concentration C to the molar conductivity at infinite dilution, i.e. limiting molar conductivity, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} , is known as the degree of dissociation. At any concentration C, if {tex}\alpha{/tex} is the degree of dissociation, then for a weak electrolyte, {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}{/tex} Oxidation half cell reaction: {tex}\mathrm{Ni}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-}{/tex} (At anode) Reduction half cell reaction: {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}(s)\right] \times 2{/tex} (At cathode) Overall cell reaction: {tex}\mathrm{Ni}(s)+2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{Ag}(s){/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{Ni}^{2+} / \mathrm{N} 1}^{\circ}=0.80-0.25{/tex} = 0.55 According to the Nernst equation, {tex}E_{cell}\;={E^o}_{cell}\;-\frac{0.0591}n\;\log\;Q{/tex} at 25 o C At equilibrium, E cell = 0 and Q = K Equilibrium constant(K) = {tex}\text {antilog }\left(\frac{n E^{\circ}}{0.0591}\right)=\text { antilog }\left(2 \times \frac{0.55}{0.0591}\right){/tex} Thus, K = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 18 Therefore, Maximum work {tex}\left(\Delta G^{\circ}\right){/tex} = -nFE o = {tex}-2 \times 96500 \times 0.55=-106150\; \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Degree of dissociation: The ratio of molar conductivity {tex}\left(\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\right){/tex} at a specific concentration C to the molar conductivity at infinite dilution, i.e. limiting molar conductivity, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex}, is known as the degree of dissociation. At any concentration C, if {tex}\alpha{/tex} is the degree of dissociation, then for a weak electrolyte,
    {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}{/tex}
  2. Oxidation half cell reaction: {tex}\mathrm{Ni}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-}{/tex} (At anode)
    Reduction half cell reaction: {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}(s)\right] \times 2{/tex} (At cathode)
    Overall cell reaction: {tex}\mathrm{Ni}(s)+2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{Ag}(s){/tex}
    {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{Ni}^{2+} / \mathrm{N} 1}^{\circ}=0.80-0.25{/tex} = 0.55
    According to the Nernst equation,
    {tex}E_{cell}\;={E^o}_{cell}\;-\frac{0.0591}n\;\log\;Q{/tex} at 25oC
    At equilibrium, Ecell = 0 and Q = K
    Equilibrium constant(K) = {tex}\text {antilog }\left(\frac{n E^{\circ}}{0.0591}\right)=\text { antilog }\left(2 \times \frac{0.55}{0.0591}\right){/tex}
    Thus, K = 4 {tex}\times{/tex}1018
    Therefore, Maximum work {tex}\left(\Delta G^{\circ}\right){/tex} = -nFEo = {tex}-2 \times 96500 \times 0.55=-106150\; \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Following reactions occur at cathode during the electrolysis of aqueous silver chloride solution:
    {tex}\mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}(s) ; E^{\circ}=+0.80 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) ; E^{\circ}=0.00 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
    On the basis of their standard reduction electrode potential (E0) values, which reaction is feasible at the cathode and why?
  2. Define limiting molar conductivity. Why conductivity of an electrolyte solution decreases with the decrease in concentration?
question_text: Following reactions occur at cathode during the electrolysis of aqueous silver chloride solution: {tex}\mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}(s) ; E^{\circ}=+0.80 \mathrm{V}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) ; E^{\circ}=0.00 \mathrm{V}{/tex} On the basis of their standard reduction electrode potential (E0) values, which reaction is feasible at the cathode and why? Define limiting molar conductivity. Why conductivity of an electrolyte solution decreases with the decrease in concentration?
explanation: Electrolysisof an aqueous solution of AgCl {tex}\operatorname{AgCl}(a q) \rightleftharpoons \operatorname{Ag}^{+}(a q)+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(a q){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q){/tex} On passing electricity, Ag + (aq) and H +( aq) move towards the cathode. Since the discharge potential of Ag + ion is lower than that H + ions. In other words, Ag + ions have more value of standard reduction potential Therefore, Ag + ions are discharged in preference of H + ions. At cathode {tex}\mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}(s){/tex} At anode {tex}\mathrm{Cl}^{-}-e^{-} \longrightarrow^{\cdot} \mathrm{Cl}{/tex} {tex} \mathrm{Cl}+ \mathrm{Cl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(g){/tex} Hence,reduction of Ag + (ion) is feasible at the cathode. Limiting molar conductivity: The limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is defined as its molar conductivity when the concentration of the electrolyte in the solution approaches zero. The conductivity of an electrolyte solution decreases with the decrease in concentration due to the fact that the number of ions per unit volume that carry the current in a solution decreases on dilution.
solution: Electrolysisof an aqueous solution of AgCl {tex}\operatorname{AgCl}(a q) \rightleftharpoons \operatorname{Ag}^{+}(a q)+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(a q){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q){/tex} On passing electricity, Ag + (aq) and H +( aq) move towards the cathode. Since the discharge potential of Ag + ion is lower than that H + ions. In other words, Ag + ions have more value of standard reduction potential Therefore, Ag + ions are discharged in preference of H + ions. At cathode {tex}\mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}(s){/tex} At anode {tex}\mathrm{Cl}^{-}-e^{-} \longrightarrow^{\cdot} \mathrm{Cl}{/tex} {tex} \mathrm{Cl}+ \mathrm{Cl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(g){/tex} Hence,reduction of Ag + (ion) is feasible at the cathode. Limiting molar conductivity: The limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is defined as its molar conductivity when the concentration of the electrolyte in the solution approaches zero. The conductivity of an electrolyte solution decreases with the decrease in concentration due to the fact that the number of ions per unit volume that carry the current in a solution decreases on dilution.
solution_html:
1. Electrolysisof an aqueous solution of AgCl
    {tex}\operatorname{AgCl}(a q) \rightleftharpoons \operatorname{Ag}^{+}(a q)+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(a q){/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q){/tex}
    On passing electricity, Ag+(aq) and H+(aq) move towards the cathode. Since the discharge potential of Ag+ ion is lower than that H+ ions. In other words, Ag+ ions have more value of standard reduction potential Therefore, Ag+ ions are discharged in preference of H+ions.
    At cathode {tex}\mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}(s){/tex}
    At anode     {tex}\mathrm{Cl}^{-}-e^{-} \longrightarrow^{\cdot} \mathrm{Cl}{/tex}
    {tex} \mathrm{Cl}+ \mathrm{Cl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(g){/tex}
    Hence,reduction of Ag+(ion) is feasible at the cathode.
  2. Limiting molar conductivity: The limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is defined as its molar conductivity when the concentration of the electrolyte in the solution approaches zero.
    The conductivity of an electrolyte solution decreases with the decrease in concentration due to the fact that the number of ions per unit volume that carry the current in a solution decreases on dilution.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Following reactions occur at cathode during the electrolysis of aqueous copper (II) chloride solution.
    Cu2+ (aq) + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu(s), Eo = + 0.34 V
    H+(aq) + e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(g){/tex}, Eo = 0.00 V
    On the basis of their standard reduction electrode potential (Eo) values, which reaction is feasible at the cathode and why?
  2. State Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions. Write its one application.
question_text: Following reactions occur at cathode during the electrolysis of aqueous copper (II) chloride solution. Cu 2+ (aq) + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu(s), E o = + 0.34 V H + (aq) + e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(g){/tex} , E o = 0.00 V On the basis of their standard reduction electrode potential (E o ) values, which reaction is feasible at the cathode and why? State Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions. Write its one application.
explanation: Electrolysis of aqueous solution of CuCl 2 . {tex}\mathrm{CuCl}_{2}(a q) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(a q){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q){/tex} On passing electricity, Cu2+(aq) and H + (aq) move towards the cathode. Since the discharge potential of Cu 2+ ion is lower than that of H + ions, or Cu 2+ ion have more value of standard reduction potential. Therefore, Cu 2+ ions are discharged in the presence of H + ions Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions states that the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be expressed as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and the cation of the electrolyte, e.g. {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)}^{\circ}=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex} The conductivity of a solution is related with the number of ions present per unit volume of the solution. When the solution is diluted, the number of ions per unit volume decreases Hence, conductivity or specific conductance of the solution decreases. Application Using this law, it is possible to calculate {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} for weak electrolyte from the {tex}\lambda^{0}{/tex} of individual ions at infinite dilution.
solution: Electrolysis of aqueous solution of CuCl 2 . {tex}\mathrm{CuCl}_{2}(a q) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(a q){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q){/tex} On passing electricity, Cu2+(aq) and H + (aq) move towards the cathode. Since the discharge potential of Cu 2+ ion is lower than that of H + ions, or Cu 2+ ion have more value of standard reduction potential. Therefore, Cu 2+ ions are discharged in the presence of H + ions Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions states that the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be expressed as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and the cation of the electrolyte, e.g. {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)}^{\circ}=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex} The conductivity of a solution is related with the number of ions present per unit volume of the solution. When the solution is diluted, the number of ions per unit volume decreases Hence, conductivity or specific conductance of the solution decreases. Application Using this law, it is possible to calculate {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} for weak electrolyte from the {tex}\lambda^{0}{/tex} of individual ions at infinite dilution.
solution_html:
1. Electrolysis of aqueous solution of CuCl2.
    {tex}\mathrm{CuCl}_{2}(a q) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(a q){/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q){/tex}
    On passing electricity, Cu2+(aq) and H+ (aq) move towards the cathode. Since the discharge potential of Cu2+ ion is lower than that of H+ ions, or Cu2+ ion have more value of standard reduction potential. Therefore, Cu2+ ions are discharged in the presence of H+ ions
  2. Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions states that the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be expressed as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and the cation of the electrolyte, e.g.
    {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)}^{\circ}=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex}
    The conductivity of a solution is related with the number of ions present per unit volume of the solution. When the solution is diluted, the number of ions per unit volume decreases Hence, conductivity or specific conductance of the solution decreases.
    Application Using this law, it is possible to calculate {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} for weak electrolyte from the {tex}\lambda^{0}{/tex} of individual ions at infinite dilution.

explanation: {tex}\Lambda _ { m } = \frac { 1 } { R } \times \frac { l } { A } \times \frac { 1000 } { M } = \frac { 1 } { 200 } \times 1 \times \frac { 1000 } { 0.01 } = 500 \mathrm { S } \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { mol } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}

question_text: A solution of Ni(NO 3 ) 2 is electrolysed between platinum electrodes using a current of 5 amperes for 20 minutes. What mass of Ni is deposited at the cathode?
explanation: given that Quantity of electricity passed {tex} = 5A \times 20 \times 60s{/tex} = 6000 C time = 20 min {tex}N{i^{2 + }} + 2{e^ - } \to Ni{/tex} Thus, 2 F, i.e. {tex}2 \times 96500\,C{/tex} deposit Ni = 1 mole i.e. 58.7 g (at mass of Ni = 58.7) Thus 2 F i.e. {tex}2 \times 96500\,C{/tex} deposit Ni = 1 mole 6000 C will deposit Ni {tex} = \frac{{58.7}}{{2 \times 96500}} \times 6000g{/tex} = 1.825 g
