question_html:
1. Write two advantages of H2 - O2 fuel cell over ordinary cell.
  2. Equilibrium constant (Kc) for the given cell reaction is 10. Calculate {tex}\boldsymbol{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex}.
    {tex}A(s)+B^{2+}(a q) \rightleftharpoons A^{2+}(a q)+B(s){/tex}
question_text: Write two advantages of H 2 - O 2 fuel cell over ordinary cell. Equilibrium constant (K c ) for the given cell reaction is 10. Calculate {tex}\boldsymbol{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} . {tex}A(s)+B^{2+}(a q) \rightleftharpoons A^{2+}(a q)+B(s){/tex}
explanation: Advantages of fuel cells: Fuel cells are highly efficient. These cells produce electricity with an efficiency of about 70% compared to the thermal plants whose efficiency is about 40%. Because of the continuous supply of fuels, these cells never become dead. These cells are usually operated at a temperature of 70-140 o C and give a potential of about 0.9V. Fuel cells do not cause any pollution, unlike thermal plant which burns fossil fuels like coal gas, oil, etc. The reaction {tex}A(s)+B^{2+}(a q) \rightleftharpoons A^{2+}(a q)+B(s){/tex} By using the Nernst equation {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{\left[A^{2+}\right]}{\left[B^{2+}\right]}{/tex} At equilibrium, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = 0, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.059}{n} \log K_{C}{/tex} K c = 10 and n = 2 (Given) {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.059}{2} \log 10{/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} = 0.0295
solution: Advantages of fuel cells: Fuel cells are highly efficient. These cells produce electricity with an efficiency of about 70% compared to the thermal plants whose efficiency is about 40%. Because of the continuous supply of fuels, these cells never become dead. These cells are usually operated at a temperature of 70-140 o C and give a potential of about 0.9V. Fuel cells do not cause any pollution, unlike thermal plant which burns fossil fuels like coal gas, oil, etc. The reaction {tex}A(s)+B^{2+}(a q) \rightleftharpoons A^{2+}(a q)+B(s){/tex} By using the Nernst equation {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{\left[A^{2+}\right]}{\left[B^{2+}\right]}{/tex} At equilibrium, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = 0, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.059}{n} \log K_{C}{/tex} K c = 10 and n = 2 (Given) {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.059}{2} \log 10{/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} = 0.0295
solution_html:
1. Advantages of fuel cells:
    1. Fuel cells are highly efficient. These cells produce electricity with an efficiency of about 70% compared to the thermal plants whose efficiency is about 40%.
    2. Because of the continuous supply of fuels, these cells never become dead. These cells are usually operated at a temperature of 70-140oC and give a potential of about 0.9V.
    3. Fuel cells do not cause any pollution, unlike thermal plant which burns fossil fuels like coal gas, oil, etc.
  2. The reaction
    {tex}A(s)+B^{2+}(a q) \rightleftharpoons A^{2+}(a q)+B(s){/tex}
    By using the Nernst equation
    {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{\left[A^{2+}\right]}{\left[B^{2+}\right]}{/tex}
    At equilibrium, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = 0,
    {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.059}{n} \log K_{C}{/tex}
    Kc = 10 and n = 2 (Given)
    {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.059}{2} \log 10{/tex}
    {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} = 0.0295

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Set-up Nernst equation for the standard dry cell. Using this equation show that the voltage of a dry cell decreases with use.
question_text: Set-up Nernst equation for the standard dry cell. Using this equation show that the voltage of a dry cell decreases with use.
explanation: Dry cell (Leclanche cell) consists of a zinc container which acts as anode and cathode is a carbon (graphite) rod surrounded by powdered manganese dioxide and carbon. The space between the electrodes is filled by a moist paste of NH 4 Cl and ZnCl 2 . The cell is sealed with a material like wax. Overall reaction: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)+2 \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{MnO}_{2}(s){/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{2+}(a q)+\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(s)+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l){/tex} By using Nernst equation, E cell = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{2.303 R T}{n F} \ln \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{2+}}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} Due to the presence of ions in the overall reaction, its voltage decreases with time.
solution: Dry cell (Leclanche cell) consists of a zinc container which acts as anode and cathode is a carbon (graphite) rod surrounded by powdered manganese dioxide and carbon. The space between the electrodes is filled by a moist paste of NH 4 Cl and ZnCl 2 . The cell is sealed with a material like wax. Overall reaction: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)+2 \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{MnO}_{2}(s){/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{2+}(a q)+\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(s)+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l){/tex} By using Nernst equation, E cell = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{2.303 R T}{n F} \ln \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{2+}}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} Due to the presence of ions in the overall reaction, its voltage decreases with time.
solution_html:
Dry cell (Leclanche cell) consists of a zinc container which acts as anode and cathode is a carbon (graphite) rod surrounded by powdered manganese dioxide and carbon. The space between the electrodes is filled by a moist paste of NH4Cl and ZnCl2. The cell is sealed with a material like wax.
Overall reaction:
{tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)+2 \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{MnO}_{2}(s){/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{2+}(a q)+\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(s)+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l){/tex}
By using Nernst equation,
Ecell = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{2.303 R T}{n F} \ln \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{2+}}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
Due to the presence of ions in the overall reaction, its voltage decreases with time.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: State Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions. Why does the conductivity of a solution decreases with dilution?
question_text: State Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions. Why does the conductivity of a solution decreases with dilution?
explanation: Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions states that the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be expressed as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and the cation of the electrolyte, e.g. {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)}^{\circ}=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex} The conductivity of a solution is related to the number of ions present per unit volume of the solution. When the solution is diluted, the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Hence, conductivity or specific conductance of the solution decreases.
solution: Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions states that the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be expressed as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and the cation of the electrolyte, e.g. {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)}^{\circ}=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex} The conductivity of a solution is related to the number of ions present per unit volume of the solution. When the solution is diluted, the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Hence, conductivity or specific conductance of the solution decreases.
solution_html:
Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions states that the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be expressed as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and the cation of the electrolyte, e.g.
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)}^{\circ}=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex}
The conductivity of a solution is related to the number of ions present per unit volume of the solution. When the solution is diluted, the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Hence, conductivity or specific conductance of the solution decreases.

    2. Fuel cells are Galvanic cells that are designed to convert the chemical energy from the combustion of fuels like hydrogen, methane, methanol directly into electrical energy are called fuel cells. The efficiency of the fuel cell is higher than that of a galvanic cell, e.g. hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell, in which hydrogen and oxygen are bubbled through porous carbon electrodes into a cone. KOH solution. Finely divided Pt or Pd metals are incorporated into the electrodes for increasing the rate of electrode reactions.
    1. Cell constant,
      G* = conductivity {tex}\times{/tex} resistance
      = 1.29 Sm-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 100{tex}\Omega{/tex} = 1.29 m-1
      = 1.29 cm-1
    2. Conductivity of 0.02 M KCl solution, 
      {tex}\kappa{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { Cell constant }}{\text { Resistance }}=\frac{1.29 \mathrm{cm}^{-1}}{520 \Omega}{/tex}
      = 2.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 S cm-1
    3. Molar conductivity, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\kappa \times 1000}{M}{/tex}
      = {tex}\frac{2.48 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000}{0.02}{/tex} = 124 S cm2 mol-1.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. State Faraday's first law of electrolysis. How much charge in terms of Faraday's is required for the reduction of 1 mole of Cu2+ to Cu.
  2. Calculate emf of the following cell at 298 K.
    {tex}\mathrm{Mg}(s)\left|\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}(0.1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(0.01)\right| \mathrm{Cu}(s){/tex}
    Given, {tex}\boldsymbol{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = +2.71 V,
    1F = 96500 C mol-1.
question_text: State Faraday's first law of electrolysis. How much charge in terms of Faraday's is required for the reduction of 1 mole of Cu 2+ to Cu. Calculate emf of the following cell at 298 K. {tex}\mathrm{Mg}(s)\left|\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}(0.1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(0.01)\right| \mathrm{Cu}(s){/tex} Given, {tex}\boldsymbol{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = +2.71 V, 1F = 96500 C mol -1 .
explanation: Faraday's first law of electrolysis The amount of substance deposited during electrolysis is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed. {tex}m \propto Q, \ m \propto I t,{/tex} m = ZIt where Z is electrochemical equivalent, I is current in ampere, t is a time in seconds and Q is charge in coulomb. Electrochemical equivalent (Z) It is the amount of substance deposited in grams when 1 ampere current is passed for 1 second, i.e. when 1coulomb of charge is passed. Its Unit is g C -1 . Charge required for the reduction of 1 mole of cu 2+ to cu = 2F {tex}\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+\mathrm{Mg} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+\mathrm{Cu}{/tex} Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Ccll}}^{\circ}=+2.71 \mathrm{V}{/tex} By using the Nernst equation, {tex}E_{\text { cell }}=E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} Here, n = 2 and {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = +2.71 V {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E_{\text { cell }}=2.71-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{(0.1)}{(0.01)}{/tex} = {tex}2.71-\frac{0.059}{2} \log 10{/tex} = 2.71 - 0.0295 [ {tex}\because {/tex} log 10 = 1] = 2.68 V
solution: Faraday's first law of electrolysis The amount of substance deposited during electrolysis is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed. {tex}m \propto Q, \ m \propto I t,{/tex} m = ZIt where Z is electrochemical equivalent, I is current in ampere, t is a time in seconds and Q is charge in coulomb. Electrochemical equivalent (Z) It is the amount of substance deposited in grams when 1 ampere current is passed for 1 second, i.e. when 1coulomb of charge is passed. Its Unit is g C -1 . Charge required for the reduction of 1 mole of cu 2+ to cu = 2F {tex}\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+\mathrm{Mg} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+\mathrm{Cu}{/tex} Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Ccll}}^{\circ}=+2.71 \mathrm{V}{/tex} By using the Nernst equation, {tex}E_{\text { cell }}=E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} Here, n = 2 and {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = +2.71 V {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E_{\text { cell }}=2.71-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{(0.1)}{(0.01)}{/tex} = {tex}2.71-\frac{0.059}{2} \log 10{/tex} = 2.71 - 0.0295 [ {tex}\because {/tex} log 10 = 1] = 2.68 V
solution_html:
1. Faraday's first law of electrolysis The amount of substance deposited during electrolysis is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed.
    {tex}m \propto Q, \ m \propto I t,{/tex} m = ZIt
    where Z is electrochemical equivalent, I is current in ampere, t is a time in seconds and Q is charge in coulomb.
    Electrochemical equivalent (Z) It is the amount of substance deposited in grams when 1 ampere current is passed for 1 second, i.e. when 1coulomb of charge is passed. Its Unit is g C-1.
    Charge required for the reduction of 1 mole of cu2+ to cu = 2F
  2. {tex}\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+\mathrm{Mg} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+\mathrm{Cu}{/tex}
    Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Ccll}}^{\circ}=+2.71 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
    By using the Nernst equation,
    {tex}E_{\text { cell }}=E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex}
    Here, n = 2 and {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = +2.71 V
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E_{\text { cell }}=2.71-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{(0.1)}{(0.01)}{/tex}
    = {tex}2.71-\frac{0.059}{2} \log 10{/tex}
    = 2.71 - 0.0295 [{tex}\because {/tex} log 10 = 1]
    = 2.68 V

question_html: Conductivity of 0.001 M acetic acid is {tex}4 \times {10}^{-5} S {cm}^{-1}{/tex}. Calculate its molar conductivity? if its molar conductivity at infinite dilution is 390 S cm2 mol-1, what is its dissociation constant?
question_text: Conductivity of 0.001 M acetic acid is {tex}4 \times {10}^{-5} S {cm}^{-1}{/tex} . Calculate its molar conductivity? if its molar conductivity at infinite dilution is 390 S cm 2 mol -1 , what is its dissociation constant?
explanation: {tex} \wedge _m^c = \frac{{k \times 1000}}{C}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 5}} \times 1000}}{{0.001}}S\;c{m^2}/mol = 40\;Sc{m^2}/mol{/tex} Degree of dissociation, {tex}\alpha = \frac{{{ \wedge ^C}}}{{{ \wedge ^\infty }}} = \frac{{40}}{{390}} = 0.10256{/tex} For the dissociation of acetic acid, CH 3 COOH {tex}\mathop {\mathop {}\limits_{Initial\;(conc.)} }\limits_{After\;time,\;t} \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_C }\limits_{(C - C\alpha )} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}CO{O^ - }}\limits_0 }\limits_{C\alpha } + \mathop {\mathop {{H^ + }}\limits_0 }\limits_{C\alpha }{/tex} Dissociation constant, {tex}{K_a} = \frac{{\left[ {C{H_3}CO{O^ - }} \right]\left[ {{H^ + }} \right]}}{{\left[ {C{H_3}COOH} \right]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{C\alpha \cdot C\alpha }}{{C - C\alpha }} = \frac{{{C^2}{\alpha ^2}}}{{C(1 - \alpha )}} = \frac{{C{\alpha ^2}}}{{(1 - \alpha )}}{/tex} {tex}{K_a} = \frac{{0.001 \times {{(0.103)}^2}}}{{(1 - 0.103)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1.061 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{0.897}} = 1.18 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex}
solution: {tex} \wedge _m^c = \frac{{k \times 1000}}{C}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 5}} \times 1000}}{{0.001}}S\;c{m^2}/mol = 40\;Sc{m^2}/mol{/tex} Degree of dissociation, {tex}\alpha = \frac{{{ \wedge ^C}}}{{{ \wedge ^\infty }}} = \frac{{40}}{{390}} = 0.10256{/tex} For the dissociation of acetic acid, CH 3 COOH {tex}\mathop {\mathop {}\limits_{Initial\;(conc.)} }\limits_{After\;time,\;t} \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_C }\limits_{(C - C\alpha )} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}CO{O^ - }}\limits_0 }\limits_{C\alpha } + \mathop {\mathop {{H^ + }}\limits_0 }\limits_{C\alpha }{/tex} Dissociation constant, {tex}{K_a} = \frac{{\left[ {C{H_3}CO{O^ - }} \right]\left[ {{H^ + }} \right]}}{{\left[ {C{H_3}COOH} \right]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{C\alpha \cdot C\alpha }}{{C - C\alpha }} = \frac{{{C^2}{\alpha ^2}}}{{C(1 - \alpha )}} = \frac{{C{\alpha ^2}}}{{(1 - \alpha )}}{/tex} {tex}{K_a} = \frac{{0.001 \times {{(0.103)}^2}}}{{(1 - 0.103)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1.061 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{0.897}} = 1.18 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \wedge _m^c = \frac{{k \times 1000}}{C}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 5}} \times 1000}}{{0.001}}S\;c{m^2}/mol = 40\;Sc{m^2}/mol{/tex}
Degree of dissociation,
{tex}\alpha = \frac{{{ \wedge ^C}}}{{{ \wedge ^\infty }}} = \frac{{40}}{{390}} = 0.10256{/tex}
For the dissociation of acetic acid, CH3COOH
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {}\limits_{Initial\;(conc.)} }\limits_{After\;time,\;t} \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_C }\limits_{(C - C\alpha )} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}CO{O^ - }}\limits_0 }\limits_{C\alpha } + \mathop {\mathop {{H^ + }}\limits_0 }\limits_{C\alpha }{/tex}
Dissociation constant,
{tex}{K_a} = \frac{{\left[ {C{H_3}CO{O^ - }} \right]\left[ {{H^ + }} \right]}}{{\left[ {C{H_3}COOH} \right]}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{C\alpha \cdot C\alpha }}{{C - C\alpha }} = \frac{{{C^2}{\alpha ^2}}}{{C(1 - \alpha )}} = \frac{{C{\alpha ^2}}}{{(1 - \alpha )}}{/tex}
{tex}{K_a} = \frac{{0.001 \times {{(0.103)}^2}}}{{(1 - 0.103)}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{1.061 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{0.897}} = 1.18 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What are fuel cells? Write the electrode reactions of a fuel cell which uses the reaction of hydrogen with oxygen?
question_text: What are fuel cells? Write the electrode reactions of a fuel cell which uses the reaction of hydrogen with oxygen?
explanation: A fuel cell is similar to a galvanic cell, it generates electricity directly by the electrochemical conversion of gaseous or liquid fuels fed to the cell as required. At anode: {tex}{H_2}(g) + 2O{H^ - }(aq) \to 2{H_2}O(l) + 2{e^ - }{/tex} At cathode: {tex}{O_2}(g) + 2{H_2}O(l) \to 4{e^ - } \to 4{H^ - }(aq){/tex} Overall reaction: {tex}2{H_2}(g) + {O_2}(g) \to 2{H_2}O(l){/tex}
solution: A fuel cell is similar to a galvanic cell, it generates electricity directly by the electrochemical conversion of gaseous or liquid fuels fed to the cell as required. At anode: {tex}{H_2}(g) + 2O{H^ - }(aq) \to 2{H_2}O(l) + 2{e^ - }{/tex} At cathode: {tex}{O_2}(g) + 2{H_2}O(l) \to 4{e^ - } \to 4{H^ - }(aq){/tex} Overall reaction: {tex}2{H_2}(g) + {O_2}(g) \to 2{H_2}O(l){/tex}
solution_html:
A fuel cell is similar to a galvanic cell, it generates electricity directly by the electrochemical conversion of gaseous or liquid fuels fed to the cell as required.
At anode: {tex}{H_2}(g) + 2O{H^ - }(aq) \to 2{H_2}O(l) + 2{e^ - }{/tex}
At cathode: {tex}{O_2}(g) + 2{H_2}O(l) \to 4{e^ - } \to 4{H^ - }(aq){/tex}
Overall reaction: {tex}2{H_2}(g) + {O_2}(g) \to 2{H_2}O(l){/tex}

question_html:
Calculate the emf of the following cell at 298 K.
{tex}\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})\left|\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(0.001 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{H}^{+}(1 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{g})(1 \text { bar }) | \mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{s}){/tex}
[Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0.44 V]
question_text: Calculate the emf of the following cell at 298 K. {tex}\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})\left|\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(0.001 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{H}^{+}(1 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{g})(1 \text { bar }) | \mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{s}){/tex} [Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0.44 V]
explanation: Anode half-reaction, Fe(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe 2+ (aq) + 2e - Cathode half reaction, {tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)\right] \times 2{/tex} Net reaction Fe(s) + 2H + (aq) ~ Fe 2+ (aq) + H 2 (g) E cell = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\left(\frac{2.303 R T}{n F}\right) \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} E cell = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\left(\frac{0.05912}{n }\right) \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} = 0.44 - {tex}\frac{{0.00591}}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{{0.001}}{1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} E cell = 0. 44 - [0.02956 {tex}\times{/tex} (-3)] = 0.44 + 0.08868 = 0.52868 V = 0.53 V
solution: Anode half-reaction, Fe(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe 2+ (aq) + 2e - Cathode half reaction, {tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)\right] \times 2{/tex} Net reaction Fe(s) + 2H + (aq) ~ Fe 2+ (aq) + H 2 (g) E cell = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\left(\frac{2.303 R T}{n F}\right) \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} E cell = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\left(\frac{0.05912}{n }\right) \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} = 0.44 - {tex}\frac{{0.00591}}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{{0.001}}{1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} E cell = 0. 44 - [0.02956 {tex}\times{/tex} (-3)] = 0.44 + 0.08868 = 0.52868 V = 0.53 V
solution_html:
Anode half-reaction, Fe(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe2+ (aq) + 2e-
Cathode half reaction,
{tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)\right] \times 2{/tex}
Net reaction
Fe(s) + 2H+ (aq) ~ Fe2+ (aq) + H2(g)
Ecell = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\left(\frac{2.303 R T}{n F}\right) \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
Ecell = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\left(\frac{0.05912}{n }\right) \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
= 0.44 - {tex}\frac{{0.00591}}{2}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{{0.001}}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ecell = 0. 44 - [0.02956 {tex}\times{/tex} (-3)]
= 0.44 + 0.08868
= 0.52868 V = 0.53 V

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The value of {tex}\Lambda _m^o{/tex} of Al2(SO4)3 is 858 S cm2 mol-1 while "{tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} is 160 S cm2 mol-1 calculate the limiting ionic conductivity of Al3+.
question_text: The value of {tex}\Lambda _m^o{/tex} of Al 2 (SO 4 ) 3 is 858 S cm 2 mol -1 while " {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} is 160 S cm 2 mol -1 calculate the limiting ionic conductivity of Al 3+ .
explanation: Given, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ} \text { of } \mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}{/tex} = 858 cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{so}_{4}^{2-}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = 160 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left[\mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}\right\}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = {tex}2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{A} \mathrm{l}^{3+}\right)+3 \lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{m}_{\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right)}{/tex} 858 = {tex}2 \lambda_{m\left(A l^{3+}\right)}^{0}+3 \times 160{/tex} 858 = {tex}2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right)}^{\circ}+480{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{A} 1^{3+}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{858-480}{2}{/tex} = 189S cm 2 mol -1
solution: Given, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ} \text { of } \mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}{/tex} = 858 cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{so}_{4}^{2-}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = 160 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left[\mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}\right\}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = {tex}2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{A} \mathrm{l}^{3+}\right)+3 \lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{m}_{\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right)}{/tex} 858 = {tex}2 \lambda_{m\left(A l^{3+}\right)}^{0}+3 \times 160{/tex} 858 = {tex}2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right)}^{\circ}+480{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{A} 1^{3+}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{858-480}{2}{/tex} = 189S cm 2 mol -1
solution_html:
Given, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ} \text { of } \mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}{/tex} = 858 cm2 mol-1
{tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{so}_{4}^{2-}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = 160 S cm2 mol-1
{tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left[\mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}\right\}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = {tex}2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{A} \mathrm{l}^{3+}\right)+3 \lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{m}_{\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right)}{/tex}
858 = {tex}2 \lambda_{m\left(A l^{3+}\right)}^{0}+3 \times 160{/tex}
858 = {tex}2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right)}^{\circ}+480{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{A} 1^{3+}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{858-480}{2}{/tex} = 189S cm2mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: How much electricity in terms of Faradays is required to produce 20g of calcium from molten CaCl2?
question_text: How much electricity in terms of Faradays is required to produce 20g of calcium from molten CaCl 2 ?
explanation: {tex}\mathrm{CaCl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}{/tex} {tex}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{a}}^{2 + }} + \mathop {2{e^ - }}\limits_{2F} {/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {Ca}\limits_{40\;g} {/tex} The electricity required to produce 40 g = 2 F Therefore, the electricity required to produce 20g {tex} = \frac{{(2 \times 20)}}{{40}} = 1F{/tex}
solution: {tex}\mathrm{CaCl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}{/tex} {tex}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{a}}^{2 + }} + \mathop {2{e^ - }}\limits_{2F} {/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {Ca}\limits_{40\;g} {/tex} The electricity required to produce 40 g = 2 F Therefore, the electricity required to produce 20g {tex} = \frac{{(2 \times 20)}}{{40}} = 1F{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\mathrm{CaCl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}{/tex}
{tex}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{a}}^{2 + }} + \mathop {2{e^ - }}\limits_{2F} {/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {Ca}\limits_{40\;g} {/tex}
The electricity required to produce 40 g = 2 F
Therefore, the electricity required to produce 20g
{tex} = \frac{{(2 \times 20)}}{{40}} = 1F{/tex}

question_html:
Calculate the emf of the following cell at 25°C.
Ag (s) | Ag+(10-3 M) || Cu2+(10-1 M) | Cu (s)
[Given,{tex}{E^o}_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = +0.46 V and log 10n = n]
question_text: Calculate the emf of the following cell at 25°C. Ag (s) | Ag + (10 -3 M) || Cu 2+ (10 -1 M) | Cu (s) [Given, {tex}{E^o}_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = +0.46 V and log 10 n = n]
explanation: The given cell is , Ag (s) | Ag + (10 -3 M) || Cu 2+ (10 -1 M) | Cu(s) At anode Ag(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ag + (aq) + e - ] {tex}\times{/tex} 2 At cathode, Cu 2+ +2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu (s) Overall reaction; 2Ag(s) + Cu 2+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Ag + + Cu(s) Now, applying Nernst equation, E cell = {tex}{E^o}_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{{[A{g^{2 + }}]}}{{[C{u^{2 + }}]}}{/tex} = 0.46 - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{{{{({{10}^{ - 3}})}^2}}}{{{{10}^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} = 0.46 - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{2}{/tex} log 10 -5 Therefore, E cell = 0.608 V
solution: The given cell is , Ag (s) | Ag + (10 -3 M) || Cu 2+ (10 -1 M) | Cu(s) At anode Ag(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ag + (aq) + e - ] {tex}\times{/tex} 2 At cathode, Cu 2+ +2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu (s) Overall reaction; 2Ag(s) + Cu 2+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Ag + + Cu(s) Now, applying Nernst equation, E cell = {tex}{E^o}_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{{[A{g^{2 + }}]}}{{[C{u^{2 + }}]}}{/tex} = 0.46 - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{{{{({{10}^{ - 3}})}^2}}}{{{{10}^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} = 0.46 - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{2}{/tex} log 10 -5 Therefore, E cell = 0.608 V
solution_html:
The given cell is , Ag (s) | Ag+(10-3M) || Cu2+(10-1 M) | Cu(s)
At anode Ag(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ag+ (aq) + e-] {tex}\times{/tex} 2
At cathode, Cu2+ +2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu (s) 
Overall reaction; 2Ag(s) + Cu2+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Ag+ + Cu(s)
Now, applying Nernst equation, Ecell = {tex}{E^o}_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{2}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{{[A{g^{2 + }}]}}{{[C{u^{2 + }}]}}{/tex} = 0.46 -  {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{2}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{{{{({{10}^{ - 3}})}^2}}}{{{{10}^{ - 1}}}}{/tex}  = 0.46 -  {tex}\frac{{0.0591}}{2}{/tex}log 10-5
Therefore, Ecell = 0.608 V

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What are fuel cells? Explain the electrode reactions involved in the working of H2 - O2 fuel cell.
question_text: What are fuel cells? Explain the electrode reactions involved in the working of H 2 - O 2 fuel cell.
explanation: Fuel cells : Those galvanic cells in which chemical energy of combustion of fuels like hydrogen, methane, etc. is converted into electrical energy are called fuel cells. H 2 -O 2 fuel cell: The cell consists of three compartments separated by a porous electrode. Hydrogen gas is introduced into one compartment and oxygen into another compartment. These gases then diffuse slowly through the electrode and react with an electrolyte that is in the central part of the cell. The electrodes are made of porous carbon and electrolyte is a resin containing concentrated aqueous sodium hydroxide solution. Hydrogen is oxidized at anode and oxygen is reduced at the cathode. The electrode reactions involved in the working of the H 2 -O 2 fuel cell are as: At cathode, {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q){/tex} At anode, {tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q) \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-}{/tex} Overall cell reaction: 2H 2 (g) + O 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} 2H 2 O(l)
solution: Fuel cells : Those galvanic cells in which chemical energy of combustion of fuels like hydrogen, methane, etc. is converted into electrical energy are called fuel cells. H 2 -O 2 fuel cell: The cell consists of three compartments separated by a porous electrode. Hydrogen gas is introduced into one compartment and oxygen into another compartment. These gases then diffuse slowly through the electrode and react with an electrolyte that is in the central part of the cell. The electrodes are made of porous carbon and electrolyte is a resin containing concentrated aqueous sodium hydroxide solution. Hydrogen is oxidized at anode and oxygen is reduced at the cathode. The electrode reactions involved in the working of the H 2 -O 2 fuel cell are as: At cathode, {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q){/tex} At anode, {tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q) \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-}{/tex} Overall cell reaction: 2H 2 (g) + O 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} 2H 2 O(l)
solution_html:
Fuel cells : Those galvanic cells in which chemical energy of combustion of fuels like hydrogen, methane, etc. is converted into electrical energy are called fuel cells.
H2-O2 fuel cell: The cell consists of three compartments separated by a porous electrode. Hydrogen gas is introduced into one compartment and oxygen into another compartment. These gases then diffuse slowly through the electrode and react with an electrolyte that is in the central part of the cell. The electrodes are made of porous carbon and electrolyte is a resin containing concentrated aqueous sodium hydroxide solution. Hydrogen is oxidized at anode and oxygen is reduced at the cathode.


The electrode reactions involved in the working of the H2-O2 fuel cell are as:
At cathode,
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q){/tex}
At anode,
{tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q) \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-}{/tex}
Overall cell reaction: 2H2(g) + O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} 2H2O(l)

question_html: The resistance of a conductivity cell containing 0.001 M KCl solution at 298 K is 1500 ohm.What is the cell constant if conductivity of 0.001 M KCl solution at 298 K is 0.146 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 S cm-1?
question_text: The resistance of a conductivity cell containing 0.001 M KCl solution at 298 K is 1500 ohm.What is the cell constant if conductivity of 0.001 M KCl solution at 298 K is 0.146 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 S cm -1 ?
explanation: Given that, Conductivity of the cell, κ = 0.146 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 S cm -1 Resistance of the cell, R = 1500 Ω Formula of cell constant Cell constant = κ {tex}\times{/tex} R Plug the values we get = 0.146 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1500 = 0.219 cm -1
solution: Given that, Conductivity of the cell, κ = 0.146 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 S cm -1 Resistance of the cell, R = 1500 Ω Formula of cell constant Cell constant = κ {tex}\times{/tex} R Plug the values we get = 0.146 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1500 = 0.219 cm -1
solution_html:
Given that,
Conductivity of the cell, κ = 0.146 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 S cm-1
Resistance of the cell, R = 1500 Ω
Formula of cell constant
Cell constant = κ {tex}\times{/tex} R
Plug the values we get
= 0.146 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1500
= 0.219 cm-1

question_html: Molar conductance of 1.5 M solution of an electrolyte is found to be 138.9 S cm2 mol-1 What would be the specific conductance of this solution?
question_text: Molar conductance of 1.5 M solution of an electrolyte is found to be 138.9 S cm 2 mol -1 What would be the specific conductance of this solution?
explanation: Given that molarity = 1.5 m molar conductivity = 138.9 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}{\lambda _m} = \frac{{1000K}}{M} = K = \frac{{{\lambda _m} \times M}}{{1000}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{138.9\,S\,c{m^2}mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 1.5mol}}{{1000\,c{m^3}}}{/tex} K = 0.20835 S cm -1 {tex} = 2.0835 \times {10^{ - 1}}\,S\,c{m^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution: Given that molarity = 1.5 m molar conductivity = 138.9 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}{\lambda _m} = \frac{{1000K}}{M} = K = \frac{{{\lambda _m} \times M}}{{1000}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{138.9\,S\,c{m^2}mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 1.5mol}}{{1000\,c{m^3}}}{/tex} K = 0.20835 S cm -1 {tex} = 2.0835 \times {10^{ - 1}}\,S\,c{m^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that

molarity = 1.5 m

molar conductivity = 138.9 S cm2 mol-1

{tex}{\lambda _m} = \frac{{1000K}}{M} = K = \frac{{{\lambda _m} \times M}}{{1000}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{138.9\,S\,c{m^2}mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 1.5mol}}{{1000\,c{m^3}}}{/tex}
K = 0.20835 S cm-1
{tex} = 2.0835 \times {10^{ - 1}}\,S\,c{m^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A voltaic cell is set-up at 25°C with the following half-cells.
Ag+(0.001 M)|Ag and Cu2+(0.10 M)|Cu
What would be the voltage of this cell? [Given, E~ell = 0.46 V]
question_text: A voltaic cell is set-up at 25°C with the following half-cells. Ag + (0.001 M)|Ag and Cu 2+ (0.10 M)|Cu What would be the voltage of this cell? [Given, E~ell = 0.46 V]
explanation: Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} =0.46V, [Ag + ], = 0.001M [Cu 2+ ] = 0.10M Cell reaction is Ag + (aq) + Cu(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu 2+ (aq) + Ag(s) Thus, n = 2 {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}0.46-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.10}{\left(10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 0.46 - 0.148 = 0. 312V
solution: Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} =0.46V, [Ag + ], = 0.001M [Cu 2+ ] = 0.10M Cell reaction is Ag + (aq) + Cu(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu 2+ (aq) + Ag(s) Thus, n = 2 {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}0.46-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.10}{\left(10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 0.46 - 0.148 = 0. 312V
solution_html:
Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex}=0.46V, [Ag+], = 0.001M [Cu2+] = 0.10M
Cell reaction is
Ag+(aq) + Cu(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu2+(aq) + Ag(s)
Thus, n = 2
{tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}0.46-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.10}{\left(10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
= 0.46 - 0.148 = 0. 312V

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A voltaic cell is set-up at 250 C with following half cells Al|Al3+(0.001 M) and Ni|Ni2+(0.50 M)
Write an equation for the reaction that occurs when the cell generates an electric current and determine the cell potential.
[Given, {tex}E_{N{i^{2 + }}/Ni}^\Theta = - 0.25\,V,{/tex}{tex}and\,E_{A{l^{3 + }}/Al}^\Theta = - 1.66\,V \log (8 \times {10^{ - 6}}){/tex} = - 5.09690]
question_text: A voltaic cell is set-up at 25 0 C with following half cells Al|Al 3+ (0.001 M) and Ni|Ni 2+ (0.50 M) Write an equation for the reaction that occurs when the cell generates an electric current and determine the cell potential. [Given, {tex}E_{N{i^{2 + }}/Ni}^\Theta = - 0.25\,V,{/tex} {tex}and\,E_{A{l^{3 + }}/Al}^\Theta = - 1.66\,V \log (8 \times {10^{ - 6}}){/tex} = - 5.09690]
explanation: For the electrochemical cell, Half cell reactions are: At cathode(Reduction): {tex}Ni^{2+}(aq)+2e^-\rightarrow Ni(s){/tex} At Anode(Oxidation): {tex}Al(s)\rightarrow Al^{3+}(aq)+3e^-{/tex} The cell reaction is {tex}2Al(s) + 3N{i^{2 + }}(aq) \to 2A{l^{3 + }}(aq) + 3Ni(s);{/tex} (Here n=6) Standard emf of the reaction is: {tex}E_{cell}^\Theta = E_{N{i^{2 + }}/Ni}^\Theta - E_{A{l^{3 + }}/Al}^\Theta {/tex} = -0.25V - (-1.66V) = 1.41V From Nernst equation, we have, {tex}{E_{cell}} = E_{cell}^\Theta - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\log \frac{{{{\left[ {A{l^{3 + }}} \right]}^2}}}{{{{\left[ {N{i^{2 + }}} \right]}^3}}}{/tex} {tex}{E_{cell}} = 1.14V - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\log \frac{{{{\left( {{{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {0.50} \right)}^3}}}{/tex} {tex}{E_{cell}} = 1.14V - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\log \frac{{8 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{1}{/tex} {tex}{E_{cell}} = 1.14V - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\left[ {\log 8 - 6\log 10} \right]{/tex} {tex}=1.14V-\frac{0.0591V}6\left[0.9031-6\times1\right]{/tex} {tex} = 1.14V - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\left[ { - 5.0969} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{E_{cell}} = 1.4602\;V{/tex}
solution: For the electrochemical cell, Half cell reactions are: At cathode(Reduction): {tex}Ni^{2+}(aq)+2e^-\rightarrow Ni(s){/tex} At Anode(Oxidation): {tex}Al(s)\rightarrow Al^{3+}(aq)+3e^-{/tex} The cell reaction is {tex}2Al(s) + 3N{i^{2 + }}(aq) \to 2A{l^{3 + }}(aq) + 3Ni(s);{/tex} (Here n=6) Standard emf of the reaction is: {tex}E_{cell}^\Theta = E_{N{i^{2 + }}/Ni}^\Theta - E_{A{l^{3 + }}/Al}^\Theta {/tex} = -0.25V - (-1.66V) = 1.41V From Nernst equation, we have, {tex}{E_{cell}} = E_{cell}^\Theta - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\log \frac{{{{\left[ {A{l^{3 + }}} \right]}^2}}}{{{{\left[ {N{i^{2 + }}} \right]}^3}}}{/tex} {tex}{E_{cell}} = 1.14V - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\log \frac{{{{\left( {{{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {0.50} \right)}^3}}}{/tex} {tex}{E_{cell}} = 1.14V - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\log \frac{{8 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{1}{/tex} {tex}{E_{cell}} = 1.14V - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\left[ {\log 8 - 6\log 10} \right]{/tex} {tex}=1.14V-\frac{0.0591V}6\left[0.9031-6\times1\right]{/tex} {tex} = 1.14V - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\left[ { - 5.0969} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{E_{cell}} = 1.4602\;V{/tex}
solution_html:
For the electrochemical cell,
Half cell reactions are:
At cathode(Reduction):
{tex}Ni^{2+}(aq)+2e^-\rightarrow Ni(s){/tex}
At Anode(Oxidation):
{tex}Al(s)\rightarrow Al^{3+}(aq)+3e^-{/tex}
The cell reaction is
{tex}2Al(s) + 3N{i^{2 + }}(aq) \to 2A{l^{3 + }}(aq) + 3Ni(s);{/tex}

(Here n=6)
Standard emf of the reaction is:

{tex}E_{cell}^\Theta = E_{N{i^{2 + }}/Ni}^\Theta - E_{A{l^{3 + }}/Al}^\Theta {/tex}

= -0.25V - (-1.66V) = 1.41V
From Nernst equation, we have,

{tex}{E_{cell}} = E_{cell}^\Theta - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\log \frac{{{{\left[ {A{l^{3 + }}} \right]}^2}}}{{{{\left[ {N{i^{2 + }}} \right]}^3}}}{/tex}

{tex}{E_{cell}} = 1.14V - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\log \frac{{{{\left( {{{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {0.50} \right)}^3}}}{/tex}

{tex}{E_{cell}} = 1.14V - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\log \frac{{8 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{1}{/tex}

{tex}{E_{cell}} = 1.14V - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\left[ {\log 8 - 6\log 10} \right]{/tex}

{tex}=1.14V-\frac{0.0591V}6\left[0.9031-6\times1\right]{/tex}
{tex} = 1.14V - \frac{{0.0591V}}{6}\left[ { - 5.0969} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{E_{cell}} = 1.4602\;V{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Zinc rod is dipped in 0.1 M solution of ZnSO4
The salt is 95% dissociated at is dilution at 298 K . Calculate the electrode potential. Given:
E0(Zn2+/Zn) = -0.76
question_text: Zinc rod is dipped in 0.1 M solution of ZnSO 4 The salt is 95% dissociated at is dilution at 298 K . Calculate the electrode potential. Given: E 0 (Zn 2+ /Zn) = -0.76
solution_html:
{tex}\left[ {Z{n^{2 + }}} \right] = 0.1 \times \frac{{95}}{{100}} = 0.095M{/tex}
{tex}Z{n^{2 + }} + 2{e^ - } \to Zn{/tex}
{tex}{E_{(Z{n^ + }/Zn)}} = E_{(Z{n^ + }/Zn)}^0 - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{1}{{\left[ {Z{n^{2 + }}} \right]}}{/tex}
{tex} = - 0.76V - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{1}{{0.095}}{/tex}
{tex} = - 0.76V - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\left[ {\log 1000 - \log 95} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = - 0.76 - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\left[ {3.000 - 1.9777} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = - 0.76V - \frac{{0.0591}}{2} \times 1.0223{/tex}
{tex} = - 0.76V - \frac{{0.0604}}{2} = 0.76 - 0.0302{/tex}
= - 0.7902 V

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The electrical resistance of a column of 0.05 mol L-1 NaOH solution of diameter 1 cm and length 50 cm is 5.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. Calculate its resistivity, conductivity and molar conductivity.
question_text: The electrical resistance of a column of 0.05 mol L -1 NaOH solution of diameter 1 cm and length 50 cm is 5.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Calculate its resistivity, conductivity and molar conductivity.
explanation: Given, concentration of NaOH = 0.05M diameter = 1 cm, length = 50 cm, resistance= 5.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Area of cross section, A = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex} = {tex}3.14 \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} {/tex} = 0.785 cm 2 Resistivity, {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R A}{l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5.55 \times 10^{3} \times 0.785}{50 }{/tex} = 87.135 {tex}\Omega{/tex} cm Conductivity, {tex}\sigma=\frac{1}{\rho}=\frac{1}{87.135}{/tex} = {tex}0.01148=1.148 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} Molar conductivity of solution {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{\sigma} \times 1000}{M}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.148 \times 10^{-2} \times 1000}{0.05}{/tex} = 229.6 Scm 2 mol -1
solution: Given, concentration of NaOH = 0.05M diameter = 1 cm, length = 50 cm, resistance= 5.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Area of cross section, A = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex} = {tex}3.14 \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} {/tex} = 0.785 cm 2 Resistivity, {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R A}{l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5.55 \times 10^{3} \times 0.785}{50 }{/tex} = 87.135 {tex}\Omega{/tex} cm Conductivity, {tex}\sigma=\frac{1}{\rho}=\frac{1}{87.135}{/tex} = {tex}0.01148=1.148 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} Molar conductivity of solution {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{\sigma} \times 1000}{M}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.148 \times 10^{-2} \times 1000}{0.05}{/tex} = 229.6 Scm 2 mol -1
solution_html:
Given,
concentration of NaOH = 0.05M 
diameter = 1 cm,
length = 50 cm,
resistance= 5.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Area of cross section, A = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex} = {tex}3.14 \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} {/tex} = 0.785 cm2
Resistivity, {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R A}{l}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5.55 \times 10^{3} \times 0.785}{50 }{/tex} = 87.135{tex}\Omega{/tex} cm
Conductivity,{tex}\sigma=\frac{1}{\rho}=\frac{1}{87.135}{/tex} = {tex}0.01148=1.148 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex}
Molar conductivity of solution {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{\sigma} \times 1000}{M}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.148 \times 10^{-2} \times 1000}{0.05}{/tex} = 229.6 Scm2 mol-1

question_html:
In the button cell, widely used in watches, the following reaction takes place
{tex}Zn\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + A{g_2}O\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}{H_2}O\left( l \right) {/tex} {tex}\to Z{n^{2 + }}\left( {aq} \right) + 2Ag\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}20{H^ - }\left( {aq} \right){/tex} Given that {tex}E_{(Ag^+/Ag)}^\Theta = 0.80 V, E_{(Zn^{2+}/Zn)}^\Theta=-0.76\;V{/tex}

Calculate standard emf and standard free Gibbs energy of the cell.
question_text: In the button cell, widely used in watches, the following reaction takes place {tex}Zn\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + A{g_2}O\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}{H_2}O\left( l \right) {/tex} {tex}\to Z{n^{2 + }}\left( {aq} \right) + 2Ag\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}20{H^ - }\left( {aq} \right){/tex} Given that {tex}E_{(Ag^+/Ag)}^\Theta = 0.80 V, E_{(Zn^{2+}/Zn)}^\Theta=-0.76\;V{/tex} Calculate standard emf and standard free Gibbs energy of the cell.
explanation: For the button cell, chemical reactions are: At anode: {tex}Zn(s) \to Z{n^{2 + }}(aq) + 2{e^ - }{/tex} At Cathode: {tex}A{g_2}O(s) + {H_2}O(l) + 2{e^ - } \to 2Ag(s) + 2O{H^ - }(aq){/tex} Complete reaction is: {tex}Zn\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + A{g_2}O\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}{H_2}O\left( l \right){/tex} {tex} \to Z{n^{2 + }}\left( {aq} \right) + 2Ag\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}20{H^ - }\left( {aq} \right){/tex} , for this reaction, n=2 moles of electrons. Now, {tex}E_{cell}^\Theta = E_{A{g^ + }/Ag}^\Theta - E_{Z{n^{2 + }}/Zn}^\Theta {/tex} = 0.80 - (-0.76) = 1.56V We know that {tex}{\Delta _r}{G^\Theta } = - nFE_{cell}^\Theta {/tex} Here, n = 2 F = 96500 C mol -1 {tex}\Delta G^\Theta=-2\times1.56\;V\times96000\;C\,\;mol^{-1}{/tex} = -301080 J mol -1 = -301.08 kJ mol -1 {tex}\therefore\Delta G^\Theta=-308.08\;kJ\;mol^{-1}{/tex}
solution: For the button cell, chemical reactions are: At anode: {tex}Zn(s) \to Z{n^{2 + }}(aq) + 2{e^ - }{/tex} At Cathode: {tex}A{g_2}O(s) + {H_2}O(l) + 2{e^ - } \to 2Ag(s) + 2O{H^ - }(aq){/tex} Complete reaction is: {tex}Zn\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + A{g_2}O\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}{H_2}O\left( l \right){/tex} {tex} \to Z{n^{2 + }}\left( {aq} \right) + 2Ag\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}20{H^ - }\left( {aq} \right){/tex} , for this reaction, n=2 moles of electrons. Now, {tex}E_{cell}^\Theta = E_{A{g^ + }/Ag}^\Theta - E_{Z{n^{2 + }}/Zn}^\Theta {/tex} = 0.80 - (-0.76) = 1.56V We know that {tex}{\Delta _r}{G^\Theta } = - nFE_{cell}^\Theta {/tex} Here, n = 2 F = 96500 C mol -1 {tex}\Delta G^\Theta=-2\times1.56\;V\times96000\;C\,\;mol^{-1}{/tex} = -301080 J mol -1 = -301.08 kJ mol -1 {tex}\therefore\Delta G^\Theta=-308.08\;kJ\;mol^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
For the button cell, chemical reactions are:

At anode:
{tex}Zn(s) \to Z{n^{2 + }}(aq) + 2{e^ - }{/tex}

At Cathode:
{tex}A{g_2}O(s) + {H_2}O(l) + 2{e^ - } \to 2Ag(s) + 2O{H^ - }(aq){/tex}

Complete reaction is:
{tex}Zn\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + A{g_2}O\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}{H_2}O\left( l \right){/tex} {tex} \to Z{n^{2 + }}\left( {aq} \right) + 2Ag\left( s \right){\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}20{H^ - }\left( {aq} \right){/tex}, for this reaction, n=2 moles of electrons.
Now,
{tex}E_{cell}^\Theta = E_{A{g^ + }/Ag}^\Theta - E_{Z{n^{2 + }}/Zn}^\Theta {/tex}
= 0.80 - (-0.76) = 1.56V

We know that
{tex}{\Delta _r}{G^\Theta } = - nFE_{cell}^\Theta {/tex}

Here, n = 2

F = 96500 C mol-1

{tex}\Delta G^\Theta=-2\times1.56\;V\times96000\;C\,\;mol^{-1}{/tex}

= -301080 J mol-1
= -301.08 kJ mol-1
{tex}\therefore\Delta G^\Theta=-308.08\;kJ\;mol^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A strip of nickel metal is placed in a 1 molar solution of Ni(NO3)2 and a strip of silver metal is placed in a one molar solution of AgNO3. An electrochemical cell is created when the two solutions are connected by a salt bridge and the two strips are connected by wires to a voltmeter.

  1. Write the balanced equations for the overall reaction occurring in the cell and calculate the cell potential.
  2. Calculate the cell potential (Ecell) at 25o C for the cell, if the initial concentration of Ni(NO3)2 is 0.100 molar and the initial concentration of AgNO3 is 1.00 molar.
    [{tex}E_{\mathrm{N}_{2}^{2+} / \mathrm{N}_{1}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.25 V, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.80 V, log 10-1 = -1].
question_text: A strip of nickel metal is placed in a 1 molar solution of Ni(NO 3 ) 2 and a strip of silver metal is placed in a one molar solution of AgNO 3 . An electrochemical cell is created when the two solutions are connected by a salt bridge and the two strips are connected by wires to a voltmeter. Write the balanced equations for the overall reaction occurring in the cell and calculate the cell potential. Calculate the cell potential (E cell ) at 25 o C for the cell, if the initial concentration of Ni(NO 3 ) 2 is 0.100 molar and the initial concentration of AgNO 3 is 1.00 molar. [ {tex}E_{\mathrm{N}_{2}^{2+} / \mathrm{N}_{1}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.25 V, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.80 V, log 10 -1 = -1].
explanation: Oxidation half cell reaction; Ni(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ni 2+ (aq) + 2e - ( At anode ) Reduction half cell reaction; 2Ag + (aq) + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} + 2Ag(s) ( At cathode ) Overall cell reaction; Ni (s) + 2Ag + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ni 2+ (aq) + 2Ag (s); Therefore, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{cathode}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{anode}}^{\circ}{/tex} = +0.80 V - (-0.25 V) = 1.05 V By applying Nernst equation, we have; E cell = E o cell - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{N} i^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{(0.1)}{(1)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}1.05-\frac{0.0591}{2} \times(-1){/tex} = 1.05 + 0.0295 = 1.0795 V.
solution: Oxidation half cell reaction; Ni(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ni 2+ (aq) + 2e - ( At anode ) Reduction half cell reaction; 2Ag + (aq) + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} + 2Ag(s) ( At cathode ) Overall cell reaction; Ni (s) + 2Ag + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ni 2+ (aq) + 2Ag (s); Therefore, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{cathode}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{anode}}^{\circ}{/tex} = +0.80 V - (-0.25 V) = 1.05 V By applying Nernst equation, we have; E cell = E o cell - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{N} i^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{(0.1)}{(1)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}1.05-\frac{0.0591}{2} \times(-1){/tex} = 1.05 + 0.0295 = 1.0795 V.
solution_html:
1. Oxidation half cell reaction; Ni(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ni2+ (aq) + 2e-  (At anode)
    Reduction half cell reaction; 2Ag+(aq) + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} + 2Ag(s) (At cathode)
    Overall cell reaction;  Ni (s) + 2Ag+ (aq){tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ni2+ (aq) + 2Ag (s); Therefore, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{cathode}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{anode}}^{\circ}{/tex} = +0.80 V - (-0.25 V) = 1.05 V
  2. By applying Nernst equation, we have;
    Ecell = Eocell - {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{N} i^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} 
    = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{(0.1)}{(1)^{2}}{/tex} 
    = {tex}1.05-\frac{0.0591}{2} \times(-1){/tex} 
    = 1.05 + 0.0295 = 1.0795 V.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
When a certain conductance cell was filled with 0.1 M KCl, it has a resistance of 85 ohms at 25°C. When the same cell was filled with an aqueous solution of 0.052 M unknown electrolyte, the resistance was 96 ohms. Calculate the molar conductance of the electrolyte at this concentration.
[Specific conductance of 0.1 M KCl = 1.29x 10-2 ohm" cm"]
question_text: When a certain conductance cell was filled with 0.1 M KCl, it has a resistance of 85 ohms at 25°C. When the same cell was filled with an aqueous solution of 0.052 M unknown electrolyte, the resistance was 96 ohms. Calculate the molar conductance of the electrolyte at this concentration. [Specific conductance of 0.1 M KCl = 1.29x 10 -2 ohm" cm"]
explanation: Given, the conductance of cell when electrolyte of 0.1 M KCl is filled = 85 ohm's The conductance of cell when unknown electrolyte of 0.052 Mis filled = 96 ohm's Specific conductance (1C) of 0.1 M KCl = {tex}1.29 \times 10^{-2} \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\kappa=\frac{1}{R} \times \text { cell constant }{/tex} Cell constant = {tex}\kappa{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} R = {tex}0.0129 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1} \times 85=1.0965 \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} = 109.65m -1 {tex}\kappa=\frac{1}{R} \times \text { cell constant }{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{96} \times 109.65 \mathrm{m}^{-1}=0.01142 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{k} \times 1000}{M}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.01142 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{cm}^{-1} \times 1000 \mathrm{cm}^{3} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{0.052 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{/tex} {tex}=219.61 \mathrm{Scm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution: Given, the conductance of cell when electrolyte of 0.1 M KCl is filled = 85 ohm's The conductance of cell when unknown electrolyte of 0.052 Mis filled = 96 ohm's Specific conductance (1C) of 0.1 M KCl = {tex}1.29 \times 10^{-2} \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\kappa=\frac{1}{R} \times \text { cell constant }{/tex} Cell constant = {tex}\kappa{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} R = {tex}0.0129 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1} \times 85=1.0965 \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} = 109.65m -1 {tex}\kappa=\frac{1}{R} \times \text { cell constant }{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{96} \times 109.65 \mathrm{m}^{-1}=0.01142 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{k} \times 1000}{M}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.01142 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{cm}^{-1} \times 1000 \mathrm{cm}^{3} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{0.052 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{/tex} {tex}=219.61 \mathrm{Scm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, the conductance of cell when electrolyte of 0.1 M KCl is filled = 85 ohm's
The conductance of cell when unknown electrolyte of 0.052 Mis filled = 96 ohm's
Specific conductance (1C) of 0.1 M KCl
= {tex}1.29 \times 10^{-2} \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\kappa=\frac{1}{R} \times \text { cell constant }{/tex}
Cell constant = {tex}\kappa{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} R
= {tex}0.0129 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1} \times 85=1.0965 \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex}
= 109.65m-1
{tex}\kappa=\frac{1}{R} \times \text { cell constant }{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{96} \times 109.65 \mathrm{m}^{-1}=0.01142 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{k} \times 1000}{M}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{0.01142 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{cm}^{-1} \times 1000 \mathrm{cm}^{3} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{0.052 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{/tex}
{tex}=219.61 \mathrm{Scm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Express the relation among cell constant, resistance of the solution in the cell and conductivity of the solution. How is molar conductivity of a solution related to its conductivity?
question_text: Express the relation among cell constant, resistance of the solution in the cell and conductivity of the solution. How is molar conductivity of a solution related to its conductivity?
explanation: Relation among cell constant, resistance and conductivity {tex}G^\ast=\frac lA\;=\;R\;\kappa{/tex} {tex}\therefore{k = \frac{l}{R} \times {G^*}}{/tex} Relation between molar conductivity and conductivity. {tex}\Lambda_m(\;S\;cm^2\;mol^{-1})=\frac{\kappa\;(\;S\;cm^{-1})}{1000\;L\;m^{-3}\;\times\;molariity\;(mol\;L^{-1})}{/tex}
solution: Relation among cell constant, resistance and conductivity {tex}G^\ast=\frac lA\;=\;R\;\kappa{/tex} {tex}\therefore{k = \frac{l}{R} \times {G^*}}{/tex} Relation between molar conductivity and conductivity. {tex}\Lambda_m(\;S\;cm^2\;mol^{-1})=\frac{\kappa\;(\;S\;cm^{-1})}{1000\;L\;m^{-3}\;\times\;molariity\;(mol\;L^{-1})}{/tex}
solution_html:
Relation among cell constant, resistance and conductivity
{tex}G^\ast=\frac lA\;=\;R\;\kappa{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{k = \frac{l}{R} \times {G^*}}{/tex}
Relation between molar conductivity and conductivity.
{tex}\Lambda_m(\;S\;cm^2\;mol^{-1})=\frac{\kappa\;(\;S\;cm^{-1})}{1000\;L\;m^{-3}\;\times\;molariity\;(mol\;L^{-1})}{/tex}
