subject: Chemistry
question_html: The chemistry of corrosion of iron is essentially an electrochemical phenomenon. Explain the reactions occurring during the corrosion of iron in the atmosphere.
question_text: The chemistry of corrosion of iron is essentially an electrochemical phenomenon. Explain the reactions occurring during the corrosion of iron in the atmosphere.
explanation: The chemistry of corrosion of iron is essentially an electrochemical phenomenon in which a metal oxide or other salt of metal forms a coating on metal oxide. The reactions occurring during the corrosion of iron in the atmosphere are as follows: At anode: {tex}2 \mathrm{Fe}(s) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+4 e^{-}, E_{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}}^{0}=-0.44 \mathrm{V}{/tex} At cathode: {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+4 e^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l){/tex} ; {tex}E_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{O}_{2} / \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}^{\circ}=1.23 \mathrm{V}{/tex} Overall reaction: 2Fe(s) + O 2 (g) + 4H + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Fe 2+ (aq) + + 2H 2 O(l); {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=1.67 \mathrm{V}{/tex} The ferrous ions are further oxidised to ferric ions by atmospheric oxygen. The reaction is as follows: {tex}2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g){/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe 2 O 3 (s) + 4H + (aq) Fe 2 O 3 + xH 2 O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {F{e_2}{O_3} \cdot x{H_2}O}\limits_{Hydrated\;ferric\;oxide} {/tex}
solution: The chemistry of corrosion of iron is essentially an electrochemical phenomenon in which a metal oxide or other salt of metal forms a coating on metal oxide. The reactions occurring during the corrosion of iron in the atmosphere are as follows: At anode: {tex}2 \mathrm{Fe}(s) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+4 e^{-}, E_{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}}^{0}=-0.44 \mathrm{V}{/tex} At cathode: {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+4 e^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l){/tex} ; {tex}E_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{O}_{2} / \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}^{\circ}=1.23 \mathrm{V}{/tex} Overall reaction: 2Fe(s) + O 2 (g) + 4H + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Fe 2+ (aq) + + 2H 2 O(l); {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=1.67 \mathrm{V}{/tex} The ferrous ions are further oxidised to ferric ions by atmospheric oxygen. The reaction is as follows: {tex}2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g){/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe 2 O 3 (s) + 4H + (aq) Fe 2 O 3 + xH 2 O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {F{e_2}{O_3} \cdot x{H_2}O}\limits_{Hydrated\;ferric\;oxide} {/tex}
solution_html:
The chemistry of corrosion of iron is essentially an electrochemical phenomenon in which a metal oxide or other salt of metal forms a coating on metal oxide. The reactions occurring during the corrosion of iron in the atmosphere are as follows:
At anode: {tex}2 \mathrm{Fe}(s) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+4 e^{-}, E_{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}}^{0}=-0.44 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
At cathode: {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+4 e^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l){/tex}; {tex}E_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{O}_{2} / \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}^{\circ}=1.23 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
Overall reaction: 2Fe(s) + O2(g) + 4H+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Fe2+(aq)+ + 2H2O(l);  {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=1.67 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
The ferrous ions are further oxidised to ferric ions by atmospheric oxygen. The reaction is as follows:
{tex}2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g){/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe2O3(s) + 4H+(aq)
Fe2O3 + xH2O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {F{e_2}{O_3} \cdot x{H_2}O}\limits_{Hydrated\;ferric\;oxide} {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Two half reactions of an electrochemical cell are given below:
MnO4(aq) + 8H+(aq) + 5e-  {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Mn2+(aq) + 4H2O(l); Eo = +1.51 V
Sn2+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Sn4+(aq) + 2e-; Eo = -0.15 V
Construct the redox equation from the standard potential of the cell and predict, if the reaction is reactant favoured or product favoured.
question_text: Two half reactions of an electrochemical cell are given below: MnO 4 (aq) + 8H + (aq) + 5e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Mn 2+ (aq) + 4H 2 O(l); E o = +1.51 V Sn 2+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Sn 4+ (aq) + 2e - ; E o = -0.15 V Construct the redox equation from the standard potential of the cell and predict, if the reaction is reactant favoured or product favoured.
explanation: The galvanic cell for the reaction is represented as {tex}{Pt}\left|\operatorname{Sn}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Sn}^{4+}(\mathrm{aq}) {/tex} {tex}\| \mathrm{MnO}^{-} 4(\mathrm{aq}) 1 \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) \mathrm{IPt}{/tex} {tex}E_{cell}^o = E_{MnO_4^ - /M{n^{2 + }}}^o - \,E_{S{n^{4 + }}/S{n^{2 + }}}^o{/tex} {tex}E_{cell}^o{/tex} = 151 V - (-0.15 )V = l 66V Since {tex}E_{cell}^o{/tex} is positive, {tex}\Delta{/tex} G will be negative, therefore, the reaction is product favoured.
solution: The galvanic cell for the reaction is represented as {tex}{Pt}\left|\operatorname{Sn}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Sn}^{4+}(\mathrm{aq}) {/tex} {tex}\| \mathrm{MnO}^{-} 4(\mathrm{aq}) 1 \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) \mathrm{IPt}{/tex} {tex}E_{cell}^o = E_{MnO_4^ - /M{n^{2 + }}}^o - \,E_{S{n^{4 + }}/S{n^{2 + }}}^o{/tex} {tex}E_{cell}^o{/tex} = 151 V - (-0.15 )V = l 66V Since {tex}E_{cell}^o{/tex} is positive, {tex}\Delta{/tex} G will be negative, therefore, the reaction is product favoured.
solution_html:
The galvanic cell for the reaction is represented as
{tex}{Pt}\left|\operatorname{Sn}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Sn}^{4+}(\mathrm{aq}) {/tex}{tex}\| \mathrm{MnO}^{-} 4(\mathrm{aq}) 1 \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) \mathrm{IPt}{/tex}
{tex}E_{cell}^o = E_{MnO_4^ - /M{n^{2 + }}}^o - \,E_{S{n^{4 + }}/S{n^{2 + }}}^o{/tex}
{tex}E_{cell}^o{/tex} = 151 V - (-0.15 )V = l 66V
Since {tex}E_{cell}^o{/tex} is positive, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G will be negative, therefore, the reaction is product favoured.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Determine the value of equilibrium constant {tex}({K_c})\,and\,\Delta {G^\theta }{/tex} for the following reaction.
{tex}Ni(s) + 2A{g^ + }(aq) \to N{i^{2 + }}(aq) + 2Ag(s){/tex}
{tex}{E^\theta } = 1.05\,V\,(1\,F = 96500\,C\,mo{l^{ - 1}}){/tex}
question_text: Determine the value of equilibrium constant {tex}({K_c})\,and\,\Delta {G^\theta }{/tex} for the following reaction. {tex}Ni(s) + 2A{g^ + }(aq) \to N{i^{2 + }}(aq) + 2Ag(s){/tex} {tex}{E^\theta } = 1.05\,V\,(1\,F = 96500\,C\,mo{l^{ - 1}}){/tex}
explanation: We have, {tex}Ni(s) + 2A{g^ + }(aq) \to N{i^{2 + }}(aq) + 2Ag(s){/tex} For the reaction n = 2, {tex} E_{cell}^\theta {/tex} = 1.05 V {tex}\Delta {G^\theta } = - nF{E^\theta }{/tex} {tex}\Delta {G^\theta } = - 2 \times 96500\,C \times 1.05\,V{/tex} {tex}\Delta {G^\theta } = - 202.65\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} For Equilibrium constant, we have, {tex}\Delta {G^\theta } = - 2.303\,RT\,\log \,{K_c}{/tex} {tex}\log {K_c} = - \frac{{\Delta {G^\Theta }}}{{2.303RT}}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{{202650}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}}{/tex} {tex}{K_c} = {\text{Antilog(35}}{\text{.5161)}}{/tex} {tex}{K_c} = 3.284 \times {10^{35}}{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex}Ni(s) + 2A{g^ + }(aq) \to N{i^{2 + }}(aq) + 2Ag(s){/tex} For the reaction n = 2, {tex} E_{cell}^\theta {/tex} = 1.05 V {tex}\Delta {G^\theta } = - nF{E^\theta }{/tex} {tex}\Delta {G^\theta } = - 2 \times 96500\,C \times 1.05\,V{/tex} {tex}\Delta {G^\theta } = - 202.65\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} For Equilibrium constant, we have, {tex}\Delta {G^\theta } = - 2.303\,RT\,\log \,{K_c}{/tex} {tex}\log {K_c} = - \frac{{\Delta {G^\Theta }}}{{2.303RT}}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{{202650}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}}{/tex} {tex}{K_c} = {\text{Antilog(35}}{\text{.5161)}}{/tex} {tex}{K_c} = 3.284 \times {10^{35}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}Ni(s) + 2A{g^ + }(aq) \to N{i^{2 + }}(aq) + 2Ag(s){/tex}
For the reaction n = 2,{tex} E_{cell}^\theta {/tex} = 1.05 V
{tex}\Delta {G^\theta } = - nF{E^\theta }{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {G^\theta } = - 2 \times 96500\,C \times 1.05\,V{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {G^\theta } = - 202.65\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
For Equilibrium constant, we have,
{tex}\Delta {G^\theta } = - 2.303\,RT\,\log \,{K_c}{/tex}
{tex}\log {K_c} = - \frac{{\Delta {G^\Theta }}}{{2.303RT}}{/tex}
{tex} = - \frac{{202650}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}}{/tex}
{tex}{K_c} = {\text{Antilog(35}}{\text{.5161)}}{/tex}
{tex}{K_c} = 3.284 \times {10^{35}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Express the relationship among the conductivity of a solution in the cell, the cell constant and the resistance of solution in the cell.
question_text: Express the relationship among the conductivity of a solution in the cell, the cell constant and the resistance of solution in the cell.
explanation: Relationship among the conductivity ( {tex}\kappa{/tex} ) of a solution in the cell, the cell constant (G * ) and the resistance (R) of solution in the cell:- We have {tex}G^\ast=\frac lA=R\kappa{/tex} where l = length of the cell and A = area of the plates of the electrolytic cell.
solution: Relationship among the conductivity ( {tex}\kappa{/tex} ) of a solution in the cell, the cell constant (G * ) and the resistance (R) of solution in the cell:- We have {tex}G^\ast=\frac lA=R\kappa{/tex} where l = length of the cell and A = area of the plates of the electrolytic cell.
solution_html:
Relationship among the conductivity ({tex}\kappa{/tex}) of a solution in the cell, the cell constant (G*) and the resistance (R) of solution in the cell:-

We have
{tex}G^\ast=\frac lA=R\kappa{/tex}
where l = length of the cell and A = area of the plates of the electrolytic cell.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What is the necessity to use a salt bridge in a Galvanic cell.
question_text: What is the necessity to use a salt bridge in a Galvanic cell.
explanation: The purpose of a salt bridge is not to move electrons from the electrolyte, rather it's to maintain charge balance because the electrons are moving from one-half cell to the other.
solution: The purpose of a salt bridge is not to move electrons from the electrolyte, rather it's to maintain charge balance because the electrons are moving from one-half cell to the other.
solution_html: The purpose of a salt bridge is not to move electrons from the electrolyte, rather it's to maintain charge balance because the electrons are moving from one-half cell to the other.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
How many moles of mercury will be produced by electrolysing 10 M Hg(NO3)2 solution with a current of 2.00 A for three hours?
[Hg(NO3)2 = 200.6gmol-1]
question_text: How many moles of mercury will be produced by electrolysing 10 M Hg(NO 3 ) 2 solution with a current of 2.00 A for three hours? [Hg(NO 3 ) 2 = 200.6gmol -1 ]
explanation: Given, current = 2A, Time = 3h = {tex}3 \times 60 \times 60 \mathrm{s}{/tex} m = Zlt = {tex}\frac{E}{96500} I t{/tex} = {tex}\frac{200.6 \times 2 \times 3 \times 60 \times 60}{2 \times 96500}{/tex} = 22.45 g Number of moles = {tex}\frac{22.45}{200.6}{/tex} = 0.112 mol
solution: Given, current = 2A, Time = 3h = {tex}3 \times 60 \times 60 \mathrm{s}{/tex} m = Zlt = {tex}\frac{E}{96500} I t{/tex} = {tex}\frac{200.6 \times 2 \times 3 \times 60 \times 60}{2 \times 96500}{/tex} = 22.45 g Number of moles = {tex}\frac{22.45}{200.6}{/tex} = 0.112 mol
solution_html:
Given, current = 2A,
Time = 3h = {tex}3 \times 60 \times 60 \mathrm{s}{/tex}
m = Zlt = {tex}\frac{E}{96500} I t{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{200.6 \times 2 \times 3 \times 60 \times 60}{2 \times 96500}{/tex}
= 22.45 g
Number of moles = {tex}\frac{22.45}{200.6}{/tex}  = 0.112 mol

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Write the formulation for the galvanic cell in which the reaction {tex}Cu(s) + 2A{g^ + }(aq) \to C{u^{2 + }}(aq) + 2Ag(s){/tex} takes place.
    Identify the cathode and the anode reactions in it.
  2. Write Nernst equation and calculate the emf of the following cell: Sn(s) | Sn2+(0.04M) || H+(0.02M) | H2(g)|Pt(s)
    {tex}\left(Given\;\;{E^\ominus}_{Sn^{2+}/Sn}=-0.14V\right){/tex}
question_text: Write the formulation for the galvanic cell in which the reaction {tex}Cu(s) + 2A{g^ + }(aq) \to C{u^{2 + }}(aq) + 2Ag(s){/tex} takes place. Identify the cathode and the anode reactions in it. Write Nernst equation and calculate the emf of the following cell: Sn(s) | Sn 2+ (0.04M) || H + (0.02M) | H 2 (g)|Pt(s) {tex}\left(Given\;\;{E^\ominus}_{Sn^{2+}/Sn}=-0.14V\right){/tex}
explanation: We have {tex}E_{(Cu^{2+}/Cu)}^\Theta=0.34V {/tex} and {tex}E_{(Ag^+/Ag)}^\Theta=0.80V {/tex} Standard emf of Cu is less than Ag, therefore it is strong reducing agent and is oxidised. Therefore Cu acts as Anode and Ag acts as Cathode. Half cell reactions are: At Cathode (Reduction): {tex}2Ag^+(aq)+2e^-\rightarrow2Ag(s){/tex} At Anode (Oxidation): {tex}Cu(s)\rightarrow Cu^{2+}(aq)+2e^- {/tex} The reactions are : At Anode: {tex}Sn(s) \to S{n^{2 + }}(aq) + 2{e^ - }{/tex} At Cathode: {tex}2H^+(aq)+2e^-\rightarrow H_2(g){/tex} Full cell reaction: {tex}Sn(s)+2H^+(aq)\rightarrow Sn^{2+}(aq)+H_2(g){/tex} Standard emf of the cell is: {tex}{E_{cell}^0 = E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^0 - E_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {S{n^{2 + }}/Sn} \end{array}}^0}{/tex} = 0 - (-0.14)V = + 0.14V For this reaction n=2 moles of electrons. Using Nernst equation, {tex}{E_{cell}} = 0.14 - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{{\left[ {S{n^2} + } \right]}}{{{{\left[ {{H^ + }} \right]}^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = 0.14 - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{{0.04}}{{{{\left( {0.02} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = 0.14- \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{4}{{100}} \times \frac{{100}}{2} \times \frac{{100}}{2}{/tex} = 0.14 V - 0.0591 V = 0.0809 V
solution: We have {tex}E_{(Cu^{2+}/Cu)}^\Theta=0.34V {/tex} and {tex}E_{(Ag^+/Ag)}^\Theta=0.80V {/tex} Standard emf of Cu is less than Ag, therefore it is strong reducing agent and is oxidised. Therefore Cu acts as Anode and Ag acts as Cathode. Half cell reactions are: At Cathode (Reduction): {tex}2Ag^+(aq)+2e^-\rightarrow2Ag(s){/tex} At Anode (Oxidation): {tex}Cu(s)\rightarrow Cu^{2+}(aq)+2e^- {/tex} The reactions are : At Anode: {tex}Sn(s) \to S{n^{2 + }}(aq) + 2{e^ - }{/tex} At Cathode: {tex}2H^+(aq)+2e^-\rightarrow H_2(g){/tex} Full cell reaction: {tex}Sn(s)+2H^+(aq)\rightarrow Sn^{2+}(aq)+H_2(g){/tex} Standard emf of the cell is: {tex}{E_{cell}^0 = E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^0 - E_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {S{n^{2 + }}/Sn} \end{array}}^0}{/tex} = 0 - (-0.14)V = + 0.14V For this reaction n=2 moles of electrons. Using Nernst equation, {tex}{E_{cell}} = 0.14 - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{{\left[ {S{n^2} + } \right]}}{{{{\left[ {{H^ + }} \right]}^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = 0.14 - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{{0.04}}{{{{\left( {0.02} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = 0.14- \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{4}{{100}} \times \frac{{100}}{2} \times \frac{{100}}{2}{/tex} = 0.14 V - 0.0591 V = 0.0809 V
solution_html:
1. We have
    {tex}E_{(Cu^{2+}/Cu)}^\Theta=0.34V {/tex} and {tex}E_{(Ag^+/Ag)}^\Theta=0.80V {/tex}
    Standard emf of Cu is less than Ag, therefore it is strong reducing agent and is oxidised. Therefore Cu acts as Anode and Ag acts as Cathode.
    Half cell reactions are:
    At Cathode (Reduction):
    {tex}2Ag^+(aq)+2e^-\rightarrow2Ag(s){/tex}
    At Anode (Oxidation):
    {tex}Cu(s)\rightarrow Cu^{2+}(aq)+2e^- {/tex}
  2. The reactions are :
    At Anode:
    {tex}Sn(s) \to S{n^{2 + }}(aq) + 2{e^ - }{/tex}
    At Cathode:
    {tex}2H^+(aq)+2e^-\rightarrow H_2(g){/tex}
    Full cell reaction:
    {tex}Sn(s)+2H^+(aq)\rightarrow Sn^{2+}(aq)+H_2(g){/tex}
    Standard emf of the cell is:
    {tex}{E_{cell}^0 = E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^0 - E_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {S{n^{2 + }}/Sn} \end{array}}^0}{/tex}
    = 0 - (-0.14)V
    = + 0.14V
    For this reaction n=2 moles of electrons. Using Nernst equation,
    {tex}{E_{cell}} = 0.14 - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{{\left[ {S{n^2} + } \right]}}{{{{\left[ {{H^ + }} \right]}^2}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 0.14 - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{{0.04}}{{{{\left( {0.02} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 0.14- \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{4}{{100}} \times \frac{{100}}{2} \times \frac{{100}}{2}{/tex}
    = 0.14 V - 0.0591 V
    = 0.0809 V

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the equilibrium constant, K, for the reaction at 298 K.
Zn(s) + Cu2+(aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Zn2+(aq)+ Cu (s)
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -212.300 kJ mol-1
[Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{{Zn}^{2+}}^{\circ} / \mathrm{Zn}}{/tex}= -0.76V, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = +0.34 V].
question_text: Calculate the equilibrium constant, K, for the reaction at 298 K. Zn(s) + Cu 2+ (aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq)+ Cu (s) {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}{/tex} = -212.300 kJ mol -1 [Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{{Zn}^{2+}}^{\circ} / \mathrm{Zn}}{/tex} = -0.76V, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = +0.34 V].
explanation: {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-2.303 R T \log K_{C}{/tex} {tex}-212300=-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \log K_{C}{/tex} {tex}\log K_{C}=\frac{212300}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}=37.2074{/tex} Kc = antilog 37.2074, K c = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 37 .
solution: {tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-2.303 R T \log K_{C}{/tex} {tex}-212300=-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \log K_{C}{/tex} {tex}\log K_{C}=\frac{212300}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}=37.2074{/tex} Kc = antilog 37.2074, K c = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 37 .
solution_html:
{tex}\Delta G^{\circ}=-2.303 R T \log K_{C}{/tex}
{tex}-212300=-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \log K_{C}{/tex}
{tex}\log K_{C}=\frac{212300}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}=37.2074{/tex}
Kc = antilog 37.2074, Kc = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1037.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Write the Nernst equation and compute the emf of the following cell at 298 K.
{tex}\left.\operatorname{Sn}(s)\right|_{(0.05 \mathrm{M})} ^{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}} \|_{(0.02 \mathrm{M})}^{\mathrm{H}}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}, 1 \mathrm{atm}\right| \mathrm{Pt}{/tex}
{tex}\left[E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Sn}}^{\circ}=-0.144 \mathrm{V}\right]{/tex}.
question_text: Write the Nernst equation and compute the emf of the following cell at 298 K. {tex}\left.\operatorname{Sn}(s)\right|_{(0.05 \mathrm{M})} ^{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}} \|_{(0.02 \mathrm{M})}^{\mathrm{H}}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}, 1 \mathrm{atm}\right| \mathrm{Pt}{/tex} {tex}\left[E_{\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Sn}}^{\circ}=-0.144 \mathrm{V}\right]{/tex} .
explanation: Cell reaction is Sn + 2H + {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Sn 2+ + H 2 ; (n = 2) E cell = E R - E L = 0 - (- 0.14) = 0.14 V According to Nernst equation, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591 \mathrm{V}}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} 0 {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}0.14 \mathrm{V}-\frac{0.0591 \mathrm{V}}{2} \log \frac{(0.05)}{(0.02)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}0.14 \mathrm{V}-\frac{0.0591 \mathrm{V} \times 2.0969}{2}=0.078 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
solution: Cell reaction is Sn + 2H + {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Sn 2+ + H 2 ; (n = 2) E cell = E R - E L = 0 - (- 0.14) = 0.14 V According to Nernst equation, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591 \mathrm{V}}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} 0 {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}0.14 \mathrm{V}-\frac{0.0591 \mathrm{V}}{2} \log \frac{(0.05)}{(0.02)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}0.14 \mathrm{V}-\frac{0.0591 \mathrm{V} \times 2.0969}{2}=0.078 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
solution_html:
Cell reaction is
Sn + 2H+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Sn2+ + H2; (n = 2)
Ecell = ER - EL = 0 - (- 0.14) = 0.14 V
According to Nernst equation,
{tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591 \mathrm{V}}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}0
{tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}0.14 \mathrm{V}-\frac{0.0591 \mathrm{V}}{2} \log \frac{(0.05)}{(0.02)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}0.14 \mathrm{V}-\frac{0.0591 \mathrm{V} \times 2.0969}{2}=0.078 \mathrm{V}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the degree of dissociation of acetic acid at 298 K, given that
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right){/tex} = 11.7 S cm2 mol-1
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{coo}^{-}\right){/tex} = 40.9 S cm2mol-1
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right){/tex}= 349.1 S cm2mol-1
question_text: Calculate the degree of dissociation of acetic acid at 298 K, given that {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right){/tex} = 11.7 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{coo}^{-}\right){/tex} = 40.9 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right){/tex} = 349.1 S cm 2 mol -1
explanation: Given, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)}{/tex} = 11.7 cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{coo}^{-}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} = 40.9 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} = 349.1S cm 2 mol -1 Degree of dissociation, {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right){/tex} = {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO^-}\right){/tex} + {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}_{\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right){/tex} = (40.9 + 349.1) = 390S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{m}}{\Lambda_{m}^{\circ}}=\frac{11.7}{390}=30 \times 10^{-2}{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)}{/tex} = 11.7 cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{coo}^{-}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} = 40.9 S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} = 349.1S cm 2 mol -1 Degree of dissociation, {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right){/tex} = {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO^-}\right){/tex} + {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}_{\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right){/tex} = (40.9 + 349.1) = 390S cm 2 mol -1 {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{m}}{\Lambda_{m}^{\circ}}=\frac{11.7}{390}=30 \times 10^{-2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)}{/tex}= 11.7 cm2mol-1
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{coo}^{-}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} = 40.9 S cm2mol-1
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{\circ}{/tex} = 349.1S cm2mol-1
Degree of dissociation, {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right){/tex} = {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO^-}\right){/tex} + {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}_{\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right){/tex} = (40.9 + 349.1) = 390S cm2mol-1
{tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{m}}{\Lambda_{m}^{\circ}}=\frac{11.7}{390}=30 \times 10^{-2}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
In the electrolysis of aqueous sodium bromide, there are two possible anodic reactions.
2H2O(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} O2(g) + 4H+(aq) + 4e-, Eo = 1.23V
2Br-(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Br2(g) + 2e-, Eo = 1.08V
Which reaction occurs at anode and why?
question_text: In the electrolysis of aqueous sodium bromide, there are two possible anodic reactions. 2H 2 O(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} O 2 (g) + 4H + (aq) + 4e-, E o = 1.23V 2Br-(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Br 2 (g) + 2e - , E o = 1.08V Which reaction occurs at anode and why?
explanation: In the electrolysis of NaBr, 2Br - (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Br 2 + 2e - occurs at anode because it has lower reduction potential, and liberation of O 2 requires over voltage.
solution: In the electrolysis of NaBr, 2Br - (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Br 2 + 2e - occurs at anode because it has lower reduction potential, and liberation of O 2 requires over voltage.
solution_html:
In the electrolysis of NaBr,
2Br-(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Br2 + 2e-
occurs at anode because it has lower reduction potential, and liberation of O2 requires over voltage.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Give an example of a fuel cell and write the cathode and anode reactions for it.
question_text: Give an example of a fuel cell and write the cathode and anode reactions for it.
explanation: H 2 -O 2 fuel cell: The cell consists of three compartments separated by a porous electrode. Hydrogen gas is introduced into one compartment and oxygen into another compartment. These gases then diffuse slowly through the electrode and react with an electrolyte that is in the central part of the cell. The electrodes are made of porous carbon and electrolyte is a resin containing concentrated aqueous sodium hydroxide solution. Hydrogen is oxidized at anode and oxygen is reduced at the cathode. At cathode: {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q){/tex} At anode: {tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q) \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-}{/tex} Overall reaction: {tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l){/tex}
solution: H 2 -O 2 fuel cell: The cell consists of three compartments separated by a porous electrode. Hydrogen gas is introduced into one compartment and oxygen into another compartment. These gases then diffuse slowly through the electrode and react with an electrolyte that is in the central part of the cell. The electrodes are made of porous carbon and electrolyte is a resin containing concentrated aqueous sodium hydroxide solution. Hydrogen is oxidized at anode and oxygen is reduced at the cathode. At cathode: {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q){/tex} At anode: {tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q) \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-}{/tex} Overall reaction: {tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l){/tex}
solution_html:
H2-O2 fuel cell: The cell consists of three compartments separated by a porous electrode. Hydrogen gas is introduced into one compartment and oxygen into another compartment. These gases then diffuse slowly through the electrode and react with an electrolyte that is in the central part of the cell. The electrodes are made of porous carbon and electrolyte is a resin containing concentrated aqueous sodium hydroxide solution. Hydrogen is oxidized at anode and oxygen is reduced at the cathode.
At cathode:
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}(g)+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q){/tex}
At anode:
{tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q) \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l)+4 e^{-}{/tex}
Overall reaction:
{tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(l){/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Mention the reactions occurring at

  1. anode
  2. cathode, during working of a mercury cell.

Why does the voltage of a mercury cell remain constant during its operation?
question_text: Mention the reactions occurring at anode cathode, during working of a mercury cell. Why does the voltage of a mercury cell remain constant during its operation?
explanation: Mercury cell Anode Zinc-mercury amalgam Cathode Paste of HgO and carbon Electrolyte Paste of KOH and ZnO Electrode reactions are as follows At anode {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 e^{-}{/tex} At cathode {tex}\mathrm{HgO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hg}(l)+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex} Overall reaction {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})+\mathrm{HgO}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}(s)+\mathrm{Hg}(l){/tex} The voltage of a mercury cell remains constant during its life as the overall reaction does not involve any ion m solution whose concentration can change during its life-time.
solution: Mercury cell Anode Zinc-mercury amalgam Cathode Paste of HgO and carbon Electrolyte Paste of KOH and ZnO Electrode reactions are as follows At anode {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 e^{-}{/tex} At cathode {tex}\mathrm{HgO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hg}(l)+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex} Overall reaction {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})+\mathrm{HgO}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}(s)+\mathrm{Hg}(l){/tex} The voltage of a mercury cell remains constant during its life as the overall reaction does not involve any ion m solution whose concentration can change during its life-time.
solution_html:
Mercury cell
Anode Zinc-mercury amalgam
Cathode Paste of HgO and carbon
Electrolyte Paste of KOH and ZnO
Electrode reactions are as follows
At anode
{tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 e^{-}{/tex}
At cathode
{tex}\mathrm{HgO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hg}(l)+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex}
Overall reaction
{tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{Hg})+\mathrm{HgO}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnO}(s)+\mathrm{Hg}(l){/tex}
The voltage of a mercury cell remains constant during its life as the overall reaction does not involve any ion m solution whose concentration can change during its life-time.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
An aqueous solution of copper sulphate, CuSO4 was electrolysed between platinum electrodes using a current of 0.1287 A for 50 min. [Given, atomic mass of Cu= 63.5 g mol-1]

  1. Write the cathodic reaction.
  2. Calculate
    1. Electric charge passed during electrolysis.
    2. Mass of copper deposited at the cathode. [Given, 1 F = 96500 Cmol-1].
question_text: An aqueous solution of copper sulphate, CuSO 4 was electrolysed between platinum electrodes using a current of 0.1287 A for 50 min. [Given, atomic mass of Cu= 63.5 g mol -1 ] Write the cathodic reaction. Calculate Electric charge passed during electrolysis. Mass of copper deposited at the cathode. [Given, 1 F = 96500 Cmol -1 ].
explanation: Given that, t = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 3000 s, I = 0.1287 A {tex}\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}(s){/tex} Q = It = 0.1287 {tex}\times{/tex} 3000 = 386.1 C m = Z {tex}\times{/tex} I {tex}\times{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{\text { Equivalent weight }}{96500}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} I {tex}\times{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{63.5}{2 \times 96500}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 386.1 = 0.127 g

question_html:
For the cell,
{tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(2 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(0.5 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s}){/tex}

  1. Write the equation for each half reaction.
  2. Calculate the cell potential at 25°C.

[Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex}= -0.76 V , {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0.34 V]
question_text: For the cell, {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(2 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(0.5 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s}){/tex} Write the equation for each half reaction. Calculate the cell potential at 25°C. [Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.76 V , {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0.34 V]
explanation: For the cell; Zn(s)∣Zn 2+ (2M)∥Cu 2+ (0.5M)∣Cu(s). The cell reactions are: Oxidation half cell reaction; Zn(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{}{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}}(a q){/tex} + 2e - (At Anode) Reduction half cell reaction; {tex}{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(a q)}{/tex} + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu(s) (At Cathode) {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{right}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{left}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.34 V- (- 0.76 V) = 1.10 V By applying Nernst equation; {tex}E_{\text { cell }}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}=1.10 -\frac{0.0591 }{2} \log \frac{2}{0.5}{/tex} {tex}E_{\text { cell }}{/tex} = {tex}1.10 -\frac{0.0591 }{2} \times 0.6021{/tex} {tex}E_{\text { cell }}{/tex} = 1.10 - 0.0178 = 1.0822 V
solution: For the cell; Zn(s)∣Zn 2+ (2M)∥Cu 2+ (0.5M)∣Cu(s). The cell reactions are: Oxidation half cell reaction; Zn(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{}{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}}(a q){/tex} + 2e - (At Anode) Reduction half cell reaction; {tex}{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(a q)}{/tex} + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu(s) (At Cathode) {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{right}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{left}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.34 V- (- 0.76 V) = 1.10 V By applying Nernst equation; {tex}E_{\text { cell }}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}=1.10 -\frac{0.0591 }{2} \log \frac{2}{0.5}{/tex} {tex}E_{\text { cell }}{/tex} = {tex}1.10 -\frac{0.0591 }{2} \times 0.6021{/tex} {tex}E_{\text { cell }}{/tex} = 1.10 - 0.0178 = 1.0822 V
solution_html:
1. For the cell; Zn(s)∣Zn2+(2M)∥Cu2+(0.5M)∣Cu(s).
    The cell reactions are:
    Oxidation half cell reaction; Zn(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{}{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}}(a q){/tex} + 2e-  (At Anode)
    Reduction half cell reaction; {tex}{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(a q)}{/tex} + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu(s) (At Cathode)
  2. {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{right}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{left}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}
    {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex}= 0.34 V- (- 0.76 V) = 1.10 V
    By applying Nernst equation; 
    {tex}E_{\text { cell }}{/tex}= {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex}
    {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}=1.10 -\frac{0.0591 }{2} \log \frac{2}{0.5}{/tex}

    {tex}E_{\text { cell }}{/tex} = 1.10 - 0.0178 = 1.0822 V

subject: Chemistry
question_html: State Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions. How can the degree of dissociation of acetic acid in a solution be calculated from its molar conductivity data?
question_text: State Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions. How can the degree of dissociation of acetic acid in a solution be calculated from its molar conductivity data?
explanation: Kohlrausch law of independent migration of ions: It states that limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. If {tex}{\lambda ^o}N{a^ + }{/tex} and {tex}{\lambda ^o}C{l^ - }{/tex} are limiting molar conductivity for sodium chloride is given by {tex}\lambda _m^0\left( {NaCl} \right) = {\lambda ^o}Na + {\lambda ^0}C{l^ - }{/tex} Calculation of degree of dissociation of weak electrolyte like acetic acid. The degree of dissociation {tex}\alpha {/tex} is given by: {tex}\alpha = \frac{{{\lambda _m}}}{{\lambda _m^0}}{/tex} where {tex}{{\lambda _m}}{/tex} be molar conductivity and {tex}{\lambda _m^0}{/tex} be the limiting molar conductivity.
solution: Kohlrausch law of independent migration of ions: It states that limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. If {tex}{\lambda ^o}N{a^ + }{/tex} and {tex}{\lambda ^o}C{l^ - }{/tex} are limiting molar conductivity for sodium chloride is given by {tex}\lambda _m^0\left( {NaCl} \right) = {\lambda ^o}Na + {\lambda ^0}C{l^ - }{/tex} Calculation of degree of dissociation of weak electrolyte like acetic acid. The degree of dissociation {tex}\alpha {/tex} is given by: {tex}\alpha = \frac{{{\lambda _m}}}{{\lambda _m^0}}{/tex} where {tex}{{\lambda _m}}{/tex} be molar conductivity and {tex}{\lambda _m^0}{/tex} be the limiting molar conductivity.
solution_html:
Kohlrausch law of independent migration of ions: It states that limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. If {tex}{\lambda ^o}N{a^ + }{/tex} and {tex}{\lambda ^o}C{l^ - }{/tex} are limiting molar conductivity for sodium chloride is given by
{tex}\lambda _m^0\left( {NaCl} \right) = {\lambda ^o}Na + {\lambda ^0}C{l^ - }{/tex}
Calculation of degree of dissociation of weak electrolyte like acetic acid. The degree of dissociation {tex}\alpha {/tex} is given by:
{tex}\alpha = \frac{{{\lambda _m}}}{{\lambda _m^0}}{/tex}
where {tex}{{\lambda _m}}{/tex} be molar conductivity and {tex}{\lambda _m^0}{/tex} be the limiting molar conductivity.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A copper-silver cell is set-up. The copper ion concentration is 0.10 M. The concentration of silver ion is not known. The cell potential when measured was 0.422 V. Determine the concentration of silver ions in the cell.
{tex}[Given,\,E_{A{g^ + }/Ag}^\Theta = + 0.80\;V,{/tex} {tex}E_{C{u^{2 + }}/Cu}^\Theta = - 0.34\;V]{/tex}
question_text: A copper-silver cell is set-up. The copper ion concentration is 0.10 M. The concentration of silver ion is not known. The cell potential when measured was 0.422 V. Determine the concentration of silver ions in the cell. {tex}[Given,\,E_{A{g^ + }/Ag}^\Theta = + 0.80\;V,{/tex} {tex}E_{C{u^{2 + }}/Cu}^\Theta = - 0.34\;V]{/tex}
explanation: Cell Reaction is {tex}Cu(s)\;+\;2Ag^+(aq)\;\rightarrow\;Cu^{2+}(aq)+2Ag(s){/tex} Half cell reaction of this reaction is: At Cathode (reduction): {tex}2Ag^+(aq)+2e^-\rightarrow 2Ag(s){/tex} At Anode (oxidation): {tex}Cu(s) \rightarrow Cu^{2+}(aq) +2e^-{/tex} for this reaction, n=2 moles of electrons takes place. Now, {tex}E_{cell}^\theta = E_R^\theta - E_L^\theta{/tex} = +0.80 - (+0.34) = 0.46 V From Nernst Equation, {tex}{E_{cell}} = E_{cell}^\Theta - \frac{{0.0591V}}{n}\log \frac{{\left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}}{/tex} for n = 2, {tex}0.422V = 0.46V - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{{\left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{{\left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}} = \frac{{0.46 - 0.422}}{{0.0295}} = 1.288{/tex} {tex}\frac{{\left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}} = {\text{Antilog}}\,\,1.288 = 19.41{/tex} {tex}\frac{{0.10}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}} = 19.41 \Rightarrow {\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]^2} = 0.00515{/tex} {tex}\left[Ag^+\right]=0.0717=7.17\times10^{-2}M.{/tex}
solution: Cell Reaction is {tex}Cu(s)\;+\;2Ag^+(aq)\;\rightarrow\;Cu^{2+}(aq)+2Ag(s){/tex} Half cell reaction of this reaction is: At Cathode (reduction): {tex}2Ag^+(aq)+2e^-\rightarrow 2Ag(s){/tex} At Anode (oxidation): {tex}Cu(s) \rightarrow Cu^{2+}(aq) +2e^-{/tex} for this reaction, n=2 moles of electrons takes place. Now, {tex}E_{cell}^\theta = E_R^\theta - E_L^\theta{/tex} = +0.80 - (+0.34) = 0.46 V From Nernst Equation, {tex}{E_{cell}} = E_{cell}^\Theta - \frac{{0.0591V}}{n}\log \frac{{\left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}}{/tex} for n = 2, {tex}0.422V = 0.46V - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{{\left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{{\left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}} = \frac{{0.46 - 0.422}}{{0.0295}} = 1.288{/tex} {tex}\frac{{\left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}} = {\text{Antilog}}\,\,1.288 = 19.41{/tex} {tex}\frac{{0.10}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}} = 19.41 \Rightarrow {\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]^2} = 0.00515{/tex} {tex}\left[Ag^+\right]=0.0717=7.17\times10^{-2}M.{/tex}
solution_html:
Cell Reaction is
{tex}Cu(s)\;+\;2Ag^+(aq)\;\rightarrow\;Cu^{2+}(aq)+2Ag(s){/tex}

Half cell reaction of this reaction is:
At Cathode (reduction):
{tex}2Ag^+(aq)+2e^-\rightarrow 2Ag(s){/tex}
At Anode (oxidation):
{tex}Cu(s) \rightarrow Cu^{2+}(aq) +2e^-{/tex}
for this reaction, n=2 moles of electrons takes place.
Now,
{tex}E_{cell}^\theta = E_R^\theta - E_L^\theta{/tex}
= +0.80 - (+0.34) = 0.46 V

From Nernst Equation,
{tex}{E_{cell}} = E_{cell}^\Theta - \frac{{0.0591V}}{n}\log \frac{{\left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}}{/tex}
for n = 2,

{tex}0.422V = 0.46V - \frac{{0.0591}}{2}\log \frac{{\left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}\log \frac{{\left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}} = \frac{{0.46 - 0.422}}{{0.0295}} = 1.288{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{\left[ {C{u^{2 + }}} \right]}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}} = {\text{Antilog}}\,\,1.288 = 19.41{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{0.10}}{{{{\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]}^2}}} = 19.41 \Rightarrow {\left[ {A{g^ + }} \right]^2} = 0.00515{/tex}

{tex}\left[Ag^+\right]=0.0717=7.17\times10^{-2}M.{/tex}

question_html:
Calculate the emf of the cell in which the following reaction takes place:
{tex}Ni(s)+2Ag^+(0.002M)\rightarrow Ni^{2+}(0.160M)+2Ag(s){/tex}
Given that {tex}{\mathrm E^\mathrm o}_\mathrm{Cell}=1.05\mathrm V{/tex}
question_text: Calculate the emf of the cell in which the following reaction takes place: {tex}Ni(s)+2Ag^+(0.002M)\rightarrow Ni^{2+}(0.160M)+2Ag(s){/tex} Given that {tex}{\mathrm E^\mathrm o}_\mathrm{Cell}=1.05\mathrm V{/tex}
explanation: Given, [Ag + ] = 0.002 M [Ni 2+ ] = 0.160 M n = 2 According to Nernst equation: E cell = {tex}\mathrm{E}^0-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} E cell = {tex}1.05-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.160}{(0.002)^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} E cell = 1.05 - 0.02955 log (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} E cell = 0.91 V
solution: Given, [Ag + ] = 0.002 M [Ni 2+ ] = 0.160 M n = 2 According to Nernst equation: E cell = {tex}\mathrm{E}^0-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} E cell = {tex}1.05-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.160}{(0.002)^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} E cell = 1.05 - 0.02955 log (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} E cell = 0.91 V
solution_html:
Given,
[Ag+] = 0.002 M
[Ni2+] = 0.160 M
n = 2
According to Nernst equation:
Ecell = {tex}\mathrm{E}^0-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ecell = {tex}1.05-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.160}{(0.002)^2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ecell = 1.05 - 0.02955 log (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 104)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ecell = 0.91 V

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What is meant by limiting molar conductivity?
question_text: What is meant by limiting molar conductivity?
explanation: Limiting molar conductivity ( {tex}Ʌ_m^\circ{/tex} ):- Limiting molar conductivity ( {tex}Ʌ_m^\circ{/tex} ) is the molar conductivity of an electrolyte when concentration approaches zero. For strong electrolytes, its relationship with molar conductivity ( {tex}Ʌ_m{/tex} ) is given by Debye Huckel Onsager equation. {tex}Ʌ_m = Ʌ_m^\circ - A c^{1/2}{/tex} For weak electrolytes, its relationship with molar conductivity ( {tex}Ʌ_m{/tex} ), degree of dissociation, concentration and dissociation constant is given by: {tex}\alpha=\frac{Ʌ_m}{Ʌ_m^\circ}{/tex} {tex}K_a=\frac{cɅ_m^2}{Ʌ_m^օ(Ʌ_m^օ-\Lambda_m)}{/tex}
solution: Limiting molar conductivity ( {tex}Ʌ_m^\circ{/tex} ):- Limiting molar conductivity ( {tex}Ʌ_m^\circ{/tex} ) is the molar conductivity of an electrolyte when concentration approaches zero. For strong electrolytes, its relationship with molar conductivity ( {tex}Ʌ_m{/tex} ) is given by Debye Huckel Onsager equation. {tex}Ʌ_m = Ʌ_m^\circ - A c^{1/2}{/tex} For weak electrolytes, its relationship with molar conductivity ( {tex}Ʌ_m{/tex} ), degree of dissociation, concentration and dissociation constant is given by: {tex}\alpha=\frac{Ʌ_m}{Ʌ_m^\circ}{/tex} {tex}K_a=\frac{cɅ_m^2}{Ʌ_m^օ(Ʌ_m^օ-\Lambda_m)}{/tex}
solution_html:
Limiting molar conductivity ({tex}Ʌ_m^\circ{/tex}):- Limiting molar conductivity ({tex}Ʌ_m^\circ{/tex}) is the molar conductivity of an electrolyte when concentration approaches zero.
For strong electrolytes, its relationship with molar conductivity ({tex}Ʌ_m{/tex}) is given by Debye Huckel Onsager equation.
{tex}Ʌ_m = Ʌ_m^\circ - A c^{1/2}{/tex}
For weak electrolytes, its relationship with molar conductivity ({tex}Ʌ_m{/tex}), degree of dissociation, concentration and dissociation constant is given by:
{tex}\alpha=\frac{Ʌ_m}{Ʌ_m^\circ}{/tex}
{tex}K_a=\frac{cɅ_m^2}{Ʌ_m^օ(Ʌ_m^օ-\Lambda_m)}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Define conductivity and molar conductivity for the solution of an electrolyte. Discuss their variation with concentration.
question_text: Define conductivity and molar conductivity for the solution of an electrolyte. Discuss their variation with concentration.
explanation: Both conductivity and molar conductivity change with the concentration of the electrolyte. Conductivity always decreases with decrease in concentration both, for weak and strong electrolytes. This can be explained by the fact that the number of ions per unit volume that carry the current in a solution decreases on dilution. The conductivity of a solution at any given concentration is the conductance of one unit volume of solution kept between two platinum electrodes with unit area of cross section and at a distance of unit length. This is clear from the equation: {tex}G = \frac{\kappa A}l= \kappa{/tex} (both A and l are unity in their appropriate units in m or cm) Molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is the conductance of the volume V of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with area of cross section A and distance of unit length. Since l = 1 and A = V (Volume containing 1 mole of electrolyte). Therefore, {tex}\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa A}l= \kappa V{/tex} Molar conductivity increases with decrease in concentration. This is because the total volume, V, of solution containing one mole of electrolyte also increases. It has been found that decrease in {tex}\kappa{/tex} on dilution of a solution is more than compensated by increase in its volume. Physically, it means that at a given concentration, {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} can be defined as the conductance of the electrolytic solution kept between the electrodes of a conductivity cell at unit distance but having area of cross section large enough to accommodate sufficient volume of solution that contains one mole of the electrolyte. When concentration approaches zero, the molar conductivity is known as limiting molar conductivity and is represented by the symbol {tex}\Lambda_m^\circ\;{/tex} . The variation in {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} with concentration is different for strong and weak electrolytes as shown in the figure. For strong electrolytes, {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} increases slowly with dilution and can be represented by the equation: {tex}Ʌ_m = Ʌ_m^\circ - A c^{1/2}{/tex} Weak electrolytes like acetic acid have lower degree of dissociation at higher concentrations and hence for such electrolytes, the change in {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} with dilution is due to increase in the degree of dissociation and consequently the number of ions in total volume of solution that contains 1 mol of electrolyte. In such cases {tex}\Lambda_m{/tex} increases steeply on dilution, especially near lower concentrations as shown in the given figure above.

Since l = 1 and A = V (Volume containing 1 mole of electrolyte). Therefore,

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Arrange the given elements with increasing order of their reducing power:
Ag, Cu, Fe, K, Mg, Cr
(Given: {tex}E_{(Ag^+/Ag)}^\Theta = 0.80\;V{/tex}, {tex}E_{(Cu^{2+}/Cu)}^\Theta = 0.34\;V{/tex}, {tex}E_{(Fe^{2+}/Fe)}^\Theta = 0.44\;V{/tex}, {tex}E_{(K^+/K)}^\Theta = -2.93\;V{/tex}, {tex}E_{(Mg^{2+}/Mg)}^\Theta = -2.36\;V{/tex}, {tex}E_{(Cr^{3+}/Cr)}^\Theta = -0.74\;V{/tex})
