subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. What is Nickel Cadmium cell? State its one merit over lead storage cell. Write the overall reaction that occurs during discharging of this cell.
  2. Silver is electro deposited on a metallic vessel of total surface area 900 cm2 by passing a current of 0.5 ampere for 2 hours.
    Calculate the thickness of silver deposited, given its density is 10.5gcm-3. (At. mass of Ag = 108 g mol-1).
question_text: What is Nickel Cadmium cell? State its one merit over lead storage cell. Write the overall reaction that occurs during discharging of this cell. Silver is electro deposited on a metallic vessel of total surface area 900 cm 2 by passing a current of 0.5 ampere for 2 hours. Calculate the thickness of silver deposited, given its density is 10.5gcm -3 . (At. mass of Ag = 108 g mol -1 ).
explanation: Nickel Cadmium cell: It is a rechargeable cell. It consists of a cadmium anode and a metal grid containing NiO 2 acting as a cathode as shown in the figure. The electrolyte in this cell is KOH. The reaction taking place during discharging and charging are shown in the figure. 1-Ni-Cd (Nickel Cadmium battery) Battery reactions: At the negative {tex}Cd + 2OH\underset{{disch\arg e}}{\overset{{ch\arg e}}{\longleftrightarrow}}Cd{(OH)_2} + 2e - {/tex} At the positive {tex}NiOOH + {H_2}O + 2e - \underset{{disch\arg e}}{\overset{{ch\arg e}}{\longleftrightarrow}}Ni{(OH)_2} + OH{/tex} Overall reaction {tex}2NiOOH + Cd + 2{H_2}O\underset{{disch\arg e}}{\overset{{ch\arg e}}{\longleftrightarrow}}2Ni{(OH)_2} + Cd{(OH)_2}{/tex} In these reactions, there is no formation of gaseous products. The reaction products generally remain sticking to the electrodes and can be reconverted by recharging the cell. The charging process is similar to lead storage battery. It produces a potential of about 1.4 V. It has longer life than the lead storage cell but more expensive than lead storage battery. However, it has some advantages because it is smaller and lighter. It can be used in portable and cordless appliances. We have, {tex}A{g^ + }(aq) + {e^ - } \to Ag(s){/tex} Thus, quantity of electricity required to deposit 1 mole of Ag(108 g) {tex}Q=nF=1mole\times96500\,C\,mol^{-1}{/tex} {tex} = 96500\,C = 9.65 \times {10^4}C{/tex} Quantity of electricity actually passed = Current in amperes × Time in seconds {tex}=0.5A\times\left(2\times60\times60\right)s{/tex} = 3600 C {tex}\therefore{/tex} 3600C of charge produce silver {tex}=\frac{108g}{9.65\times10^4C}\times3600C\;=4.03\;g{/tex} Volume of silver deposited {tex}=\frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Density}}=\left(\frac{4.03g}{10.5\,g\,cm^{-3}}\right)=0.384\;cm^3{/tex} Therefore,thickness of the silver deposited {tex} = \frac{{{\text{Volume}}}}{{{\text{Surface}}\,{\text{Area}}}} = \frac{{\left( {0.384\,c{m^3}} \right)}}{{\left( {900\,c{m^2}} \right)}} {/tex} {tex}= 4.27 \times {10^{ - 4}}\;cm{/tex}
solution: Nickel Cadmium cell: It is a rechargeable cell. It consists of a cadmium anode and a metal grid containing NiO 2 acting as a cathode as shown in the figure. The electrolyte in this cell is KOH. The reaction taking place during discharging and charging are shown in the figure. 1-Ni-Cd (Nickel Cadmium battery) Battery reactions: At the negative {tex}Cd + 2OH\underset{{disch\arg e}}{\overset{{ch\arg e}}{\longleftrightarrow}}Cd{(OH)_2} + 2e - {/tex} At the positive {tex}NiOOH + {H_2}O + 2e - \underset{{disch\arg e}}{\overset{{ch\arg e}}{\longleftrightarrow}}Ni{(OH)_2} + OH{/tex} Overall reaction {tex}2NiOOH + Cd + 2{H_2}O\underset{{disch\arg e}}{\overset{{ch\arg e}}{\longleftrightarrow}}2Ni{(OH)_2} + Cd{(OH)_2}{/tex} In these reactions, there is no formation of gaseous products. The reaction products generally remain sticking to the electrodes and can be reconverted by recharging the cell. The charging process is similar to lead storage battery. It produces a potential of about 1.4 V. It has longer life than the lead storage cell but more expensive than lead storage battery. However, it has some advantages because it is smaller and lighter. It can be used in portable and cordless appliances. We have, {tex}A{g^ + }(aq) + {e^ - } \to Ag(s){/tex} Thus, quantity of electricity required to deposit 1 mole of Ag(108 g) {tex}Q=nF=1mole\times96500\,C\,mol^{-1}{/tex} {tex} = 96500\,C = 9.65 \times {10^4}C{/tex} Quantity of electricity actually passed = Current in amperes × Time in seconds {tex}=0.5A\times\left(2\times60\times60\right)s{/tex} = 3600 C {tex}\therefore{/tex} 3600C of charge produce silver {tex}=\frac{108g}{9.65\times10^4C}\times3600C\;=4.03\;g{/tex} Volume of silver deposited {tex}=\frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Density}}=\left(\frac{4.03g}{10.5\,g\,cm^{-3}}\right)=0.384\;cm^3{/tex} Therefore,thickness of the silver deposited {tex} = \frac{{{\text{Volume}}}}{{{\text{Surface}}\,{\text{Area}}}} = \frac{{\left( {0.384\,c{m^3}} \right)}}{{\left( {900\,c{m^2}} \right)}} {/tex} {tex}= 4.27 \times {10^{ - 4}}\;cm{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Nickel Cadmium cell:
    It is a rechargeable cell. It consists of a cadmium anode and a metal grid containing NiO2 acting as a cathode as shown in the figure. The electrolyte in this cell is KOH. The reaction taking place during discharging and charging are shown in the figure.

    1-Ni-Cd (Nickel Cadmium battery)
    Battery reactions:
    At the negative
    {tex}Cd + 2OH\underset{{disch\arg e}}{\overset{{ch\arg e}}{\longleftrightarrow}}Cd{(OH)_2} + 2e - {/tex}
    At the positive
    {tex}NiOOH + {H_2}O + 2e - \underset{{disch\arg e}}{\overset{{ch\arg e}}{\longleftrightarrow}}Ni{(OH)_2} + OH{/tex}
    Overall reaction
    {tex}2NiOOH + Cd + 2{H_2}O\underset{{disch\arg e}}{\overset{{ch\arg e}}{\longleftrightarrow}}2Ni{(OH)_2} + Cd{(OH)_2}{/tex}
    In these reactions, there is no formation of gaseous products. The reaction products generally remain sticking to the electrodes and can be reconverted by recharging the cell. The charging process is similar to lead storage battery.
    It produces a potential of about 1.4 V. It has longer life than the lead storage cell but more expensive than lead storage battery. However, it has some advantages because it is smaller and lighter. It can be used in portable and cordless appliances.
  2. We have,
    {tex}A{g^ + }(aq) + {e^ - } \to Ag(s){/tex}
    Thus, quantity of electricity required to deposit 1 mole of Ag(108 g)
    {tex}Q=nF=1mole\times96500\,C\,mol^{-1}{/tex}
    {tex} = 96500\,C = 9.65 \times {10^4}C{/tex}
    Quantity of electricity actually passed = Current in amperes × Time in seconds
    {tex}=0.5A\times\left(2\times60\times60\right)s{/tex}
    = 3600 C
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} 3600C of charge produce silver {tex}=\frac{108g}{9.65\times10^4C}\times3600C\;=4.03\;g{/tex}
    Volume of silver deposited {tex}=\frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Density}}=\left(\frac{4.03g}{10.5\,g\,cm^{-3}}\right)=0.384\;cm^3{/tex}
    Therefore,thickness of the silver deposited {tex} = \frac{{{\text{Volume}}}}{{{\text{Surface}}\,{\text{Area}}}} = \frac{{\left( {0.384\,c{m^3}} \right)}}{{\left( {900\,c{m^2}} \right)}} {/tex} {tex}= 4.27 \times {10^{ - 4}}\;cm{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} for acetic acid,
Given that,
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex}(HCI) = 426S cm2 ml-1
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex}(NaCl)=126S cm2 ml-1
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex}(CH3COONa) = 91S cm2 ml-1
question_text: Calculate {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} for acetic acid, Given that, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} (HCI) = 426S cm 2 ml -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} (NaCl)=126S cm 2 ml -1 {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} (CH 3 COONa) = 91S cm 2 ml -1
explanation: Given, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\circ}=426 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaCl})}^{\mathrm{o}}=126 \mathrm{Scm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right)}^{\circ}=91 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{cooH}\right)=\Lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{Na}^{+}}^{\circ}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{coona}\right)-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaCl})}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = (426 + 91 - 126)S cm 2 mol -1 = 391S cm 2 mol -1
solution: Given, {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\circ}=426 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaCl})}^{\mathrm{o}}=126 \mathrm{Scm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right)}^{\circ}=91 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{cooH}\right)=\Lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{Na}^{+}}^{\circ}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}}^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{coona}\right)-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaCl})}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = (426 + 91 - 126)S cm 2 mol -1 = 391S cm 2 mol -1
solution_html:
Given,
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\circ}=426 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaCl})}^{\mathrm{o}}=126 \mathrm{Scm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right)}^{\circ}=91 \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{cooH}\right)=\Lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{Na}^{+}}^{\circ}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{HCl})}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{coona}\right)-\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{NaCl})}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}
= (426 + 91 - 126)S cm2mol-1
= 391S cm2mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Write the anode and cathode reactions occurring in a commonly used mercury cells. How is the overall reaction represented?
question_text: Write the anode and cathode reactions occurring in a commonly used mercury cells. How is the overall reaction represented?
explanation: Mercury cell is a primary cell suitable for low current devices like hearing aids, electronic watches etc. It consists of zinc – mercury amalgam as anode and a paste of HgO and carbon as the cathode. The electrolyte is a paste of ZnO and KOH. The cell process is given below: At anode: Zn(Hg) + 2OH - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} ZnO(s) + H 2 O + 2e - At cathode: HgO(s) + H 2 O + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Hg(l) + 2OH - Overall reaction: Zn(Hg) + HgO(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} ZnO(s) + Hg(l) The voltage of a mercury cell remains constant (approximately 1.35V) during its life as the overall reaction does not involve any ion in solution whose concentration can change its lifetime.
solution: Mercury cell is a primary cell suitable for low current devices like hearing aids, electronic watches etc. It consists of zinc – mercury amalgam as anode and a paste of HgO and carbon as the cathode. The electrolyte is a paste of ZnO and KOH. The cell process is given below: At anode: Zn(Hg) + 2OH - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} ZnO(s) + H 2 O + 2e - At cathode: HgO(s) + H 2 O + 2e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Hg(l) + 2OH - Overall reaction: Zn(Hg) + HgO(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} ZnO(s) + Hg(l) The voltage of a mercury cell remains constant (approximately 1.35V) during its life as the overall reaction does not involve any ion in solution whose concentration can change its lifetime.
solution_html:
Mercury cell is a primary cell suitable for low current devices like hearing aids, electronic watches etc. It consists of zinc – mercury amalgam as anode and a paste of HgO and carbon as the cathode. The electrolyte is a paste of ZnO and KOH.
The cell process is given below: 
At anode:
Zn(Hg) + 2OH- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} ZnO(s) + H2O + 2e-
At cathode:
HgO(s) + H2O + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Hg(l) + 2OH-
Overall reaction:
Zn(Hg) + HgO(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} ZnO(s) + Hg(l)
The voltage of a mercury cell remains constant (approximately 1.35V) during its life as the overall reaction does not involve any ion in solution whose concentration can change its lifetime.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: State and explain Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions. Write an expression for the molar conductivity of acetic acid at infinite dilution according to Kohlrausch's law.
question_text: State and explain Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions. Write an expression for the molar conductivity of acetic acid at infinite dilution according to Kohlrausch's law.
explanation: Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions states that the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be expressed as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and the cation of the electrolyte, e.g. {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{cooH}\right)=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex} The conductivity of a solution is related to the number of ions present per unit volume of the solution. When the solution is diluted, the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Hence, conductivity or specific conductance of the solution decreases.
solution: Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions states that the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be expressed as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and the cation of the electrolyte, e.g. {tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{cooH}\right)=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex} The conductivity of a solution is related to the number of ions present per unit volume of the solution. When the solution is diluted, the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Hence, conductivity or specific conductance of the solution decreases.
solution_html:
Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions states that the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be expressed as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and the cation of the electrolyte, e.g.
{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{cooH}\right)=\lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^{\circ}+\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}{/tex}
The conductivity of a solution is related to the number of ions present per unit volume of the solution. When the solution is diluted, the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Hence, conductivity or specific conductance of the solution decreases.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
One half-cell in a voltaic cell is constructed from a silver wire dipped in silver nitrate solution of unknown concentration. The other half-cell consists of a zinc electrode in 1.0 M solution of Zn(NO3)2. A voltage of 1.48 V is measured for this cell. Use this information to calculate the concentration of silver nitrate solution.
[{tex}\text { Given, } E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}= -0.763 V{/tex} and {tex}E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}=+0.80 \mathrm{V}{/tex}].
question_text: One half-cell in a voltaic cell is constructed from a silver wire dipped in silver nitrate solution of unknown concentration. The other half-cell consists of a zinc electrode in 1.0 M solution of Zn(NO 3 ) 2 . A voltage of 1.48 V is measured for this cell. Use this information to calculate the concentration of silver nitrate solution. [ {tex}\text { Given, } E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}= -0.763 V{/tex} and {tex}E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}=+0.80 \mathrm{V}{/tex} ].
explanation: It is clear that, {tex}E_{Z \mathrm{n}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}=-0.763 \mathrm{V} < E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}=+0.80 \mathrm{V}{/tex} Therefore, cell can be represented as Zn(s) | Zn 2+ (aq) (1M) II Ag + (aq) | Ag(s) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E_{\text { Cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\text { Cathode }}^{\circ}-E_{\text { Anode }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.80 - (-0.76) = 1.56 ; E cell = 1.48V (given) By applying Nernst equation, we get E cell = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} 1.48 = {tex}1.56-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} 0.08 = {tex}0.0295 \log \frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{0.08}{0.0295}=-2 \log \left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]{/tex} {tex}-\frac{0.08}{0.0295 \times 2}=\log \left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]{/tex} log[Ag + ] = -1.356 [Ag + ] = Antilog [-1.356] Therefore, [Ag + ] = 0.0441 = 4.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 M
solution: It is clear that, {tex}E_{Z \mathrm{n}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}=-0.763 \mathrm{V} < E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}=+0.80 \mathrm{V}{/tex} Therefore, cell can be represented as Zn(s) | Zn 2+ (aq) (1M) II Ag + (aq) | Ag(s) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E_{\text { Cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\text { Cathode }}^{\circ}-E_{\text { Anode }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.80 - (-0.76) = 1.56 ; E cell = 1.48V (given) By applying Nernst equation, we get E cell = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} 1.48 = {tex}1.56-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} 0.08 = {tex}0.0295 \log \frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{0.08}{0.0295}=-2 \log \left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]{/tex} {tex}-\frac{0.08}{0.0295 \times 2}=\log \left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]{/tex} log[Ag + ] = -1.356 [Ag + ] = Antilog [-1.356] Therefore, [Ag + ] = 0.0441 = 4.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 M
solution_html:
It is clear that, {tex}E_{Z \mathrm{n}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}=-0.763 \mathrm{V} < E_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}=+0.80 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
Therefore, cell can be represented as Zn(s) | Zn2+(aq) (1M) II Ag+(aq) | Ag(s)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E_{\text { Cell }}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\text { Cathode }}^{\circ}-E_{\text { Anode }}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.80 - (-0.76) = 1.56 ; Ecell = 1.48V  (given)
By applying Nernst equation, we get
Ecell = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
1.48 = {tex}1.56-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
0.08 = {tex}0.0295 \log \frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{0.08}{0.0295}=-2 \log \left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]{/tex}
{tex}-\frac{0.08}{0.0295 \times 2}=\log \left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]{/tex}
log[Ag+] = -1.356
[Ag+] = Antilog [-1.356]
Therefore, [Ag+] = 0.0441 = 4.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 M

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate {tex}{E}_{cell}{/tex} for following:
{tex}2Cr(s) + 3{Fe}^{2+}(aq) \to 2{Cr}^{3+}(aq) +3Fe(s){/tex}
{tex}Cr(s)| {Cr}^{3+} (aq)(0.1 M) || {Fe}^{2+} (aq)(0.01 M) | Fe(s){/tex}
{tex}E_{(Cr^{3+}/Cr)}^\ominus\;=-0.74\;V{/tex}
{tex}E_{(Fe^{2+}/Fe)}^\ominus\;=-0.44\;V{/tex}
question_text: Calculate {tex}{E}_{cell}{/tex} for following: {tex}2Cr(s) + 3{Fe}^{2+}(aq) \to 2{Cr}^{3+}(aq) +3Fe(s){/tex} {tex}Cr(s)| {Cr}^{3+} (aq)(0.1 M) || {Fe}^{2+} (aq)(0.01 M) | Fe(s){/tex} {tex}E_{(Cr^{3+}/Cr)}^\ominus\;=-0.74\;V{/tex} {tex}E_{(Fe^{2+}/Fe)}^\ominus\;=-0.44\;V{/tex}
explanation: We have {tex}2Cr(s) + 3{Fe}^{2+}(aq) \to 2{Cr}^{3+}(aq) +3Fe(s)​​​​​​​{/tex} {tex}Cr(s)| {Cr}^{3+} (aq)(0.1 M) || {Fe}^{2+} (aq)(0.01 M) | Fe(s){/tex} Anode half reaction: {tex}[Cr \to C{r^{3 + }} + 3{e^ - }] \times 2{/tex} Cathode half reaction: {tex}[F{e^{2 + }} + 2{e^ - } \to Fe] \times 3{/tex} Net cell reaction: {tex}2Cr + 3F{e^{2 + }} \to 2C{r^{3 + }} + 3Fe{/tex} Also standard emf of the cell is {tex}E_{cell}^\Theta\;=\;\;E_{\left(Fe^{2+}/Fe\right)}^\Theta\;-\;\;E_{\left(Cr^{3+}/Cr\right)}^\Theta{/tex} = -0.44-(-0.74) = 0.30 V According to Nernst equation [Here n=6 moles of electrons ] {tex}{E_{cell}} = E_{cell}^\Theta - \frac{{0.059}}{n}\log \frac{{{{\left[ {C{r^{3 + }}} \right]}^2}}}{{{{\left[ {F{e^{2 + }}} \right]}^3}}}{/tex} {tex}E_{cell}=E_{cell}^\Theta\;-\;\frac{0.059}6\log\left[\frac{\left(0.1\right)^2}{\left(0.01\right)^3}\right]{/tex} {tex}=0.30\;-\;\frac{0.059}6\log\left[\frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-6}}\right]{/tex} {tex} = 0.30 - \frac{{0.059}}{6}\log {10^4}{/tex} {tex}=0.3-\frac{0.059}6\times4{/tex} = 0.30 - 0.039 = 0.261 V {tex}\;\therefore E_{cell}\;=\;0.261\;V{/tex}
solution: We have {tex}2Cr(s) + 3{Fe}^{2+}(aq) \to 2{Cr}^{3+}(aq) +3Fe(s)​​​​​​​{/tex} {tex}Cr(s)| {Cr}^{3+} (aq)(0.1 M) || {Fe}^{2+} (aq)(0.01 M) | Fe(s){/tex} Anode half reaction: {tex}[Cr \to C{r^{3 + }} + 3{e^ - }] \times 2{/tex} Cathode half reaction: {tex}[F{e^{2 + }} + 2{e^ - } \to Fe] \times 3{/tex} Net cell reaction: {tex}2Cr + 3F{e^{2 + }} \to 2C{r^{3 + }} + 3Fe{/tex} Also standard emf of the cell is {tex}E_{cell}^\Theta\;=\;\;E_{\left(Fe^{2+}/Fe\right)}^\Theta\;-\;\;E_{\left(Cr^{3+}/Cr\right)}^\Theta{/tex} = -0.44-(-0.74) = 0.30 V According to Nernst equation [Here n=6 moles of electrons ] {tex}{E_{cell}} = E_{cell}^\Theta - \frac{{0.059}}{n}\log \frac{{{{\left[ {C{r^{3 + }}} \right]}^2}}}{{{{\left[ {F{e^{2 + }}} \right]}^3}}}{/tex} {tex}E_{cell}=E_{cell}^\Theta\;-\;\frac{0.059}6\log\left[\frac{\left(0.1\right)^2}{\left(0.01\right)^3}\right]{/tex} {tex}=0.30\;-\;\frac{0.059}6\log\left[\frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-6}}\right]{/tex} {tex} = 0.30 - \frac{{0.059}}{6}\log {10^4}{/tex} {tex}=0.3-\frac{0.059}6\times4{/tex} = 0.30 - 0.039 = 0.261 V {tex}\;\therefore E_{cell}\;=\;0.261\;V{/tex}
solution_html:
We have
{tex}2Cr(s) + 3{Fe}^{2+}(aq) \to 2{Cr}^{3+}(aq) +3Fe(s)​​​​​​​{/tex}
{tex}Cr(s)| {Cr}^{3+} (aq)(0.1 M) || {Fe}^{2+} (aq)(0.01 M) | Fe(s){/tex}
Anode half reaction:
{tex}[Cr \to C{r^{3 + }} + 3{e^ - }] \times 2{/tex}
Cathode half reaction:
{tex}[F{e^{2 + }} + 2{e^ - } \to Fe] \times 3{/tex}
Net cell reaction:
{tex}2Cr + 3F{e^{2 + }} \to 2C{r^{3 + }} + 3Fe{/tex}
Also standard emf of the cell is 
{tex}E_{cell}^\Theta\;=\;\;E_{\left(Fe^{2+}/Fe\right)}^\Theta\;-\;\;E_{\left(Cr^{3+}/Cr\right)}^\Theta{/tex}
= -0.44-(-0.74)
= 0.30 V
According to Nernst equation
[Here n=6 moles of electrons ]
{tex}{E_{cell}} = E_{cell}^\Theta - \frac{{0.059}}{n}\log \frac{{{{\left[ {C{r^{3 + }}} \right]}^2}}}{{{{\left[ {F{e^{2 + }}} \right]}^3}}}{/tex}
{tex}E_{cell}=E_{cell}^\Theta\;-\;\frac{0.059}6\log\left[\frac{\left(0.1\right)^2}{\left(0.01\right)^3}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=0.30\;-\;\frac{0.059}6\log\left[\frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-6}}\right]{/tex}
{tex} = 0.30 - \frac{{0.059}}{6}\log {10^4}{/tex}
{tex}=0.3-\frac{0.059}6\times4{/tex}
= 0.30 - 0.039
= 0.261 V
{tex}\;\therefore E_{cell}\;=\;0.261\;V{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the equilibrium constant for the reaction.
{tex}Fe(s) + C{d^{2 + }}(aq) \to F{e^{2 + }}(aq) + Cd(s){/tex}
Given : E0(Cd2+/Cd) = 0.40V, E0(Fe2+/Fe) = -0.44V
question_text: Calculate the equilibrium constant for the reaction. {tex}Fe(s) + C{d^{2 + }}(aq) \to F{e^{2 + }}(aq) + Cd(s){/tex} Given : E 0 (Cd 2+ /Cd) = 0.40V, E 0 (Fe 2+ /Fe) = -0.44V
solution_html:
{tex}E_{cell}^0 = [{E_{cathode}} - {E_{anode}}]{/tex}
= [- 0.40 - (-0.44)]
= [- 0.40 + 0.44] = 0.04 V
Since {tex}E_{cell}^0 = \frac{{0.059}}{n}\log {K_c}{/tex}
{tex}\log {K_c} = \frac{{2 \times 0.04}}{{0.059}} = 1.356{/tex}
Kc = anti log
(1.356) = 22.70

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Calculate Eo(Ni2+|Ni), if emf of the cell, Ni(s)|Ni2+(0.01M) || Cu2+(0.1M)|Cu(s) is 0.059 V. [Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0.34 V].
question_text: Calculate E o (Ni 2+ |Ni), if emf of the cell, Ni(s)|Ni 2+ (0.01M) || Cu 2+ (0.1M)|Cu(s) is 0.059 V. [Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}{/tex} = + 0.34 V].
explanation: Given, E cell = 0.059 V: {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}{/tex} = +0.34 and E o (Ni 2+ |Ni) = ? [Ni 2+ ] = 0.01M and [Cu 2+ ] = 0.1M By applying Nernst equation, We have E cell = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(a q)\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(a q)\right]}{/tex} 0.059 {tex}=E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{(0.01)}{0.1}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} n = 2) 0.059 = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{1}{10}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} log10 -1 = -1] {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.059 = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}+0.0295 \times 1{/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.059 - 0.0295 = 0.0295 V {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.03 V Also, We have, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\text { cathode }}^{0}-E_{\text { anode }}^{0}{/tex} 0.03 = 0.34 - {tex}E_{\text { anode }}^{0}{/tex} or {tex}E_{\text { anode }}^{0}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{N}{\mathrm{i}}^{2+} / \mathrm{N}{i}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.34 - 0.03 = 0.31 V
solution: Given, E cell = 0.059 V: {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}{/tex} = +0.34 and E o (Ni 2+ |Ni) = ? [Ni 2+ ] = 0.01M and [Cu 2+ ] = 0.1M By applying Nernst equation, We have E cell = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(a q)\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(a q)\right]}{/tex} 0.059 {tex}=E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{(0.01)}{0.1}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} n = 2) 0.059 = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{1}{10}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} log10 -1 = -1] {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.059 = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}+0.0295 \times 1{/tex} {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.059 - 0.0295 = 0.0295 V {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.03 V Also, We have, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\text { cathode }}^{0}-E_{\text { anode }}^{0}{/tex} 0.03 = 0.34 - {tex}E_{\text { anode }}^{0}{/tex} or {tex}E_{\text { anode }}^{0}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{N}{\mathrm{i}}^{2+} / \mathrm{N}{i}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.34 - 0.03 = 0.31 V
solution_html:
Given, Ecell = 0.059 V: {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}{/tex} = +0.34 and Eo (Ni2+|Ni) = ?
[Ni2+] = 0.01M and [Cu2+] = 0.1M

By applying Nernst equation, We have
Ecell = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(a q)\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(a q)\right]}{/tex} 
0.059 {tex}=E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{(0.01)}{0.1}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} n = 2)
0.059 = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{1}{10}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} log10-1 = -1]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.059 = {tex}E_{\text { cell }}^{\circ}+0.0295 \times 1{/tex}
{tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.059 - 0.0295 = 0.0295 V {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.03 V
Also, We have, {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}{/tex}= {tex}E_{\text { cathode }}^{0}-E_{\text { anode }}^{0}{/tex}
0.03 = 0.34 - {tex}E_{\text { anode }}^{0}{/tex}
or {tex}E_{\text { anode }}^{0}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\mathrm{N}{\mathrm{i}}^{2+} / \mathrm{N}{i}}^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.34 - 0.03 = 0.31 V

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Three electrolytic cells A, B and C containing electrolytes of zinc sulphate, silver nitrate and copper sulphate respectively are connected in series. A steady current of 1.50 amperes was passed through them until 1.45 g of silver deposited at the cathode of cell B. How long did the current flow? What weight of copper and zinc get deposited? (Atomic weight: Cu=63.5 g mol-1, Ag=108 g mol-1 and Zn=65.3 g mol-1)
question_text: Three electrolytic cells A, B and C containing electrolytes of zinc sulphate, silver nitrate and copper sulphate respectively are connected in series. A steady current of 1.50 amperes was passed through them until 1.45 g of silver deposited at the cathode of cell B. How long did the current flow? What weight of copper and zinc get deposited? (Atomic weight: Cu=63.5 g mol -1 , Ag=108 g mol -1 and Zn=65.3 g mol -1 )
explanation: Cell B contains AgNO 3 and reaction may be represented as: {tex}AgNO_3 \rightleftharpoons Ag^+ + NO_3^-{/tex} {tex}Ag^+ + e^- \rightleftharpoons Ag\; (At\; cathode){/tex} According to the equation, 1 mol or 108 g of silver is deposited by 96500 C 1.45 g silver is deposited by {tex}=\frac{96500\times1.45}{108}{/tex} = 1295.6 C Now, {tex}Q=I\;\times\;t{/tex} {tex}1295.6=1.5\;\times\;t{/tex} {tex}\therefore\;t\;=\;\frac{1295.6}{1.5}=863\;s{/tex} The weights of copper and zinc can be calculated by using Faraday's second law of electrolysis. In cell A, the electrode reaction is {tex}Zn^{2+}+ 2e^-\rightarrow Zn{/tex} 2 mol of electrons or {tex}2\times96500\;C{/tex} of current produce 1 mol or 65.3 g of Zn so that {tex}2\times96500\;C{/tex} of electricity deposit Zn = 65.3 g 1295.6 C of electricity deposit Zn = {tex}\frac{65.3\times1295.6}{2\times96500}=0.438\;g{/tex} In cell C, The electrode reaction is {tex}Cu^{2+}+ 2e^-\rightarrow Cu{/tex} 2 mol of electrons or {tex}2\times 96500\;C{/tex} of current produce 1 mol or 63.5 g of Cu so that {tex}2\times 96500\;C{/tex} of current deposit Cu = 63.5 g 1295.6 C of current deposit Cu = {tex}\frac{63.5 \times 1295.6}{2\times 96500}=0.426\;g{/tex}
solution: Cell B contains AgNO 3 and reaction may be represented as: {tex}AgNO_3 \rightleftharpoons Ag^+ + NO_3^-{/tex} {tex}Ag^+ + e^- \rightleftharpoons Ag\; (At\; cathode){/tex} According to the equation, 1 mol or 108 g of silver is deposited by 96500 C 1.45 g silver is deposited by {tex}=\frac{96500\times1.45}{108}{/tex} = 1295.6 C Now, {tex}Q=I\;\times\;t{/tex} {tex}1295.6=1.5\;\times\;t{/tex} {tex}\therefore\;t\;=\;\frac{1295.6}{1.5}=863\;s{/tex} The weights of copper and zinc can be calculated by using Faraday's second law of electrolysis. In cell A, the electrode reaction is {tex}Zn^{2+}+ 2e^-\rightarrow Zn{/tex} 2 mol of electrons or {tex}2\times96500\;C{/tex} of current produce 1 mol or 65.3 g of Zn so that {tex}2\times96500\;C{/tex} of electricity deposit Zn = 65.3 g 1295.6 C of electricity deposit Zn = {tex}\frac{65.3\times1295.6}{2\times96500}=0.438\;g{/tex} In cell C, The electrode reaction is {tex}Cu^{2+}+ 2e^-\rightarrow Cu{/tex} 2 mol of electrons or {tex}2\times 96500\;C{/tex} of current produce 1 mol or 63.5 g of Cu so that {tex}2\times 96500\;C{/tex} of current deposit Cu = 63.5 g 1295.6 C of current deposit Cu = {tex}\frac{63.5 \times 1295.6}{2\times 96500}=0.426\;g{/tex}
solution_html:
Cell B contains AgNO3 and reaction may be represented as:
{tex}AgNO_3 \rightleftharpoons Ag^+ + NO_3^-{/tex}
{tex}Ag^+ + e^- \rightleftharpoons Ag\; (At\; cathode){/tex}
According to the equation,
1 mol or 108 g of silver is deposited by 96500 C
1.45 g silver is deposited by {tex}=\frac{96500\times1.45}{108}{/tex}
= 1295.6 C
Now, {tex}Q=I\;\times\;t{/tex}
{tex}1295.6=1.5\;\times\;t{/tex}
{tex}\therefore\;t\;=\;\frac{1295.6}{1.5}=863\;s{/tex}
The weights of copper and zinc can be calculated by using Faraday's second law of electrolysis.
In cell A, the electrode reaction is
{tex}Zn^{2+}+ 2e^-\rightarrow Zn{/tex}
2 mol of electrons or {tex}2\times96500\;C{/tex} of current produce 1 mol or 65.3 g of Zn so that
{tex}2\times96500\;C{/tex} of electricity deposit Zn = 65.3 g
1295.6 C of electricity deposit Zn = {tex}\frac{65.3\times1295.6}{2\times96500}=0.438\;g{/tex}
In cell C, The electrode reaction is
{tex}Cu^{2+}+ 2e^-\rightarrow Cu{/tex}
2 mol of electrons or {tex}2\times 96500\;C{/tex} of current produce 1 mol or 63.5 g of Cu so that
{tex}2\times 96500\;C{/tex} of current deposit Cu = 63.5 g
1295.6 C of current deposit Cu = {tex}\frac{63.5 \times 1295.6}{2\times 96500}=0.426\;g{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the strength of the current required to deposit 1.2g of magnesium from molten MgCl2 in 1h.
[1F = 96500 Cmol-1, atomic mass of Mg = 24.0].
question_text: Calculate the strength of the current required to deposit 1.2g of magnesium from molten MgCl 2 in 1h. [1F = 96500 Cmol -1 , atomic mass of Mg = 24.0].
explanation: The half reaction occurring during deposition is: Mg 2 + + 2e - ----------> Mg(s) So to produce 1 mole of Mg we need 2 moles of electrons, 1 mol of Mg = 24 g Thus 1.2 g of Mg = ( {tex}\frac{1.2}{24}{/tex} ) = 0.05 moles Hence no. of moles of electrons required to deposit = 0.05 x 2 = 0.1 moles Qty. of electricity passed (Q) = moles of electrons passed x 96500 = 0.1 x 96500 = 9650 C I = {tex}\frac Qt{/tex} I = {tex}\frac {9650}{60 \times 60}{/tex} (converting 1 hr into seconds) I = 2.68 A .
solution: The half reaction occurring during deposition is: Mg 2 + + 2e - ----------> Mg(s) So to produce 1 mole of Mg we need 2 moles of electrons, 1 mol of Mg = 24 g Thus 1.2 g of Mg = ( {tex}\frac{1.2}{24}{/tex} ) = 0.05 moles Hence no. of moles of electrons required to deposit = 0.05 x 2 = 0.1 moles Qty. of electricity passed (Q) = moles of electrons passed x 96500 = 0.1 x 96500 = 9650 C I = {tex}\frac Qt{/tex} I = {tex}\frac {9650}{60 \times 60}{/tex} (converting 1 hr into seconds) I = 2.68 A .
solution_html:
The half reaction occurring during deposition is:
Mg2+ + 2e- ----------> Mg(s)
So to produce 1 mole of Mg we need 2 moles of electrons,
1 mol of Mg = 24 g
Thus 1.2 g of Mg = ({tex}\frac{1.2}{24}{/tex}) = 0.05 moles
Hence no. of moles of electrons required to deposit  = 0.05 x 2 = 0.1 moles
Qty. of electricity passed (Q) = moles of electrons passed x 96500
 = 0.1 x 96500 = 9650 C
I = {tex}\frac Qt{/tex}
I = {tex}\frac {9650}{60 \times 60}{/tex} (converting 1 hr into seconds)
I = 2.68 A.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Define molar conductivity of a substance and describe how for weak and strong electrolytes, molar conductivity changes with concentration of solute. How is such change explained?
question_text: Define molar conductivity of a substance and describe how for weak and strong electrolytes, molar conductivity changes with concentration of solute. How is such change explained?
explanation: Molar conductivity : Molar conductivity is the conducting power of all the ions produced by one mole of an electrolyte in a solution of specified concentration. It is denoted by Λ m and is related to conductivity ( {tex}\kappa{/tex} ) by the relation: Λ m = {tex}\frac{\kappa}C{/tex} Variation of Molar Conductivity with Concentration: From Fig. we find that the increase in molar conductivity for a strong electrolyte like KCl is very gradual on dilution and also the value is high at all concentrations. Whereas for a weak electrolyte like CH 3 COOH, there is a gradual increase in Λ m on dilution which is followed by a sharper increase in further dilution.
solution: Molar conductivity : Molar conductivity is the conducting power of all the ions produced by one mole of an electrolyte in a solution of specified concentration. It is denoted by Λ m and is related to conductivity ( {tex}\kappa{/tex} ) by the relation: Λ m = {tex}\frac{\kappa}C{/tex} Variation of Molar Conductivity with Concentration: From Fig. we find that the increase in molar conductivity for a strong electrolyte like KCl is very gradual on dilution and also the value is high at all concentrations. Whereas for a weak electrolyte like CH 3 COOH, there is a gradual increase in Λ m on dilution which is followed by a sharper increase in further dilution.
solution_html:
Molar conductivity: Molar conductivity is the conducting power of all the ions produced by one mole of an electrolyte in a solution of specified concentration.
It is denoted by Λm and is related to conductivity ({tex}\kappa{/tex}) by the relation: Λm = {tex}\frac{\kappa}C{/tex}

Variation of Molar Conductivity with Concentration: 

From Fig. we find that the increase in molar conductivity for a strong electrolyte like KCl is very gradual on dilution and also the value is high at all concentrations.  Whereas for a weak electrolyte like CH3COOH, there is a gradual increase in  Λm on dilution which is followed by a sharper increase in further dilution.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the cell emf and {tex}\Delta_{r} G{/tex} for the cell reaction at 25oC.
{tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(0.1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cd}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Cd}(s){/tex}; [Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.763 V, {tex}E_{C d^{2+} / C d}^{\circ}{/tex} =- 0.403 V;  1F = 96500 C mol-1,R = 8.314 JK-1mol-1].
question_text: Calculate the cell emf and {tex}\Delta_{r} G{/tex} for the cell reaction at 25 o C. {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(0.1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cd}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Cd}(s){/tex} ; [Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.763 V, {tex}E_{C d^{2+} / C d}^{\circ}{/tex} =- 0.403 V; 1F = 96500 C mol -1 ,R = 8.314 JK -1 mol -1 ].
explanation: For the given cell; {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(0.1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cd}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Cd}(s) |{/tex} Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.763 V < {tex}E_{C d^{2+} / C d}^{\circ}{/tex} = - 0.403 V Therefore, E o cell = {tex}E_{\mathrm{right}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{left}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.403 - (-0.763) = 0.36 V. By applying Nernst equation, we have E cell = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}(a q)\right]}{/tex} E cell = {tex}0.36-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{0.1}{0.01}{/tex} E cell = 0.36 - 0.0295 log 10 E cell = {tex}0.36-0.0295 \times 1=0.3305 \mathrm{V}{/tex} Also, We know that : {tex}\Delta_{r} G{/tex} = {tex}-n F E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}-2 \times 96500 \times 0.33{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{r} G{/tex} = -63786.5J mol -1 = -63.79 kJ mol -1
solution: For the given cell; {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(0.1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cd}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Cd}(s) |{/tex} Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.763 V < {tex}E_{C d^{2+} / C d}^{\circ}{/tex} = - 0.403 V Therefore, E o cell = {tex}E_{\mathrm{right}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{left}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.403 - (-0.763) = 0.36 V. By applying Nernst equation, we have E cell = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}(a q)\right]}{/tex} E cell = {tex}0.36-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{0.1}{0.01}{/tex} E cell = 0.36 - 0.0295 log 10 E cell = {tex}0.36-0.0295 \times 1=0.3305 \mathrm{V}{/tex} Also, We know that : {tex}\Delta_{r} G{/tex} = {tex}-n F E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}-2 \times 96500 \times 0.33{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{r} G{/tex} = -63786.5J mol -1 = -63.79 kJ mol -1
solution_html:
For the given cell; {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(s)\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(0.1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cd}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Cd}(s) |{/tex}
Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.763 V < {tex}E_{C d^{2+} / C d}^{\circ}{/tex} = - 0.403 V
Therefore, Eocell = {tex}E_{\mathrm{right}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{left}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.403 - (-0.763) = 0.36 V.
By applying Nernst equation, we have
Ecell = {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(a q)\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}(a q)\right]}{/tex}
Ecell = {tex}0.36-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{0.1}{0.01}{/tex}
Ecell = 0.36 - 0.0295 log 10
Ecell = {tex}0.36-0.0295 \times 1=0.3305 \mathrm{V}{/tex}
Also, We know that : {tex}\Delta_{r} G{/tex} = {tex}-n F E_{\mathrm{cell}}{/tex} = {tex}-2 \times 96500 \times 0.33{/tex}
{tex}\Delta_{r} G{/tex} = -63786.5J mol-1 = -63.79 kJ mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Illustrate with the help of a diagram how the molar conductivities of

  1. a strong electrolyte and
  2. a weak electrolyte vary with dilution of solution. Give a reason for these variations.
question_text: Illustrate with the help of a diagram how the molar conductivities of a strong electrolyte and a weak electrolyte vary with dilution of solution. Give a reason for these variations.
explanation: Molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is the conductance of the volume V of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with area of cross section A and distance of unit length. Ʌ m = К c Here, c = concentration in moles per volume К = specific conductivity Ʌ m = molar conductivity. As the solution contains only one mole of electrolyte the above equation can be modified as: Ʌ m = КV Molar conductivity increases with decrease in concentration as the total volume V of solution containing one mole of electrolyte also increases. Upon dilution, the concentration decreases. When the concentration approaches zero, molar conductivity of the solution is known as limiting molar conductivity. Variation of molar conductivity with concentration is different for strong and weak electrolytes. Variation of molar conductivity with concentration for strong electrolytes: For strong electrolytes the molar conductivity increases slowly with the dilution. The plot between the molar conductivity and c 1/2 is a straight line having y-intercept equal to Λ°m. The value of limiting molar conductivity, {tex} \wedge _m^o{/tex} can be determined from the graph or with the help of Kohlrausch law. For strong electrolytes the molar conductivity increases slowly with the dilution. The plot between the molar conductivity and c 1/2 is a straight line having y-intercept equal to {tex} \wedge _m^o{/tex} . The value of limiting molar conductivity {tex} \wedge _m^o{/tex} can be determined from the graph or with the help of Kohlrausch law. The general equation for the plot is given as: {tex}{ \wedge _m} = \wedge _m^o - A{c^{1/2}}{/tex} Where, −A is a constant equal to the slope of the line. For a given solvent, the value of A depends on type of electrolyte at a particular temperature. Variation of molar conductivity with concentration for weak electrolyte: For weak electrolytes, the graph plotted between molar conductivity and c 1/2 (where c is the concentration) is not a straight line. Weak electrolytes have lower molar conductivities and lower degree of dissociation at higher concentrations which increases steeply at lower concentrations. Therefore, limiting molar conductivity, {tex} \wedge _m^o{/tex} cannot be obtained by extrapolation of molar conductivity to zero concentration. Hence, we use Kohlrausch law of independent migration of ions for determining limiting molar conductivity, {tex} \wedge _m^o{/tex} of weak electrolytes.
solution: Molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is the conductance of the volume V of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with area of cross section A and distance of unit length. Ʌ m = К c Here, c = concentration in moles per volume К = specific conductivity Ʌ m = molar conductivity. As the solution contains only one mole of electrolyte the above equation can be modified as: Ʌ m = КV Molar conductivity increases with decrease in concentration as the total volume V of solution containing one mole of electrolyte also increases. Upon dilution, the concentration decreases. When the concentration approaches zero, molar conductivity of the solution is known as limiting molar conductivity. Variation of molar conductivity with concentration is different for strong and weak electrolytes. Variation of molar conductivity with concentration for strong electrolytes: For strong electrolytes the molar conductivity increases slowly with the dilution. The plot between the molar conductivity and c 1/2 is a straight line having y-intercept equal to Λ°m. The value of limiting molar conductivity, {tex} \wedge _m^o{/tex} can be determined from the graph or with the help of Kohlrausch law. For strong electrolytes the molar conductivity increases slowly with the dilution. The plot between the molar conductivity and c 1/2 is a straight line having y-intercept equal to {tex} \wedge _m^o{/tex} . The value of limiting molar conductivity {tex} \wedge _m^o{/tex} can be determined from the graph or with the help of Kohlrausch law. The general equation for the plot is given as: {tex}{ \wedge _m} = \wedge _m^o - A{c^{1/2}}{/tex} Where, −A is a constant equal to the slope of the line. For a given solvent, the value of A depends on type of electrolyte at a particular temperature. Variation of molar conductivity with concentration for weak electrolyte: For weak electrolytes, the graph plotted between molar conductivity and c 1/2 (where c is the concentration) is not a straight line. Weak electrolytes have lower molar conductivities and lower degree of dissociation at higher concentrations which increases steeply at lower concentrations. Therefore, limiting molar conductivity, {tex} \wedge _m^o{/tex} cannot be obtained by extrapolation of molar conductivity to zero concentration. solution_html:
Molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is the conductance of the volume V of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with area of cross section A and distance of unit length.
Ʌm = К c
Here,
c = concentration in moles per volume
К = specific conductivity
Ʌm = molar conductivity.
As the solution contains only one mole of electrolyte the above equation can be modified as:
Ʌm = КV
Molar conductivity increases with decrease in concentration as the total volume V of solution containing one mole of electrolyte also increases. Upon dilution, the concentration decreases. When the concentration approaches zero, molar conductivity of the solution is known as limiting molar conductivity. Variation of molar conductivity with concentration is different for strong and weak electrolytes.

Variation of molar conductivity with concentration for strong electrolytes: For strong electrolytes the molar conductivity increases slowly with the dilution. The plot between the molar conductivity and c1/2 is a straight line having y-intercept equal to Λ°m. The value of limiting molar conductivity, {tex} \wedge _m^o{/tex} can be determined from the graph or with the help of Kohlrausch law.
For strong electrolytes the molar conductivity increases slowly with the dilution. The plot between the molar conductivity and c1/2 is a straight line having y-intercept equal to {tex} \wedge _m^o{/tex}. The value of limiting molar conductivity {tex} \wedge _m^o{/tex} can be determined from the graph or with the help of Kohlrausch law. The general equation for the plot is given as:
{tex}{ \wedge _m} = \wedge _m^o - A{c^{1/2}}{/tex}
Where, −A is a constant equal to the slope of the line. For a given solvent, the value of A depends on type of electrolyte at a particular temperature.
Variation of molar conductivity with concentration for weak electrolyte: For weak electrolytes, the graph plotted between molar conductivity and c1/2 (where c is the concentration) is not a straight line. Weak electrolytes have lower molar conductivities and lower degree of dissociation at higher concentrations which increases steeply at lower concentrations. Therefore, limiting molar conductivity, {tex} \wedge _m^o{/tex} cannot be obtained by extrapolation of molar conductivity to zero concentration. Hence, we use Kohlrausch law of independent migration of ions for determining limiting molar conductivity, {tex} \wedge _m^o{/tex} of weak electrolytes.

question_html:
The resistance of a conductivity cell when filled with 0.05 M solution of an electrolytes X is 100 ohms at 40°C. The same conductivity cell filled with 0.01 M solution of electrolyte Y has a resistance of 50 ohms. The conductivity of 0.05 M solution of electrolyte X is {tex}1.0 \times {10^{ - 4}}S\,c{m^{ - 1}}{/tex}calculate.

  1. Cell constant
  2. Conductivity of 0.01 MY solution
  3. Molar conductivity of 0.01 MY solution.
question_text: The resistance of a conductivity cell when filled with 0.05 M solution of an electrolytes X is 100 ohms at 40°C. The same conductivity cell filled with 0.01 M solution of electrolyte Y has a resistance of 50 ohms. The conductivity of 0.05 M solution of electrolyte X is {tex}1.0 \times {10^{ - 4}}S\,c{m^{ - 1}}{/tex} calculate. Cell constant Conductivity of 0.01 MY solution Molar conductivity of 0.01 MY solution.
explanation: For electrolyte X Molarity = 0.05 M Resistance = {tex} 100\Omega {/tex} Conductivity = {tex} 1.0 \times {10^{ - 4}}S\,c{m^{ - 1}}{/tex} For electrolyte Y Molarity = 0.01 M Resistance = {tex} 50\Omega {/tex} Conductivity = ? Cell constant = Conductivity (K) {tex}\times{/tex} Resistance (R) {tex}{G^*} = 1.0 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 100{/tex} = 10 -2 cm -1 Conductivity of solution Y is {tex}K = \frac{{{G^*}}}{R} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{50}}{/tex} {tex} = 0.02 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} {tex} = 2 \times {10^{ - 4}}S\,c{m^{ - 1}}{/tex} Concentration C = 0.01 M {tex} = 0.01\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex} = 0.01 \times 1000\,mol\,m{l^{ - 3}}{/tex} = 10mol cm -3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Molar concentration {tex}{\lambda _m} = \frac{K}{C} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{10}}{/tex} {tex} = 0.2 \times {10^{ - 4}}S\,c{m^2}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution: For electrolyte X Molarity = 0.05 M Resistance = {tex} 100\Omega {/tex} Conductivity = {tex} 1.0 \times {10^{ - 4}}S\,c{m^{ - 1}}{/tex} For electrolyte Y Molarity = 0.01 M Resistance = {tex} 50\Omega {/tex} Conductivity = ? Cell constant = Conductivity (K) {tex}\times{/tex} Resistance (R) {tex}{G^*} = 1.0 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 100{/tex} = 10 -2 cm -1 Conductivity of solution Y is {tex}K = \frac{{{G^*}}}{R} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{50}}{/tex} {tex} = 0.02 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} {tex} = 2 \times {10^{ - 4}}S\,c{m^{ - 1}}{/tex} Concentration C = 0.01 M {tex} = 0.01\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex} = 0.01 \times 1000\,mol\,m{l^{ - 3}}{/tex} = 10mol cm -3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Molar concentration {tex}{\lambda _m} = \frac{K}{C} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{10}}{/tex} {tex} = 0.2 \times {10^{ - 4}}S\,c{m^2}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
For electrolyte X
Molarity = 0.05 M
Resistance = {tex} 100\Omega {/tex}
Conductivity = {tex} 1.0 \times {10^{ - 4}}S\,c{m^{ - 1}}{/tex}
For electrolyte Y
Molarity = 0.01 M
Resistance = {tex} 50\Omega {/tex}
Conductivity = ?

  1. Cell constant = Conductivity (K) {tex}\times{/tex}Resistance (R)
    {tex}{G^*} = 1.0 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 100{/tex}
    = 10-2cm-1
  2. Conductivity of solution Y is
    {tex}K = \frac{{{G^*}}}{R} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{50}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 0.02 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 2 \times {10^{ - 4}}S\,c{m^{ - 1}}{/tex}
  3. Concentration C = 0.01 M
    {tex} = 0.01\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 0.01 \times 1000\,mol\,m{l^{ - 3}}{/tex}
    = 10mol cm-3
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Molar concentration
    {tex}{\lambda _m} = \frac{K}{C} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{10}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 0.2 \times {10^{ - 4}}S\,c{m^2}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Conductivity of 0.00241 M acetic acid is 7.896 × 10-5 S cm-1. Calculate its molar conductivity and if for acetic acid is 390.5 S cm2 mol-1, what is its dissociation constant?
question_text: Conductivity of 0.00241 M acetic acid is 7.896 × 10 -5 S cm -1 . Calculate its molar conductivity and if for acetic acid is 390.5 S cm 2 mol -1 , what is its dissociation constant?
explanation: Given that, κ = 7.896 × 10 -5 S m -1 C=M= 0.00241 mol L -1 The formula of molar conductivity, Λ m = (k × 1000)/M Plug the value we get {tex}{ \wedge _m} = \frac{{(7.896 \times {{10}^{ - 5}} \times 1000)}}{{0.00241}}{/tex} = 32.76S cm 2 mol -1 The formula of degree of dissociation α = Λm/ Λom Plug the value we get α = 32.76S/390.5 = 0.084 The formula of dissociation constant K = Cα/(1 - α) Plug the values we get K = 0.00241 × 0.084/(1- 0.084) = 1.86 × 10 -5 mol L -1
solution: Given that, κ = 7.896 × 10 -5 S m -1 C=M= 0.00241 mol L -1 The formula of molar conductivity, Λ m = (k × 1000)/M Plug the value we get {tex}{ \wedge _m} = \frac{{(7.896 \times {{10}^{ - 5}} \times 1000)}}{{0.00241}}{/tex} = 32.76S cm 2 mol -1 The formula of degree of dissociation α = Λm/ Λom Plug the value we get α = 32.76S/390.5 = 0.084 The formula of dissociation constant K = Cα/(1 - α) Plug the values we get K = 0.00241 × 0.084/(1- 0.084) = 1.86 × 10 -5 mol L -1
solution_html:
Given that, κ = 7.896 × 10-5 S m-1
C=M= 0.00241 mol L-1
The formula of molar conductivity,
Λm = (k × 1000)/M
Plug the value we get

{tex}{ \wedge _m} = \frac{{(7.896 \times {{10}^{ - 5}} \times 1000)}}{{0.00241}}{/tex}

= 32.76S cm2 mol-1

The formula of degree of dissociation
α = Λm/ Λom
Plug the value we get
α = 32.76S/390.5

= 0.084
The formula of dissociation constant
K = Cα/(1 - α)
Plug the values we get
K = 0.00241 × 0.084/(1- 0.084)
= 1.86 × 10-5 mol L-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What are primary cell?
question_text: What are primary cell?
explanation: Primary cell are those cells which are not rechargeable and cell reaction occurs only once and then cell become dead. The most common example of dry cell is Leclanche cell.
solution: Primary cell are those cells which are not rechargeable and cell reaction occurs only once and then cell become dead. The most common example of dry cell is Leclanche cell.
solution_html:
Primary cell are those cells which are not rechargeable and cell reaction occurs only once and then cell become dead.

The most common example of dry cell is Leclanche cell.

question_html:
Formulate the galvanic cell in which the following reaction takes place.
Zn(s) + 2Ag+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2 +(aq) + 2Ag(s);  Eo(Zn2+|Zn) = -0.76V and Eo (Ag+|Ag) = 0.80 V

  1. Which one of its electrodes is negatively charged?
  2. The reaction taking place at each of its electrode.
  3. The carriers of current within this cell.
question_text: Formulate the galvanic cell in which the following reaction takes place. Zn(s) + 2Ag + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2 + (aq) + 2Ag(s); E o (Zn 2+ |Zn) = -0.76V and E o (Ag + |Ag) = 0.80 V Which one of its electrodes is negatively charged? The reaction taking place at each of its electrode. The carriers of current within this cell.
explanation: The cell reaction is Zn(s) + 2Ag + (aq) ⇌ Zn 2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s). The cell is represented as {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) \| \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{s}){/tex} ; E o (Zn 2+ |Zn) = -0.76V and E o (Ag + |Ag) = 0.80 V. Since zinc (Zn) is more reactive than silver (Ag). So, Zn electrode is negatively charged. At anode : Oxidation will takes place; Zn(s) → Zn 2+ (aq) + 2e - (oxidation) At cathode: Reduction will takes place; Ag + (aq) + e - → Ag(s) (reduction) Ions are the carriers of current within the cell.
solution: The cell reaction is Zn(s) + 2Ag + (aq) ⇌ Zn 2+ (aq) + 2Ag(s). The cell is represented as {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) \| \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{s}){/tex} ; E o (Zn 2+ |Zn) = -0.76V and E o (Ag + |Ag) = 0.80 V. Since zinc (Zn) is more reactive than silver (Ag). So, Zn electrode is negatively charged. At anode : Oxidation will takes place; Zn(s) → Zn 2+ (aq) + 2e - (oxidation) At cathode: Reduction will takes place; Ag + (aq) + e - → Ag(s) (reduction) Ions are the carriers of current within the cell.
solution_html:
The cell reaction is Zn(s) + 2Ag+ (aq) ⇌  Zn2+(aq) + 2Ag(s).
The cell is represented as {tex}\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) \| \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{s}){/tex}; Eo (Zn2+|Zn) = -0.76V and Eo (Ag+|Ag) = 0.80 V.

  1. Since zinc (Zn) is more reactive than silver (Ag). So, Zn electrode is negatively charged.
  2. At anode : Oxidation will takes place; Zn(s) → Zn2+(aq) + 2e- (oxidation)
    At cathode: Reduction will takes place;  Ag+ (aq) + e- → Ag(s) (reduction)
  3. Ions are the carriers of current within the cell.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Calculate the standard cell potential of a galvanic cell in which the following reaction takes place.
2Cr(s) + 3Cd2+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr3+(aq) + 3Cd (s)
Calculate {tex}\Delta{/tex}Go and equilibrium constant,
K, of the above reaction at 25oC.
[Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cr}^{3+} / \mathrm{Cr}}^{0}{/tex} = -0.74 V, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cd}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cd}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.40 V, 1F = 96500 C mol-1]
question_text: Calculate the standard cell potential of a galvanic cell in which the following reaction takes place. 2Cr(s) + 3Cd 2+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ (aq) + 3Cd (s) Calculate {tex}\Delta{/tex} G o and equilibrium constant, K, of the above reaction at 25 o C. [Given, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cr}^{3+} / \mathrm{Cr}}^{0}{/tex} = -0.74 V, {tex}E_{\mathrm{Cd}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cd}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.40 V, 1F = 96500 C mol -1 ]
explanation: Here, n = 6, T = 273 + 25 o C = 298 K {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{cathode}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{anode}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.40- (- 0.74) = 0.34 V {tex}\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-n F E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{0}{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-6 \times 96500 \times 0.34{/tex} = 196860J mol -1 {tex}\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-2.303 R T \log K_{c}{/tex} log K c = {tex}-\frac{\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}}{2.303 R T}=\frac{196860}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{/tex} log K c = 34.5014 K c = antilog 34.5014 K c = {tex}3.173 \times 10^{34}{/tex}
solution: Here, n = 6, T = 273 + 25 o C = 298 K {tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{cathode}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{anode}}^{\circ}{/tex} = -0.40- (- 0.74) = 0.34 V {tex}\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-n F E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{0}{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-6 \times 96500 \times 0.34{/tex} = 196860J mol -1 {tex}\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-2.303 R T \log K_{c}{/tex} log K c = {tex}-\frac{\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}}{2.303 R T}=\frac{196860}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{/tex} log K c = 34.5014 K c = antilog 34.5014 K c = {tex}3.173 \times 10^{34}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, n = 6, T = 273 + 25oC = 298 K
{tex}E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{cathode}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{anode}}^{\circ}{/tex}
= -0.40- (- 0.74) = 0.34 V
{tex}\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-n F E_{\mathrm{cell}}^{0}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-6 \times 96500 \times 0.34{/tex} = 196860J mol-1
{tex}\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}=-2.303 R T \log K_{c}{/tex}
log Kc = {tex}-\frac{\Delta_{r} G^{\circ}}{2.303 R T}=\frac{196860}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{/tex}
log Kc = 34.5014
Kc = antilog 34.5014
Kc = {tex}3.173 \times 10^{34}{/tex}
